origin_brazil.blurb: The world’s largest producer and the backbone of espresso blends — lower-grown, nutty, chocolatey, and easy to roast.

regionMap.note: Concentrated in the southeastern states — Minas Gerais (Cerrado, Sul de Minas), São Paulo (Mogiana), and Bahia.

origin_sumatra.name: Sumatra / Indonesia

sections.body: Indonesian coffees from Sumatra and Sulawesi are known for earthy, spicy, herbal character with full body and low acidity — a signature partly created by the region's distinctive wet-hulled processing. These flavors become more pronounced in darker roasts, which is where the origin is traditionally taken.

Sumatra can handle more aggressive heat application during the middle (Maillard) stage to develop its full body — a head roaster's approach that would obliterate a delicate Ethiopian. Its low acidity and heavy body mean the earthy, herbal signature holds up rather than being lost, so it tolerates and often benefits from a firmer hand than high-grown washed coffees.

Medium-dark to dark. Sumatra's earthy depth and body are traditionally showcased at Full City and beyond, where the roast character complements rather than erases the origin. It's a natural fit for darker blends and for drinkers who want a heavy, low-acid, brooding cup.

Sumatra is the most famous of Indonesia's islands for coffee, and Indonesian coffee traces back to the Dutch colonial era, when the Netherlands established Arabica on Java in the 1700s — making “Java” a synonym for coffee itself. Sumatran coffee is grown largely by smallholders in the north of the island, and it produces one of the most distinctive and polarizing cups in the world: heavy-bodied, low-acid, earthy, herbal, and savory, with notes people describe as cedar, tobacco, forest floor, and dark chocolate. You either love it or you don't — and it's a mainstay of dark roasts and many classic blends.

sections.h: Wet-hulling: the secret to the Sumatran cup
sections.body: That singular flavor comes largely from a processing method nearly unique to Indonesia: wet-hulling, known locally as giling basah. Instead of drying the parchment-covered bean fully before hulling (as almost everywhere else), Sumatran producers hull the parchment off while the bean is still quite wet, then finish drying the bare green bean. This is a practical adaptation to the humid, rainy climate — and it produces the characteristic low acidity, heavy body, earthy-savory flavors, and the distinctive blue-green, sometimes misshapen appearance of the beans. It's the clearest example in coffee of processing defining flavor more than variety does.

The northern growing areas each contribute. Gayo, in the Aceh highlands at the northern tip, is the largest and most celebrated — often cleaner and more refined, with herbal and citrus complexity. Lintong (around Lake Toba) is the classic heavy-bodied, low-acid Sumatran profile. Mandheling isn't a place so much as a traditional trade name for northern Sumatran coffee, associated with the classic earthy, savory, wet-hulled character. Varieties are a mix of older types — Typica descendants, Catimor, and local selections like Ateng and Tim Tim (Timor-derived).

keypoints: Indonesia's famous island; Dutch colonial legacy (Java = coffee). Cup = heavy body, low acid, earthy/herbal/savory — polarizing, great in dark roasts.
Wet-hulling (giling basah) — hull parchment while bean still wet → the signature earthy/savory low-acid cup + blue-green misshapen beans. Processing > variety here.
Regions: Gayo/Aceh (largest, cleaner), Lintong (classic heavy-bodied), Mandheling (trade name for northern Sumatran).

refs.label: God of Beans — origin characteristics & density

origin_sumatra.blurb: The savory outlier — wet-hulled processing gives a heavy-bodied, low-acid, earthy cup unlike anywhere else on earth.

regionMap.note: On the island of Sumatra in Indonesia — Gayo highlands in Aceh (north), Lintong and Mandheling further south.

origin_kenya.name: Kenya

sections.body: Kenyan coffee has a flavor fingerprint professionals recognize instantly: intense, wine-like acidity with blackcurrant and berry, sometimes a savory tomato edge, and remarkable clarity. It comes from the SL28 and SL34 varieties (selected by Scott Laboratories in the 1930s), high-altitude red volcanic soil around Mt. Kenya (Nyeri, Kirinyaga, Embu), and a distinctive double-washed processing with extended fermentation. Kenya's rigorous auction system means specialty lots are consistently excellent — and consistently expensive. These are dense, high-grown beans.

Kenya is a light-to-medium coffee — its whole appeal is that bright, complex acidity and blackcurrant character, and dark roasting destroys exactly the compounds that make it special. Treat it as a dense, high-grown bean that takes and wants heat, but watch the back half: dense Kenyans carry significant exothermic energy after first crack, and if you're not planning for it you can lose control of the rate of rise. Aim to protect acidity and clarity — drop it before the origin character bakes out.

Light to medium (City to City+), stopping around or shortly after first crack. The goal is to showcase the blackcurrant, berry, and vivid acidity — this is a pour-over and filter coffee first, where that clarity sings. A note on grades: Kenya AA refers to bean size, not quality; read the estate and processing alongside it.

If Ethiopia is coffee's birthplace, Kenya is its technical powerhouse. Cultivation only began around 1900 under British colonists — late compared to its neighbors — but Kenya built the most rigorous quality infrastructure in the coffee world. Its coffee has an instantly recognizable fingerprint: bright wine-like acidity, blackcurrant and berry, sometimes a savory “tomato” quality, and a juicy, sparkling structure often credited to phosphoric acid and the region's red volcanic soil. That distinctiveness comes from a specific combination of variety, soil, altitude, and meticulous processing that few origins can match.

sections.h: SL28, SL34, and the double wash
sections.body: Two varieties define Kenyan coffee: SL28 and SL34, selected by Scott Agricultural Laboratories (hence “SL”) in the 1930s–40s. Bourbon-derived, deep-rooted, drought-tolerant, and capable of extraordinary complexity, they are the backbone of Kenya's reputation — though both are rust-susceptible, which drove the later disease-resistant hybrids Ruiru 11 and Batian (generally lower in cup quality). Kenya is also known for a distinctive double-wash process: after pulping, beans ferment under water (often 24–72 hours), are washed, then soaked again in clean water — the “Kenyan double wash” that's widely credited for the coffee's exceptional clarity and clean brightness.

Kenya's quality is underpinned by its institutions. Most coffee comes from over 600,000 smallholders who deliver cherry to cooperative-run wet mills called “factories” (names like Gatomboya, Mutwewathi, Ndaroini). Lots are then sold through a competitive weekly auction at the Nairobi Coffee Exchange that rewards quality — top lots command high prices, with a large share going back to farmers, which motivates the whole system to chase excellence. You'll also see grades like AA, AB, and PB (peaberry): crucially, these indicate bean size, not cup quality — a well-processed AB from a top estate can beat a poorly-processed AA, so read the factory name and process alongside the grade. Nyeri and Kirinyaga (on the slopes of Mt. Kenya) are the benchmark regions.

keypoints: Africa's technical powerhouse; fingerprint = blackcurrant, wine-like/phosphoric acidity, savory 'tomato', red volcanic soil.
SL28 & SL34 (Scott Labs, 1930s–40s Bourbon-derived) are the backbone; rust-susceptible → Ruiru 11/Batian hybrids.
Distinctive double-wash process (ferment + soak) → clarity. Sold via Nairobi auction rewarding quality. AA/AB/PB = bean SIZE not quality. Nyeri & Kirinyaga are benchmark.
origin_kenya.blurb: The acidity benchmark — SL28/SL34 on volcanic soil, double-washed, giving intense blackcurrant and a wine-like sparkle.

regionMap.note: Grown on the fertile volcanic slopes around Mt. Kenya, just north of the capital — Nyeri, Kirinyaga, Embu.

origin_guatemala.name: Guatemala

sections.body: Guatemala's high-altitude microclimates, volcanic soil, and steady rainfall make it one of Central America's finest origins. Most Guatemalan coffee is traditionally washed, though naturals and honeys are growing. The regions each have a character: Antigua is prized for a well-balanced, chocolatey, full-bodied cup; Huehuetenango, high and dry, gives brighter, more complex, fruit-and-citrus-forward coffees. Common varieties include Bourbon, Caturra, Catuai, and Typica. High-grown lots are dense — you'll often see 'SHB' (Strictly Hard Bean) marking the highest elevations.

Guatemala is versatile, which is part of its appeal. Washed high-grown lots take a medium roast beautifully, developing the chocolate, caramel, and gentle fruit that make Antigua a crowd-pleaser and espresso-friendly. Huehuetenango's brighter, more acidic profile rewards a slightly lighter hand to keep its complexity and citrus alive. As dense high-grown coffee, it wants adequate energy through the roast; SHB lots especially.

Medium is the sweet spot for most Guatemalans (City+ to Full City), balancing body and sweetness — Antigua in particular shines here and works well in espresso. Push Huehuetenango a touch lighter to preserve its brighter, fruitier complexity for filter. A dependable, flexible origin that suits a range of menus.

Guatemala is one of Central America's great coffee origins, and its defining feature is volcanic geography — dramatic highlands, volcanic soils, and a wide spread of microclimates that give real regional distinctiveness. The national coffee association (Anacafé) formally recognizes eight distinct growing regions, each marketed on its own character, which is unusually organized for a producing country. Coffee is grown at altitude (commonly 1,300–2,000 m), largely shade-grown, hand-picked, and washed, by a mix of smallholders and established estates (fincas).

The classic Guatemalan profile is a clean, complex, washed cup with real depth — chocolate and caramel sweetness, bright but balanced acidity, medium-to-full body, and often notes of spice, orange, or cocoa. Volcanic soil and high altitude give the coffee density and complexity, and it takes roast well across a range, holding sweetness and body even as it develops. Bourbon, Caturra, Catuai, and Typica are the common varieties, with Pacamara and others appearing in specialty lots.

Among the eight regions, a few stand out. Antigua — the famous valley ringed by three volcanoes — is the classic: rich, balanced, chocolate-and-spice, full-bodied, protected by its reputation (and its own authenticity seal). Huehuetenango, high in the northwest and one of the highest non-volcanic regions, gives bright, fruit-forward, high-acid cups. Atitlán (around the lake) is volcanic and full-bodied; Cobán is rainy and gives delicate, fruity coffees. The regional framework means Guatemalan coffee rewards knowing where in the country your bag came from.

keypoints: Volcanic geography + 8 officially recognized regions, each marketed on its own character. High-grown, shade, washed.
Classic cup: clean, complex, chocolate/caramel with balanced bright acidity, medium-full body; takes roast well.
Signature regions: Antigua (volcano valley, rich/balanced, own seal), Huehuetenango (high, bright/fruit), Atitlán (full-bodied), Cobán (rainy, delicate).
origin_guatemala.blurb: High-grown and versatile — Antigua for balanced chocolate, Huehuetenango for brighter complexity.

regionMap.note: Across volcanic highlands — Antigua in the central highlands, Huehuetenango in the west, Atitlán by the lake.

origin_costarica.name: Costa Rica

sections.body: Costa Rica is small — under 1% of world supply — but punches far above its weight on quality, backed by strict national standards (historically Arabica-only) and a thriving micro-mill culture. Tarrazú is the flagship region, with the Central and West Valleys close behind, all high-grown on volcanic soil. Costa Rica is the home of refined honey processing — you'll see it split into white, yellow, red, and black honey by mucilage retained — alongside washed and a growing number of naturals. 'SHB' (Strictly Hard Bean) marks the highest, densest lots.

Classic washed Costa Ricans are clean, bright, and balanced — a medium roast brings out their caramel sweetness and crisp acidity. Honey-processed lots carry more sweetness and body, and reward a careful medium that lets that syrupy character show without baking it. As dense, high-grown SHB coffee, it takes energy well; with the honeys and naturals, mind the extra sugars during development so you don't scorch or bake.

Medium (City+ to Full City) for most — enough to develop the sweetness and body Costa Rica is loved for while keeping its clean acidity. Washed lots can go slightly lighter for filter clarity; honey and natural lots shine at a balanced medium that plays up their fruit and syrupy body. A reliably clean, sweet, crowd-pleasing origin.

Costa Rica is a small producer but a high-quality, high-reputation one, with a coffee culture as developed as anywhere in Central America. It's historically been a leader in quality and organization — famously, it's illegal to grow lower-quality Robusta commercially there (though this has been evolving), reflecting a national commitment to Arabica quality. Coffee is grown at altitude on volcanic soil by many small farmers, and the country has been at the forefront of processing innovation.

sections.h: The micro-mill revolution and honey process
sections.body: Costa Rica is closely associated with two things in specialty coffee: the micro-mill revolution and honey processing. Historically farmers sold cherry to large centralized mills (beneficios); starting in the 2000s, a wave of small producers built their own micro-mills to process and sell their own coffee, capturing more value and enabling traceable, single-farm lots. Costa Rica also popularized honey processing (and its variants — white, yellow, red, black honey), where varying amounts of sticky mucilage are left on the bean during drying to build sweetness and body while keeping cleanliness. The classic cup is clean, sweet, bright, and balanced.

The regions track altitude and volcanic geography. Tarrazú, high in the mountains south of San José, is the flagship — high-grown, crisp, bright, and clean, among the country's most prized. The Central Valley and West Valley (Valle Central and Valle Occidental) are classic, historically important regions, the West Valley especially known for honey-processed lots and distinctive varieties. Other regions like Tres Ríos (the “Bordeaux of Costa Rica”), Orosi, and Brunca round out a small but quality-obsessed origin.

keypoints: Small, high-quality, high-reputation; long Arabica-only tradition (Robusta was banned commercially).
Micro-mill revolution (2000s) → farmers process their own traceable lots; popularized honey processing (white/yellow/red/black).
Regions: Tarrazú (flagship, high/crisp/clean), Central & West Valley (classic, honey lots), Tres Ríos/Orosi/Brunca.

refs.label: Leaderboard Coffee — Costa Rica regions & processing
Tectonic Coffee — Tarrazú & SL28 in Costa Rica

origin_costarica.blurb: The home of refined honey processing — clean, sweet, crisp coffee from a country with strict quality standards.

regionMap.note: Small country, high quality — Tarrazú south of the capital is the flagship, with the Central and West Valleys nearby.

origin_panama.name: Panama
origin_panama.blurb: A small industry that rewired the coffee world when Geisha from Boquete won Best of Panama in 2004. Now the benchmark for floral, tea-like, record-price coffee.

sections.body: Panama is a small producer that punches astronomically above its weight, and it's the origin most associated with Proud Mary's rarest lots. The story is the Geisha variety (also spelled Gesha): originally from Ethiopia, brought to Panama in the 1960s for its leaf-rust resistance, and largely ignored until Hacienda La Esmeralda entered a lot in the 2004 Best of Panama competition. Judges thought an Ethiopian coffee had been slipped in by mistake — it was that floral and elegant. It won, broke price records, and has kept breaking them since (competition lots have sold for thousands of dollars a pound). The growing is centered on the slopes of Volán Barú: Boquete on the east (the historic, balanced region, moderated by a constant mist called the bajareque), Volán/Tierras Altas on the west (higher, drier, cleaner — home to the most awarded Geishas), and Renacimiento (the emerging frontier). Most of it is dense, high-grown Arabica.

A Panama Geisha is the coffee you roast with the lightest possible touch. The entire value is in the aromatics — jasmine, bergamot, orange blossom — and a delicate, tea-like body, all of which a darker roast will trample. Think City to a whisper past first crack, no second-crack development. As dense high-grown coffee it needs enough energy to develop evenly, but the goal is clarity and floral lift, not browning. It's genuinely 'almost a shame to take any darker' — pushing it to medium mutes the florals into a duller, tea-like sweetness and throws away what you paid for. Non-Geisha Panama (Caturra, Typica, Pacamara) is more forgiving and takes a light-medium roast well.

Light (City), dropped just after first crack, to showcase floral aromatics and bright, clean acidity. This is a pour-over and filter coffee above all — the method that lets the fragrance and tea-like clarity speak. Reserve espresso for deliberately showcasing a Geisha shot; most of the time this coffee belongs in a glass, black, no milk.

Panama is a small producer that changed specialty coffee forever, and the reason is one variety: Geisha (Gesha). In 2004, the Peterson family's Hacienda La Esmeralda entered a Geisha — grown from Ethiopian seed stock that had been sitting largely ignored — into the Best of Panama competition, and its intense floral, jasmine, and bergamot, tea-like cup stunned judges and shattered price records. Geisha proved that variety and genetics could dominate flavor, launched the modern obsession with variety, and made Panama synonymous with the pinnacle of coffee quality. Panamanian Geisha regularly sells at auction for hundreds or even thousands of dollars per pound.

Panama's specialty coffee is concentrated in the western highlands near the Costa Rican border, around the Barú Volcano in Chiriquí province. Boquete is the most famous area — high altitude, volcanic soil, misty microclimates, and the cool nights that let Geisha express its floral complexity. Volcán and Renacimiento (on the other side of the volcano) are also important. The combination of high elevation, volcanic soil, and specific microclimates is what lets Panama's best coffees reach their extraordinary aromatic heights.

While Geisha is the headline, Panama produces excellent coffee across varieties. Traditional Bourbon, Caturra, Catuai, and Typica grow alongside the celebrated Geisha, and Panamanian producers are among the most experimental in the world with processing — natural, honey, and anaerobic/extended fermentations that push aromatic intensity further. The country is small and its top lots are expensive and limited, but as a demonstration of just how transcendent coffee can be, Panama sits at the very top of the specialty pyramid.

keypoints: Small producer that changed specialty forever via Geisha — 2004 Best of Panama (Hacienda La Esmeralda) proved genetics dominate flavor; sells for $100s–$1000s/lb.
Specialty concentrated in western highlands (Chiriquí) around Barú Volcano — Boquete, Volcán, Renacimiento; high + volcanic + misty lets Geisha express florals.
Beyond Geisha: excellent Bourbon/Caturra/Catuai/Typica; among the most experimental processors (natural, honey, anaerobic).

refs.label: Proud Mary Academy — the Geisha variety
The Way to Coffee — Boquete origin story

regionMap.note: A tiny sliver of the country near the Costa Rican border — the highlands around Volcán Barú: Boquete and Volcán.

origin_elsalvador.name: El Salvador
origin_elsalvador.blurb: A country whose turbulent history accidentally preserved old-growth heirloom Bourbon — giving juicy, sweet, complex cups and the homegrown Pacamara.

sections.body: El Salvador's coffee is defined by a historical accident: civil war in the 1980s and a commodity-price collapse in the 1990s meant farmers never had the capital to rip out their old Bourbon trees and replace them with higher-yielding, lower-quality varieties, as many neighbors did. The result is a country unusually rich in old-growth heirloom Bourbon, which gives a juicy, sweet, layered cup. El Salvador is also the birthplace of Pacamara — a striking large-beaned cross of Pacas and Maragogipe — which can be intensely floral and complex. Growing is concentrated on the volcanic Apaneca-Ilamatepec range in the west.

Bourbon-driven Salvadorans shine at a balanced medium roast that draws out their signature sweetness — think milk chocolate, caramel, and ripe stone fruit — without tipping into roasty. They're a touch softer in acidity than a Kenyan or a washed Ethiopian, so there's less need to protect brightness with an ultra-light approach; the sweetness is the point. Pacamara lots, being large and sometimes unevenly dense, reward attention through the drying and Maillard phases so the big beans develop evenly. Honey and natural lots carry extra sugar — mind the back half so they don't scorch.

Medium (City+ to Full City) for the classic sweet, balanced, chocolate-and-stone-fruit profile that makes El Salvador such a crowd-pleaser and a strong espresso and milk-drink component. Lighter for a bright washed Pacamara you want to show off on filter. A versatile, approachable origin.

El Salvador is a small country with an outsized place in specialty coffee, and much of that is about one thing: it preserved old Bourbon genetics when other countries replaced them. A very large share of El Salvador's coffee is traditional Bourbon (and its local mutations, Pacas and the famous Pacamara), grown the old way — shade-grown under diverse canopy, hand-picked, at altitude. Where other origins chased higher-yielding or disease-resistant varieties, El Salvador's relative conservatism left it with a treasury of the heirloom Bourbon-family varieties that specialty buyers prize for sweetness and complexity.

Two homegrown varieties define El Salvador. Pacas is a natural dwarf mutation of Bourbon discovered on a Salvadoran farm (the Pacas family) in the 1950s — compact and productive while keeping Bourbon quality. Pacamara is the country's showpiece: a cross of Pacas and the giant-beaned Maragogype, producing enormous beans and a wild, complex, floral-and-fruity cup that's a regular at the top of Cup of Excellence tables. Grown on El Salvador's volcanic soils at altitude, these varieties give the country's classic profile — sweet, balanced, with chocolate, caramel, and gentle stone fruit.

El Salvador's coffee grows along volcanic mountain ranges. The Apaneca-Ilamatepec range (in the west, around Santa Ana and Ahuachapán) is the flagship — high, volcanic, and the source of many of the country's best lots. Alotepec-Metapán in the northwest and the Chichontepec volcano (San Vicente) in the center are also notable. For its size, El Salvador punches far above its weight in specialty, precisely because of the Bourbon heritage and volcanic terroir.

keypoints: Preserved old Bourbon genetics when others replaced them → a treasury of heirloom Bourbon-family varieties prized for sweetness.
Homegrown varieties: Pacas (Bourbon dwarf mutation, 1950s) and Pacamara (Pacas×Maragogype) — huge beans, wild complex cup, Cup of Excellence star.
Regions: Apaneca-Ilamatepec (west, flagship), Alotepec-Metapán, Chichontepec volcano. Punches above its size on Bourbon heritage + volcanic soil.

refs.label: Stone Street — guide to Central American coffee

regionMap.note: Across the volcanic ranges of the west and center — the Apaneca-Ilamatepec range holds the best-known estates.

origin_honduras.name: Honduras
origin_honduras.regions: Copán, Montecillos, Opalaca, Agalta, Comayagua, El Paraíso
origin_honduras.blurb: One of Central America’s largest exporters, once commodity-only, now producing clean, sweet, balanced specialty across six distinct regions.

sections.body: Honduras is a Central American production heavyweight — consistently one of the region's largest exporters (around 5 million-plus 60kg bags a season) — that historically sold mostly into blends, but has built a serious specialty sector over the last decade. It has six recognized growing regions (Copán, Montecillos, Opalaca, Agalta, Comayagua, El Paraíso), each with its own character, spread across mountainous terrain. Investment in processing and drying (its humid climate historically made drying a challenge) has pushed quality up sharply. Expect sweet, clean, well-balanced coffee, sometimes with gentle fruit — approachable rather than wild.

Honduran coffee is forgiving and rewards a balanced medium roast that plays up its caramel sweetness and rounded body. Acidity is moderate and nutty-sweet rather than sharp, so it doesn't demand a light-roast rescue of brightness the way an East African does. As mid-altitude coffee (generally a touch lower and less dense than Guatemala or Panama), it takes heat readily — just avoid rushing so the sweetness develops fully. It's a strong, cost-effective blend and espresso base as well as a pleasant single origin.

Medium (City+ to Full City) for the sweet, caramel, balanced profile that suits both filter and espresso and makes Honduras such a reliable everyday and blending coffee. Slightly lighter for higher-grown regional lots (Montecillos, Opalaca) where you want more fruit and clarity.

Honduras is the largest coffee producer in Central America and one of the biggest in the world — a fact that surprises people, because for a long time Honduran coffee was sold anonymously into blends rather than celebrated as single origin. That has changed dramatically over the past decade-plus: investment in processing, the rise of IHCAFE (the national coffee institute) support, and a growing number of producers pursuing quality have moved Honduras from bulk commodity toward serious specialty, with its own Cup of Excellence lots.

Honduran coffee is grown by hundreds of thousands of smallholders at altitude (commonly 1,000–1,700 m), shade-grown, hand-picked, and washed. The historical challenge was less about the coffee's potential than about infrastructure — drying and processing in a humid climate, and market access — and much of the recent quality gain has come from better post-harvest handling. At its best, Honduran coffee is sweet and balanced with chocolate, caramel, and bright fruit acidity; the range is wide because the country is large and varied. Common varieties include Bourbon, Catuai, Caturra, Lempira, and the rust-resistant hybrids that helped the country recover from leaf rust.

Honduras has six recognized producing regions, each with a character. Copán (west, near the Guatemalan border and the Maya ruins) gives sweet, chocolatey cups. Montecillos (which includes the celebrated Marcala area, with its own denomination of origin) is known for fruit and bright acidity. Comayagua and Agalta produce balanced, clean coffees, and El Paraíso is the largest-producing region. As traceability improves, these regional identities are becoming part of how Honduran specialty is sold.

keypoints: Central America's LARGEST producer; long sold anonymously into blends, now rising fast into specialty (IHCAFE, Cup of Excellence lots).
Hundreds of thousands of smallholders at altitude; recent quality gains mostly from better post-harvest handling in a humid climate.
6 regions: Copán (chocolatey), Montecillos incl. Marcala DO (fruit/bright), Comayagua, Agalta, El Paraíso (largest).

refs.label: Stone Street — guide to Central American coffee

regionMap.note: Spread across six mountainous regions in the west and center — Copán, Montecillos, Opalaca, Agalta, Comayagua, El Paraíso.

origin_nicaragua.name: Nicaragua
origin_nicaragua.blurb: Often described as a blend of its Honduran and Costa Rican neighbors — well-balanced, mild-to-bright, with elegant naturals emerging.

sections.body: Nicaragua sits geographically and stylistically between Honduras and Costa Rica, and its coffee often tastes like a bridge between them: well-balanced, both mildly bright and mildly sweet. Growing centers on the northern highlands — Jinotega and Matagalpa produce the bulk, while high, shade-grown Nueva Segovia and Madriz near the Honduran border yield some of the most refined lots. Volcanic soil and a range of altitudes give a spread of profiles, from soft nutty-caramel to livelier florals and red fruit. It also grows large-beaned Maragogipe and Pacamara. Proud Mary has featured elegant Nicaraguan naturals with brown spice, milk chocolate, and stone fruit.

Nicaragua is versatile and even-tempered — a balanced medium roast suits most lots, drawing out milk-chocolate sweetness, gentle stone fruit, and a rounded body. Acidity is moderate, so there's flexibility: go a little lighter on high-grown Nueva Segovia lots to keep their brightness and floral lift, or a touch fuller for a comforting, sweet everyday cup. Natural and honey lots bring extra fruit and sugar; watch development so the sweetness lands without scorching. Large Maragogipe/Pacamara beans want even heat to develop uniformly.

Medium (City+ to Full City) for a balanced, sweet, chocolatey cup that works across filter, espresso, and milk. Lighter for high-grown or natural lots you want to show off for their fruit and complexity. A dependable, food-friendly origin.

Nicaragua is a mid-sized Central American producer that has steadily built a strong specialty reputation. Coffee is grown at altitude in the north of the country, largely shade-grown on both smallholder farms and larger estates, and Nicaragua has been a consistent strong performer in Cup of Excellence. It shares the broad Central American profile — clean, sweet, balanced, washed coffee — but its best lots show real distinctiveness, and the country has embraced both traditional Bourbon-family varieties and, notably, large-beaned Maragogype and Pacamara.

Nicaraguan coffee grows on rich soils (volcanic in places) at altitude, with the cool, misty highland conditions that build sweetness and complexity. It's typically washed, hand-picked, and increasingly processed with care for traceable, single-farm lots. Bourbon, Caturra, Catuai, Maragogype, and Pacamara are all grown, and the country's specialty cup tends toward sweet chocolate and caramel with balanced fruit acidity and a rounded body — approachable but capable of real complexity in top lots.

Three northern regions lead. Nueva Segovia (in the far north, bordering Honduras) is the flagship specialty region — high-grown, with the country's most celebrated fruit-and-floral lots and a strong Cup of Excellence record. Jinotega is the largest-producing region, giving good body and balance. Matagalpa, historically important and scenic, produces sweet, chocolatey, well-rounded coffee. Together they've moved Nicaragua firmly onto the specialty map.

keypoints: Rising specialty origin; consistent Cup of Excellence performer. Broad clean/sweet/balanced Central American profile.
Grown at altitude in the north; embraces Bourbon-family plus big-beaned Maragogype & Pacamara. Sweet chocolate/caramel + balanced fruit.
Regions: Nueva Segovia (far north flagship, fruit/floral), Jinotega (largest, body/balance), Matagalpa (sweet, chocolatey).

refs.label: Stone Street — guide to Central American coffee

regionMap.note: Concentrated in the northern highlands — Jinotega and Matagalpa produce the bulk; Nueva Segovia sits near the Honduran border.

origin_hawaii.name: Hawaii (Kona)

sections.body: Hawaii is the one U.S. state that produces coffee commercially, and its most famous coffee — Kona — is among the most expensive and recognizable in the world. Coffee arrived in the 1820s (missionary Samuel Ruggles planted it in Kona in 1828), and the Kona Typica variety, a Guatemalan-descended Typica, has been grown there for around two centuries. Genuine 100% Kona is scarce — Hawaii's total output is tiny by world standards — and it commands premium prices, often $40–60+ per pound for the real thing, which is why so much “Kona blend” exists (Hawaiian law lets a bag call itself “Kona blend” with as little as 10% actual Kona, so read labels carefully).

sections.h: The Kona belt and its terroir
sections.body: What makes Kona singular is a very specific place: the Kona Coffee Belt, a narrow strip only about 20–30 miles long and a mile or two wide on the western slopes of the Hualalai and Mauna Loa volcanoes on the Big Island. The magic is the daily weather rhythm — sunny mornings, reliable afternoon cloud cover and gentle rain, mild nights — combined with rich, well-drained volcanic soil and moderate elevation. Around 600–900 small farms work this belt, hand-picking cherry (the terrain and value make machine harvest impractical). That microclimate produces Kona's hallmark cup: smooth, medium-bodied, very low acidity, buttery, with nutty, cocoa, brown-sugar, and gentle fruit notes — crowd-pleasing and mellow rather than bright and wild.

Kona is the headline, but the other Hawaiian coffees are worth knowing — they grow under similar volcanic conditions, compete in the same cupping competitions, and cost considerably less. Kaʻū, on the Big Island's southern tip (former sugarcane country), has risen fast and often shows brighter, fruitier, more complex cups than Kona. Puna and the Hāmākua Coast produce distinctive coffees on the wetter side of the Big Island. And the other islands grow coffee too — Maui (including the large Kaʻanapali estate) tends brighter and more diverse, and there's production on Kauai (one of the largest single estates in the U.S.), Molokai, and Oahu. For value, the non-Kona Hawaiian coffees are often the connoisseur's pick.

keypoints: Only U.S. state growing coffee commercially; Kona = smooth, low-acid, buttery, nutty/cocoa — and very expensive & scarce. Watch for '10% Kona blend' labels.

The Kona belt: a ~20–30 mi × ~1 mi strip on Hualalai/Mauna Loa volcanic slopes. Sunny mornings + afternoon clouds + mild nights + volcanic soil = the mellow signature cup. Kona Typica variety, hand-picked.
Beyond Kona (similar terroir, lower price): Kaʻū (brighter/fruitier, rising star), Puna & Hāmākua Coast, plus Maui, Kauai, Molokai. The value picks.

refs.label: Love Big Island — Kona history & prices
Perfect Daily Grind — Hawaiian coffee production

origin_hawaii.blurb: The only U.S. state that grows coffee commercially — Kona is smooth, mellow, low-acid, and famously expensive.

regionMap.note: The narrow Kona belt on the volcanic western slopes of the Big Island, plus other islands.

origin_vietnam.name: Vietnam

sections.body: Vietnam is the world's second-largest coffee producer after Brazil, and by far the largest producer of Robusta — which makes up roughly 95% of its output and over 40% of all Robusta grown globally. That's a staggering position for a country where coffee was barely a national crop 40 years ago. The French introduced coffee in 1857, but the real story is recent: after the Vietnam War and collectivization, the 1986 Đổi Mới economic reforms reintroduced private land ownership, and the government launched a massive coordinated push — land, loans, irrigation, resettlement — that grew production from under 100,000 tons in 1980 to well over 1.5 million tons by the early 2000s. It's the fastest rise of any major origin in history.

sections.h: Robusta, red basalt, and the Central Highlands
sections.body: Around 80% of Vietnam's coffee grows in the Central Highlands (Tây Nguyên) — provinces like Đắk Lắk (and its capital Buôn Ma Thuột, the coffee heart), Lâm Đồng, and Gia Lai. The region's red basalt (volcanic) soil, distinct wet/dry seasons, and lower elevations are ideal for large-scale Robusta, grown mostly by smallholders on 1–2 hectare plots with intensive methods. The classic Vietnamese cup is bold, heavy, low-acid, and intensely chocolatey, nutty, and earthy — Robusta's punchy, high-caffeine profile. Most of it is sold at scale into instant coffee, espresso blends, and energy drinks; over 90% is exported.

Vietnam isn't just a volume producer — it has one of the world's great coffee cultures. Coffee is brewed single-cup in a small metal drip filter called a phin, traditionally over sweetened condensed milk (used originally because fresh milk was hard to keep in the tropics), producing the iconic cà phê sữa đá (iced coffee with condensed milk) and inventive drinks like cà phê trứng (egg coffee). And the quality story is shifting: Arabica (mostly Catimor, with growing Bourbon and Typica) is expanding in higher-altitude areas like Lâm Đồng, and a specialty-Robusta movement is working to overturn Robusta's low-quality reputation — well-grown, carefully-processed high-grown Robusta can be genuinely good. Vietnam is also, for better and worse, a case study in the environmental cost of rapid coffee expansion (deforestation, water use).

keypoints: World's #2 producer, #1 Robusta origin (~95% of its output, 40%+ of global Robusta). Rose from almost nothing since the 1986 Đổi Mới reforms — fastest rise of any major origin.

~80% grown in the Central Highlands (Đắk Lắk/Buôn Ma Thuột, Lâm Đồng, Gia Lai) on red basalt soil by smallholders. Classic cup: bold, heavy, low-acid, dark-chocolate/nutty/earthy. Mostly → instant, blends, energy drinks.
Great coffee culture: phin filter + condensed milk = cà phê sữa đá, plus egg coffee. Arabica (Catimor) & a specialty-Robusta movement are rising. Also a cautionary tale on deforestation/water.
origin_vietnam.regions: Central Highlands (Đắk Lắk, Lâm Đồng, Gia Lai)
origin_vietnam.blurb: The world's #2 producer and #1 Robusta origin — bold, chocolatey, the base of instant coffee and cà phê sữa đá.

regionMap.note: Concentrated in the Central Highlands (Tây Nguyên) on red basalt plateaus.

origin_yemen.name: Yemen

sections.body: If Ethiopia is where coffee grew wild, Yemen is where coffee cultivation and the coffee trade began. By the 15th century, Sufi monasteries in Yemen were cultivating and brewing coffee as a drink to stay awake for prayer, and the port of Mokha (al-Makha) on the Red Sea became the world's coffee-trading hub — giving its name to “mocha” and, for centuries, a near-monopoly on the coffee trade. This is the origin of coffee as a beverage and a commodity, and Yemeni coffee still carries that weight of history in every cup. The seeds smuggled out of Yemen seeded the coffee that spread across the world.

Yemeni coffee is grown in some of the most extreme conditions of any origin: on hand-built stone terraces cut into steep, arid mountainsides at high altitude (often 1,500–2,400 m), with scarce water, by smallholders using methods essentially unchanged for centuries. The coffee is all natural-processed (dry, sun-dried on rooftops), which suits the arid climate, and it comes from ancient indigenous landrace varieties found nowhere else — genetically distinct even from Ethiopian coffee, recently studied and grouped under names like “Yemenia.” The result is a wild, intense, complex cup: deep dried fruit (raisin, date), winey and boozy notes, warm spice, and heavy body — unmistakable and unlike anything else.

Yemeni coffee is rare and expensive, and the reasons are sobering: tiny yields from harsh terrain, aging trees, water scarcity, the competition from qat (a cash crop farmers often grow instead), and, above all, a devastating ongoing war that has made production and export extremely difficult. Quality is variable and traceability historically poor. Yet the best Yemeni coffees — from regions like Haraz and Bani Matar, and championed by a new wave of specialty exporters — are among the most singular and sought-after in the world, prized precisely for that wild, ancient, dried-fruit intensity that no other origin can replicate. Drinking Yemeni coffee is drinking coffee's origin story.

keypoints: Where coffee CULTIVATION & the coffee TRADE began (15th c. Sufi monasteries; port of Mokha → 'mocha'). Coffee as a drink and commodity started here.

Extreme terroir: ancient stone terraces on steep arid high mountains, scarce water, centuries-old methods. All natural-processed. Genetically distinct ancient landraces ('Yemenia'). Cup: wild, winey, dried fruit (raisin/date), spice, heavy body.
Rare & expensive: tiny yields, aging trees, water scarcity, qat competition, and a devastating war. The best (Haraz, Bani Matar) are singular and among the world's most sought-after.

refs.label: Perfect Daily Grind — Yemen specialty coffee

origin_yemen.blurb: Where coffee cultivation and commerce began — intense, wild, winey, dried-fruit naturals from ancient mountain terraces.

regionMap.note: Hand-built stone terraces on steep, arid high mountains in western Yemen.

origin_rwanda.name: Rwanda

sections.body: Rwanda's specialty coffee story is one of the most remarkable in the industry, and it's inseparable from the country's history. In the aftermath of the 1994 genocide, coffee became a deliberate engine of economic recovery and reconciliation. With support from international development programs in the early 2000s, Rwanda shifted from producing low-value commodity coffee to high-value specialty — building washing stations, training farmers, and organizing smallholders into cooperatives. Within a decade, Rwanda went from an unknown origin to a specialty darling, and in 2008 it became the first African country to host a Cup of Excellence competition. Coffee here is genuinely a story of a country rebuilding through quality.

Rwanda is “the land of a thousand hills,” and that high, hilly terrain (much coffee grows at 1,400–2,000 m) plus rich soils gives excellent growing conditions. Almost all of it is the Bourbon variety (prized for sweetness and quality), grown by hundreds of thousands of smallholders on tiny plots. Like East Africa generally, farmers deliver cherry to centralized washing stations (locally often called CWS — coffee washing stations), and the quality of that station's processing is central to the cup. Rwanda is overwhelmingly washed, and the classic profile is clean, bright, and elegant — floral, red fruit (red currant, cherry), citrus, and a silky body, with none of the earthiness of, say, Indonesia.

sections.h: The potato defect and the modern scene
sections.body: One quirk worth knowing: Rwanda (and neighboring Burundi) occasionally suffers the “potato defect,” where an individual bean tastes and smells like raw potato — caused by a bacterial issue — which producers and roasters manage through careful sorting. Beyond that, the modern Rwandan scene is vibrant: a growing number of quality-focused washing stations, experimentation with natural and honey processing alongside the washed tradition, and strong Cup of Excellence results. Regions around Lake Kivu in the west and the Southern Province are especially noted. Rwanda proved that East African quality isn't limited to Ethiopia and Kenya.

keypoints: Post-1994-genocide specialty success story: coffee as recovery & reconciliation. Went from commodity to specialty in a decade; first African Cup of Excellence host (2008).
High hilly terrain ('thousand hills', 1,400–2,000 m), almost all BOURBON, smallholders → centralized washing stations (CWS). Classic cup: clean, bright, floral, red fruit, citrus, silky.
Watch for the occasional 'potato defect' (a bean tasting of raw potato, bacterial, managed by sorting). Lake Kivu (west) & Southern Province noted; growing natural/honey experimentation.
origin_rwanda.regions: Western Province, Lake Kivu, Southern Province
origin_rwanda.blurb: A post-genocide specialty success story — clean, bright, floral Bourbon washed at cooperative stations.

regionMap.note: High hills across the west (Lake Kivu) and Southern Province.

origin_burundi.name: Burundi

sections.body: Burundi is one of the smallest and least-known specialty origins, but its coffee punches well above its size. A tiny, landlocked, densely-populated country in the Great Lakes region just south of Rwanda, it shares much of its neighbor's profile: high hilly terrain, rich soils, almost entirely Bourbon variety, grown by hundreds of thousands of smallholders on very small plots. Coffee is Burundi's dominant export and a huge share of its economy. Because it's landlocked, getting coffee to port (through Tanzania or Kenya) is a logistical challenge that has historically held the origin back — but the cup quality is excellent.

Like Rwanda and Kenya, Burundi is overwhelmingly washed, with farmers delivering cherry to washing stations (here often called “SOGESTALs” historically, the regional washing-station companies). Grown high (commonly 1,400–2,000 m), the best Burundian coffee is bright, clean, and elegant — juicy citrus and red fruit, floral aromatics, notable sweetness, and a refined body. It's frequently compared to Rwandan and high-grown Kenyan coffee, sometimes with an extra delicacy. The main producing regions are in the north and center — Kayanza and Ngozi especially, and Kirimiro.

Burundi shares the potato-defect issue with Rwanda (managed by sorting) and has faced political and economic instability that complicates its coffee sector. But it has increasingly appeared in Cup of Excellence and specialty menus, and for buyers looking for East African brightness and floral complexity at a relative value, Burundi is one of the most rewarding and underrated origins. As washing-station quality and traceability improve, it's an origin on the rise — a small country whose coffee deserves more attention than it gets.

keypoints: Tiny, landlocked Great Lakes origin just south of Rwanda; coffee is its dominant export. Shares Rwanda's profile: high hills, almost all Bourbon, smallholders. Landlocked logistics held it back.

Overwhelmingly washed at washing stations, grown high (1,400–2,000 m). Best cups: bright, clean, elegant — juicy citrus/red fruit, floral, sweet. Compared to Rwanda & high Kenya, sometimes more delicate.
Regions: Kayanza & Ngozi (north), Kirimiro (center). Has the potato defect (managed by sorting). Underrated, improving, great East African value.
origin_burundi.blurb: Rwanda's small neighbor — high-grown washed Bourbon with elegant, juicy, floral cups; an underrated origin.

regionMap.note: High hills in the north and center of the country.

origin_tanzania.name: Tanzania

sections.body: Tanzania grows coffee across a huge, varied country, most famously on the volcanic slopes of Mount Kilimanjaro and Mount Meru in the north, but increasingly in the southern highlands around Mbeya and in the west near Lake Tanganyika (Kigoma). Coffee was established under German and then British colonial rule and today is a mix of smallholder cooperatives and some larger estates. It's one of Africa's notable Arabica origins (Tanzania also grows Robusta, especially around Lake Victoria), and its northern coffees in particular draw natural comparisons to neighboring Kenya.

Tanzania is strongly associated in the market with peaberry — the natural mutation where a coffee cherry produces a single round bean instead of two flat ones. Peaberries are sorted out and sold as a premium grade (“Tanzania Peaberry” or PB), prized by some for a concentrated, lively cup, and Tanzania markets them heavily. The classic Tanzanian profile, especially from the north, is bright and clean with berry, citrus, and sometimes wine-like acidity — Kenya-adjacent, though often a touch softer. Varieties include Bourbon, Kent (an old India-derived variety), and newer disease-resistant selections; processing is mostly washed, with growing natural and honey lots.

The regional map is worth knowing. The northern Kilimanjaro/Arusha area gives the classic bright, wine-like, Kenya-like cups. The southern highlands (Mbeya, especially the Mbozi area, and Ruvuma) have become a major quality source with clean, sweet, balanced coffees. Kigoma in the far west produces distinctive coffees near the Burundi border. Tanzania faces the familiar challenges of aging trees, smallholder economics, and price volatility, but it remains a reliable source of bright, characterful East African Arabica — and the peaberry branding keeps it visible on menus worldwide.

keypoints: Grown on Kilimanjaro/Meru volcanic slopes (north), the southern highlands (Mbeya/Mbozi), and the west (Kigoma). Notable African Arabica origin (plus some Robusta). Northern coffees are Kenya-adjacent.
Famous for PEABERRY (single round bean mutation) sold as a premium grade — concentrated, lively. Classic cup: bright, clean, berry/citrus, sometimes wine-like. Mostly washed; Bourbon/Kent varieties.
Regions: Kilimanjaro/Arusha (bright, wine-like), southern highlands Mbeya/Mbozi & Ruvuma (clean, sweet, rising), Kigoma (west). Reliable bright East African Arabica.
origin_tanzania.blurb: Home of the famous 'Tanzania peaberry' — bright, berry-and-citrus washed coffee grown on Kilimanjaro's slopes.

regionMap.note: Kilimanjaro/Meru in the north, southern highlands (Mbeya), and the west (Kigoma).

origin_india.name: India

sections.body: India has grown coffee for centuries — legend credits the saint Baba Budan with smuggling seven seeds out of Yemen (Mokha) around 1670 and planting them in the hills of Karnataka, making India one of the oldest coffee cultures outside Arabia. Today India is a significant producer, growing both Arabica and a large amount of Robusta, concentrated in the hills of the south — Karnataka (the biggest by far), Kerala, and Tamil Nadu, along the Western Ghats mountain range. Indian coffee is distinctive for being almost entirely shade-grown under dense forest canopy, often intercropped with spices like pepper, cardamom, and vanilla — which is part of why Indian coffees can carry a spicy character.

sections.h: Monsooned Malabar: a process found nowhere else
sections.body: India's signature contribution is Monsooned Malabar — a processing method unique to the country. Historically, green coffee shipped from India to Europe in wooden sailing vessels was exposed to months of humid sea air, and arrived pale, swollen, and mellow. When faster shipping ended that accidental transformation, producers recreated it deliberately: exposing dry green coffee to the humid monsoon winds on India's Malabar coast for weeks, swelling the beans to a pale golden color and stripping out acidity. The result is a heavy-bodied, extremely low-acid, mellow, musty-sweet cup with notes of spice, chocolate, tobacco, and earth — utterly distinctive and a longtime espresso-blend favorite. It's the clearest example of a flavor created entirely by a place and its climate.

sections.h: The regions and the modern cup
sections.body: Beyond monsooned coffee, India produces plenty of conventional washed and natural Arabica and Robusta. Famous estate areas include Karnataka's Coorg (Kodagu), Chikmagalur (where Baba Budan's hills are), and Baba Budangiri; plus the Nilgiris and other Western Ghats zones. Varieties include Kent and S795 (older, quality Arabicas) and Indian-bred rust-resistant selections like Cauvery (a Catimor). The typical Indian cup — shade-grown, often lower-altitude — leans full-bodied, low-acid, chocolatey and spicy rather than bright, which makes Indian coffee (both Arabica and high-quality “Robusta AA”) a valued blending and espresso component, with a growing single-origin specialty scene.

keypoints: Centuries-old coffee culture (legend: Baba Budan smuggled 7 seeds from Yemen ~1670). Grows Arabica AND lots of Robusta in the southern Western Ghats (Karnataka biggest, Kerala, Tamil Nadu). Almost all shade-grown, often intercropped with spices.

Signature: MONSOONED MALABAR — expose dry green coffee to humid monsoon winds for weeks (recreating old sailing-ship transformation) → pale swollen beans, heavy body, ultra-low acid, musty-sweet, spice/chocolate/tobacco. Espresso-blend favorite.
Also conventional washed/natural Arabica & Robusta. Estate areas: Coorg, Chikmagalur, Baba Budangiri, Nilgiris. Varieties Kent, S795, Cauvery. Typical cup: full-bodied, low-acid, chocolatey/spicy.
origin_india.blurb: Shade-grown in the Western Ghats — home of the unique 'Monsooned Malabar' process and spicy, low-acid cups.

regionMap.note: The hills of the Western Ghats across Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu.

origin_mexico.name: Mexico

sections.body: Mexico is one of the world's larger coffee producers and the biggest in North America, yet it's often overlooked in specialty conversations. Coffee grows in the mountainous south, especially Chiapas (near the Guatemalan border), Oaxaca, and Veracruz. Production is dominated by hundreds of thousands of smallholders, many of them indigenous communities, often farming tiny plots at altitude. Mexico is also one of the world's leading sources of certified organic coffee — a lot of its production is shade-grown with minimal inputs, which has made it a go-to origin for organic and fair-trade buyers.

The classic Mexican profile is mild, clean, and approachable — gentle acidity, light-to-medium body, and chocolate, nutty, and soft-fruit notes, often with a pleasant caramel sweetness. High-grown Mexican coffee (the top grade is labeled “SHG” — Strictly High Grown, or Altura) from Chiapas and Oaxaca can be genuinely complex, while lower-grown coffee is milder and blends well. It's mostly washed, from traditional varieties like Typica, Bourbon, and Mundo Novo plus Mexican-bred selections. The gentle, chocolatey character makes it popular for blends, decaf, and everyday drinking, and increasingly for single-origin specialty from top estates and cooperatives.

Mexican coffee has weathered real hardship — the 2012–13 leaf rust epidemic hit hard, and low prices and migration have pressured farming communities. But the specialty scene has grown: cooperatives and a new generation of producers in Chiapas (regions like the Sierra Madre de Chiapas and near Tapachula), Oaxaca (Pluma), and Veracruz are pushing quality, traceability, and higher-value processing. For buyers, Mexico offers clean, sweet, chocolatey highland coffee — often organic — with a strong story of smallholder and indigenous production, and its proximity to the U.S. market makes it especially significant there.

keypoints: Biggest coffee producer in North America; grows in the mountainous south — Chiapas, Oaxaca, Veracruz. Hundreds of thousands of smallholders (many indigenous). A leading source of certified ORGANIC coffee.

Classic cup: mild, clean, approachable — gentle acidity, chocolate/nutty/soft-fruit, caramel sweetness. Top grade 'SHG' (Strictly High Grown/Altura) can be complex. Mostly washed; Typica/Bourbon/Mundo Novo.
Hit hard by 2012–13 leaf rust; specialty scene rising (Chiapas Sierra Madre, Oaxaca Pluma, Veracruz). Clean, sweet, often-organic highland coffee; especially significant to the U.S. market.
origin_mexico.blurb: North America's big producer — mild, clean, chocolatey highland coffee, and a major source of organic beans.

regionMap.note: The mountainous south — Chiapas, Oaxaca, and Veracruz.

origin_peru.name: Peru

sections.body: Peru is one of the world's larger coffee producers and a giant in the organic and fair-trade world — for years it has been among the top exporters of certified organic coffee globally. Production comes almost entirely from smallholders — hundreds of thousands of small farms scattered across remote Andean and high-jungle regions, many organized into cooperatives that made certification and export possible. Coffee grows across a long stretch of the country, from Cajamarca and Amazonas in the north to Cusco and Puno in the south, generally at good altitude (1,200–2,000 m) on the eastern Andean slopes.

The classic Peruvian profile is mild, sweet, clean, and balanced — gentle acidity, medium body, and nutty, chocolatey, soft-fruit notes. It's approachable and versatile rather than wild or intense, which historically made it a blending and organic staple. But high-grown Peruvian coffee from top regions can be genuinely bright and complex, and the specialty scene has grown a lot: better processing, traceable single-cooperative and single-farm lots, and strong Cup of Excellence results have raised Peru's profile from bulk-organic origin toward serious specialty. It's mostly washed, from Typica, Bourbon, Caturra, and rust-resistant Catimor.

sections.h: Remote geography and the quality story
sections.body: Peru's biggest challenges are geography and infrastructure: much of its coffee grows in remote areas with difficult access, limited processing facilities, and long transport to port, which historically capped quality and traceability. The northern regions — Cajamarca, Amazonas (the celebrated area around Rodríguez de Mendoza), and San Martín — are especially noted for quality, along with Cusco and Puno in the south. As cooperatives invest in wet mills and quality control, Peru is increasingly recognized not just as a reliable organic source but as an origin capable of clean, sweet, and sometimes strikingly complex coffee at real value.

keypoints: One of the world's larger producers and a leader in certified ORGANIC & fair-trade coffee. Almost entirely smallholders across remote Andean/high-jungle regions, organized into cooperatives. Grown 1,200–2,000 m.

Classic cup: mild, sweet, clean, balanced — gentle acidity, nutty/chocolatey/soft fruit. Approachable & versatile; high-grown lots can be bright and complex. Mostly washed; Typica/Bourbon/Caturra/Catimor.
Challenge = remote geography/infrastructure. Top regions: Cajamarca, Amazonas (Rodríguez de Mendoza), San Martín (north), Cusco & Puno (south). Rising from bulk-organic toward serious specialty.
origin_peru.blurb: A major organic and fair-trade origin — gentle, sweet, balanced highland coffee from remote Andean smallholders.

regionMap.note: The eastern Andean slopes, from Cajamarca/Amazonas in the north to Cusco/Puno in the south.

origin_png.name: Papua New Guinea

sections.body: Papua New Guinea (PNG) grows coffee in its remote, mountainous interior — the Highlands provinces (Eastern Highlands, Western Highlands, Simbu/Chimbu, Jiwaka) — and it's one of the more distinctive and rugged origins in the world. Coffee arrived relatively recently (early 20th century, with seed stock famously including Jamaica Blue Mountain Typica), and today production is a striking mix: a small number of large colonial-era plantations/estates, but overwhelmingly hundreds of thousands of smallholders growing coffee in small “gardens” as part of subsistence tribal agriculture. For many rural highland communities, coffee is the main source of cash income.

PNG's cup is distinctive and often described as wild — full-bodied, with bright but rounded acidity, tropical and stone fruit, chocolate, and sometimes an earthy or herbal edge that hints at its Indonesian neighbors (though it's cleaner than Sumatra). It's mostly washed, and the varieties are largely traditional Typica and Bourbon (a Bourbon type known locally as Arusha), giving it good clarity and sweetness. Smallholder coffee is often sold as regional blends (e.g. “PNG Highlands”) while some estates offer traceable single-origin lots — the classic being the AA and A grades from the highlands.

PNG's defining challenge is isolation and infrastructure: extremely rugged terrain, poor roads, tribal land tenure, and law-and-order issues make growing, processing, and transporting coffee genuinely difficult, which caps consistency and traceability. Yet the raw material is excellent — high altitude, good varieties, rich soil — and PNG remains a beloved origin for those who appreciate its full-bodied, fruity, characterful cup. As a producer where coffee is woven into the fabric of highland village life, and one capable of real quality, it's a distinctive and rewarding origin that sits a bit off the specialty beaten path.

keypoints: Grown in PNG's remote, rugged Highlands provinces (Eastern/Western Highlands, Simbu, Jiwaka). Seed stock famously included Jamaica Blue Mountain Typica. Mostly smallholders growing in small 'gardens' as subsistence tribal agriculture — coffee is key cash income.

Cup: distinctive & 'wild' — full-bodied, rounded acidity, tropical/stone fruit, chocolate, sometimes earthy/herbal (neighbor to Indonesia but cleaner). Mostly washed; traditional Typica & Bourbon (Arusha). Sold as Highlands blends + some estate single-origins (AA/A).
Defining challenge = isolation/infrastructure (rugged terrain, poor roads, land tenure) capping consistency. Excellent raw material; a beloved, characterful, off-the-beaten-path origin.

refs.label: Sweet Maria's — Papua New Guinea overview

origin_png.regions: Eastern Highlands, Western Highlands, Simbu, Jiwaka
origin_png.blurb: Rugged highland coffee grown by tribal smallholder 'gardens' — fruity, full-bodied, and distinctively wild.

regionMap.note: The rugged Highlands provinces of the mountainous interior.

origin_java.name: Java

sections.h: The island that named the drink
sections.body: Java is so central to coffee history that its name became slang for coffee itself. It was the first place the Dutch established commercial coffee in Indonesia — seeds smuggled from Yemen planted around Batavia (Jakarta) in the late 1600s, with commercial exports to Europe by the early 1700s, making Indonesia the first place outside Arabia and Ethiopia to grow coffee at scale. For a century Java produced almost entirely Arabica and was Europe's premier source. Java coffee also formed half of the world's first famous coffee blend: “Mocha-Java,” pairing winey Yemeni Mocha with rustic Java Arabica — a combination roasters still make today.

Java's history pivoted on catastrophe: the leaf-rust epidemic of the 1880s devastated the island's Typica Arabica, and the Dutch responded by replanting much of Java with Robusta (and briefly Liberica). Today Java grows a lot of Robusta at lower elevations, but the specialty story is the government-run estates on the Ijen Plateau in East Java — large colonial-era farms (names like Blawan, Jampit, Pancur) at altitude that produce washed Arabica. This estate, fully-washed tradition makes East Javanese Arabica cleaner and less funky than wet-hulled Sumatra — heavier-bodied and spicy, but with more clarity.

The classic Java Arabica profile is full-bodied, low-acid, and rustic — earthy and spicy with notes of cedar, tobacco, and dark chocolate, though the washed East Java estates give a cleaner, sweeter, more refined version than the wet-hulled islands. Some aged Java (“Old Java” / “Old Brown”) is deliberately held to deepen its mellow, woody character. West and Central Java also produce coffee, with high-altitude West Javanese lots showing more fruit. Java is the historical anchor of the whole Indonesian coffee world — the island where it all began, and still a distinctive, spicy, heavy-bodied origin.

keypoints: The island so central to coffee that 'java' = slang for coffee. First commercial Indonesian coffee (Dutch, smuggled Yemeni seed, ~1700). Half of the original 'Mocha-Java' blend.
1880s leaf rust devastated its Typica → much replanted with Robusta. Specialty story = government estates on the Ijen Plateau (East Java): washed Arabica, cleaner than wet-hulled Sumatra.
Cup: full-bodied, low-acid, rustic — earthy, spicy, cedar/tobacco/chocolate; East Java washed estates cleaner & sweeter. 'Old Java' aged for mellow woody depth.

refs.label: Coffee with the Queen — Indonesian coffee island by island

origin_java.regions: Ijen Plateau (East Java), West & Central Java
origin_java.blurb: The island so central to coffee that 'java' became slang for it — rustic, spicy, and half of the original Mocha-Java blend.

regionMap.note: Government estates on the Ijen Plateau in East Java, plus West and Central Java.

origin_sulawesi.name: Sulawesi (Toraja)

sections.body: Sulawesi — the oddly-shaped island east of Borneo, formerly called Celebes — grows most of its Arabica in the mountainous Tana Toraja highlands and the Enrekang district to the south (traded through the town of Kalosi, itself a specialty brand name). The Toraja people are famous for distinctive architecture and elaborate death rituals, and that respect for tradition extends to coffee: over 90% of Sulawesi's Arabica is grown by smallholders on tiny family plots, often planted wild wherever trees fit, at high altitude with big day-night temperature swings. Sulawesi is a smaller, more premium Indonesian origin — its coffee has long commanded high prices, especially in Japan, where demand is fierce.

Like Sumatra, most Sulawesi coffee is wet-hulled (giling basah) — hulled while the bean is still wet — which gives it the low acidity and heavy body characteristic of Indonesia. But Sulawesi is often described as a cleaner, more refined version of the Sumatran profile: full-bodied and earthy, yes, but with a lighter, buttery, syrupy mouthfeel (people compare it to maple syrup), a more intense cedar aroma, and warm spice notes — cinnamon, cardamom, sometimes black pepper — with a smooth, palate-coating finish. Its sweetness, as with most Indonesian coffee, is tied to its body rather than its acidity.

Sulawesi sits between Sumatra's wild earthiness and the cleaner cup of washed origins — bold and distinctly Indonesian, but with more clarity and a gentler, spicier, syrupy character. Beyond Toraja and Kalosi/Enrekang, the regions of Mamasa (west of Toraja) and Gowa (near Makassar in the south) also grow Arabica. For drinkers who find Sumatra too funky but still want that heavy-bodied, low-acid, spicy Indonesian style, Sulawesi is often the sweet spot — which is exactly why it's so prized and priced accordingly.

keypoints: Sulawesi (formerly Celebes) grows Arabica mainly in the Tana Toraja highlands + Enrekang/Kalosi. 90%+ smallholder, high-altitude, big day-night swings. Premium, expensive, especially in Japan.
Wet-hulled (giling basah) like Sumatra → low acid, heavy body — but CLEANER & more refined: buttery/syrupy (maple-syrup) mouthfeel, cedar aroma, warm spice (cinnamon/cardamom/pepper), smooth finish.
The sweet spot between wild Sumatra and clean washed coffees. Other areas: Mamasa, Gowa. Bold but with clarity + spicy, syrupy character.
origin_sulawesi.blurb: Toraja highland coffee (once 'Celebes') — clean, syrupy-bodied, and savory-spicy; a refined cousin of Sumatra.

regionMap.note: The Tana Toraja highlands and Enrekang district in the island's south.

origin_bali.name: Bali (Kintamani)

sections.body: Bali is a relative newcomer to Indonesian coffee — Arabica arrived comparatively recently (brought by traders, not the original Dutch expansion) — and it produces a cup noticeably cleaner and brighter than Sumatra or Sulawesi. Most Balinese specialty Arabica grows in the Kintamani highlands on the slopes of Mount Batur, an active volcano, in rich volcanic soil at altitude. Almost all of it is hand-picked (Balinese farmers are known for careful selective harvesting), and a large share is exported to Japan. Bali is a small-volume, quality-focused origin with a genuinely different character.

Bali's coffee carries a cultural signature: the Subak Abian, a traditional Balinese cooperative farming system rooted in the Hindu philosophy of Tri Hita Karana (harmony between people, nature, and the spiritual), which governs how many farmers organize and cultivate — often organically, intercropped with citrus and other crops. The classic Kintamani cup reflects this: clean, mild, and well-balanced, with bright citrus acidity (sometimes attributed to the citrus grown alongside the coffee), a full but smooth body, and nutty, sweet notes. It's far from the funky earthiness people associate with Indonesia — lighter, cleaner, and more approachable.

Bali is often the gateway Indonesian origin for drinkers who want island character without the heavy wet-hulled funk — washed and natural processing are common here, giving cleaner results. Bali Blue Moon and Kintamani are the names you'll see. Grown in volcanic highland soil under a distinctive cultural-agricultural system, it's a small but rising origin that shows Indonesia's range extends well beyond the classic Sumatran style — proof that the archipelago can produce clean, bright, balanced coffee too.

keypoints: Newer Indonesian origin (Arabica arrived recently). Most specialty Arabica grows in the Kintamani highlands on volcanic Mount Batur. Hand-picked, small-volume, much exported to Japan.
Cultural signature: Subak Abian cooperative system (Hindu Tri Hita Karana philosophy) — often organic, intercropped with citrus. Cup: clean, mild, balanced, bright citrus, smooth body, nutty/sweet.
The clean/bright side of Indonesia (washed & natural common) — no wet-hulled funk. Names: Kintamani, Bali Blue Moon. Approachable gateway to island coffee.
origin_bali.blurb: A newer, cleaner Indonesian origin — bright, mild Kintamani coffee grown under the Subak Abian tradition.

regionMap.note: The Kintamani highlands on the slopes of Mount Batur.

origin_flores.name: Flores (Bajawa)

sections.body: Flores is a small, rugged, volcanic island in eastern Indonesia (the Nusa Tenggara chain) and one of the most exciting up-and-coming origins in the archipelago. Its coffee history is different from the Dutch islands: Flores and neighboring Timor were under Portuguese influence, and their Arabica came from different root stock — and because eastern Indonesia was less affected by the leaf-rust epidemic, some of these older Arabica lines survived where Java's and Sumatra's did not. Flores is dotted with seventeen active and dormant volcanoes, and that volcanic soil at altitude gives its coffee real quality potential.

The heart of Flores specialty coffee is the Bajawa highlands (in the Ngada region), where the Bajawa tribes have grown coffee for generations, largely organically and intercropped, between the Inerie and Ebulobo volcanoes. Flores coffee is prized for a distinctive, clean, sweet profile — chocolate and caramel, floral and sometimes citrus or stone-fruit notes, a smooth full body, and lower acidity, but with more clarity and sweetness than Sumatra. Processing varies (washed, natural, and wet-hulled all appear), and the washed Bajawa lots in particular can be strikingly clean and complex for an Indonesian coffee.

Flores is a small-volume, quality-focused origin that's increasingly sought after by specialty roasters looking for something distinctive from Indonesia beyond the classic Sumatran style. Its combination of surviving old Arabica genetics, rich volcanic terroir, organic smallholder cultivation, and a sweet-and-chocolatey-yet-clean cup makes it stand out. As traceability and processing improve, Flores (especially Bajawa) is exactly the kind of emerging origin that rewards seeking out — an eastern-Indonesian gem still flying somewhat under the radar.

keypoints: Small volcanic island in eastern Indonesia (Nusa Tenggara). Portuguese (not Dutch) coffee heritage — different Arabica root stock, some old lines survived the rust that hit Java/Sumatra. 17 volcanoes.

Heart = Bajawa highlands (Ngada), grown by Bajawa tribes, largely organic/intercropped between volcanoes. Cup: clean, sweet — chocolate/caramel, floral, smooth full body, lower acid, more clarity than Sumatra.
Small-volume quality-focused rising star. Processing varies (washed/natural/wet-hulled); washed Bajawa can be strikingly clean & complex. An under-the-radar eastern-Indonesian gem.
origin_flores.blurb: A rising eastern-Indonesian gem — volcanic Bajawa coffee with chocolate, florals, and a smooth full body.

regionMap.note: The Bajawa highlands in the Ngada region, between volcanoes.

origin_jamaica.name: Jamaica

sections.body: Jamaica Blue Mountain is one of the most famous and expensive coffees in the world, and its reputation rests on a specific place and tight regulation. Coffee arrived in Jamaica in the 1720s, and the Blue Mountains in the island's east — steep, cool, often mist-shrouded slopes — proved ideal. The name “Jamaica Blue Mountain” (JBM) is legally protected: only coffee grown in defined parishes at defined altitudes (roughly 900–1,700 m) qualifies, certified by the Jamaica Coffee Industry regulator. That scarcity, the labor of steep hand-picking, meticulous processing, and enormous demand (especially from Japan, which buys the majority) drive prices to among the highest of any coffee — and, as with Kona, spawn a lot of dubious “Blue Mountain blend” product.

JBM is grown from the classic Typica variety (the Jamaica Blue Mountain Typica, a prized strain that seeded other origins including PNG), washed with great care, and often shipped in wooden barrels rather than jute bags — a traditional mark of the coffee. The high, cool, misty Blue Mountain terroir slows cherry development, and the meticulous production yields a coffee known above all for balance and smoothness: mild, clean, exceptionally well-rounded, with gentle acidity, a smooth medium body, mild sweetness, and subtle nutty, chocolatey, herbal notes — nothing extreme, but polished and refined.

Blue Mountain's profile is the source of both its fame and some specialty-world debate: it's celebrated for being supremely smooth, balanced, and free of any harshness — a “luxury” coffee — but some specialty drinkers, chasing bright acidity and bold distinctive flavors, find it too mild for the price. What's undeniable is its quality, consistency, and cleanliness, and its historical importance. Beyond the Blue Mountains, Jamaica grows other coffee (“High Mountain” and lowland), but JBM is the name that made the island one of the most recognizable origins in the world — mild, refined, and famously dear.

keypoints: Jamaica Blue Mountain (JBM) = one of the world's most famous & expensive coffees. Legally protected name: only defined Blue Mountain parishes/altitudes qualify, certified. Japan buys most. Beware 'Blue Mountain blend'.
Grown from prized Jamaica Blue Mountain Typica (seeded PNG & others), washed carefully, traditionally shipped in wooden barrels. High cool misty terroir slows development.

Cup: prized for BALANCE & smoothness — mild, clean, rounded, gentle acidity, smooth body, subtle nutty/chocolatey. Debated: some find it too mild for the price, but undeniably clean/consistent/refined.
origin_jamaica.regions: Blue Mountains (St. Andrew, Portland, St. Thomas)
origin_jamaica.blurb: Home of Jamaica Blue Mountain — one of the world's most famous, mild, and expensive coffees, tightly certified.

regionMap.note: The steep, misty Blue Mountains in the island's east.

origin_uganda.name: Uganda

sections.body: Uganda is one of Africa's largest coffee exporters and, crucially, one of the places where Robusta (Coffea canephora) is actually native — wild Robusta still grows in Uganda's forests, giving the country a deep historical and genetic connection to the species. Coffee is central to Uganda's economy: it accounts for a large share of foreign-exchange earnings, and several million families depend on it, overwhelmingly smallholders on tiny plots. The bulk of production is Robusta, much of it used in commercial blends and instant coffee — but the story getting more interesting is Uganda's Arabica.

sections.h: Mount Elgon, Rwenzori, and the Arabica rise
sections.body: Uganda's Arabica grows on the mountainous eastern and western borders. In the east, around the extinct volcano Mount Elgon (on the Kenyan border), washed Arabica is produced that's often exported under the traditional name Bugisu (or Bugishu) — and the best of it is genuinely excellent, with the bright, wine-like, fruity character that reflects its proximity to Kenya. In the west, the Rwenzori Mountains (the “Mountains of the Moon”) traditionally used natural processing, historically yielding lower-grade strip-picked coffee known as DRUGAR (Dried Uganda Arabica), though quality-focused production is rising there too. Uganda even has a rare native Arabica-like species, Coffea eugenioides (an Arabica parent), growing wild.

Uganda's coffee is a study in transition: a huge-volume, historically commodity-Robusta origin that's increasingly recognized for quality — both specialty-grade washed Arabica from Mount Elgon and, notably, a growing appreciation for fine Ugandan Robusta as the specialty world reconsiders the species. The best Ugandan Arabica offers bright, fruity, wine-like, full-bodied cups comparable to other East African coffees, while its Robusta can be clean and characterful when well-grown. As processing and traceability improve, Uganda is emerging from behind its commodity reputation as an origin worth serious attention.

keypoints: One of Africa's largest coffee exporters and a place Robusta is NATIVE (wild Robusta still grows there). Coffee = huge share of the economy, millions of smallholder families. Mostly Robusta (blends/instant).

Arabica rising on the borders: east = Mount Elgon washed Arabica (exported as 'Bugisu/Bugishu', excellent, Kenya-like bright/wine-like/fruity); west = Rwenzori naturals (historically low-grade 'DRUGAR', improving).
A commodity-Robusta giant finding quality — specialty washed Elgon Arabica + growing appreciation for fine Ugandan Robusta. Also home to wild Coffea eugenioides (an Arabica parent).

refs.label: FNB Coffee — top exporters incl. Uganda

origin_uganda.blurb: One of the places Robusta is native — a huge African exporter now gaining recognition for Mount Elgon Arabica.

regionMap.note: Mount Elgon in the east (Bugisu), the Rwenzori range in the west, and West Nile.

origin_ecuador.name: Ecuador

sections.body: Ecuador is a small coffee producer that punches above its weight in the specialty world, and it has a geographic advantage few can match: the Andes give it very high altitudes right on the equator, and its position lets it grow both Arabica (in the highlands) and Robusta (in the lowlands and coast). Ecuador is unusual in that it has historically imported coffee to meet domestic and instant-coffee demand even as it exports specialty — but the specialty story is what's exciting, with a new generation of quality-focused producers earning international attention.

Ecuador has become closely associated with a buzzy variety: Sidra (a Bourbon/Typica-related selection, sometimes linked to Ethiopian genetics) that produces intensely aromatic, floral, complex cups and has starred in competitions and fetched high auction prices. Grown at extreme altitude in regions like Loja (in the south) and the northern provinces around Pichincha and Imbabura, and processed with a lot of modern experimentation (washed, natural, honey, and anaerobic), the best Ecuadorian coffee is clean, sweet, floral, and strikingly complex — more in the bright, expressive, high-altitude style than the earthy or chocolatey origins.

Ecuador even grows coffee in one of the most unusual places on earth: the Galápagos Islands, where coffee is cultivated (often organically, in the islands' protected environment) at surprisingly low elevation but under cool oceanic conditions that mimic higher altitude. Mainland Ecuador remains a small, emerging, quality-driven origin — held back historically by economics that made other crops more attractive, but increasingly recognized for exceptional high-grown lots.
