sections.body: A log of numbers is only half the picture — its real power comes from pairing it with the cup. The discipline is to cup each batch (or a regular sample) and write the tasting result next to the curve that produced it. Over time this builds a library: you can see that the batches that stalled in Maillard taste flat, that dropping thirty seconds earlier kept more acidity, that a particular coffee wants more heat than you'd expect. This feedback loop — roast, log, cup, adjust — is how a roaster actually improves, and it's why logging without tasting (or tasting without logging) leaves most of the value on the table.

keypoints: Logged roasts are reproducible and diagnosable; unlogged ones are lucky batches.
Log green/lot, weights (loss %), charge, TP, drying end, first crack, drop, total time, DTR, gas/air moves, tasting note.
Artisan: free, open-source, connects to most machines — the standard starting point.
Cropster: commercial platform adding planning, inventory, QC, BBP — common in larger roasteries.

brew_extraction.name: Extraction Theory

sections.body: Every brew method does the same fundamental thing: hot water dissolves flavor compounds out of ground coffee. How much dissolves is the extraction yield — the percentage of the coffee's dry mass that ends up in your cup. How concentrated the result is, is the total dissolved solids (TDS) — the fraction of the liquid that's dissolved coffee. Extraction yield and TDS are different things, linked by your brew ratio, and nearly all of brewing is managing these two numbers.

Decades of research (the SCA Golden Cup standard, rooted in Lockhart's 1950s work) put the sweet spot at 18–22% extraction yield. Below 18% the coffee is under-extracted: sour, grassy, weak, because you haven't pulled the sugars and desirable compounds yet. Above 22% it's over-extracted: bitter, dry, astringent, because you've started pulling the harsh stuff. The art is landing inside that window. For strength, filter coffee typically targets a TDS around 1.15–1.35%, while espresso is far more concentrated at roughly 8–12%.

sections.h: Why flavors come out in order
sections.body: The reason under- and over-extraction taste the way they do is that coffee's compounds don't all dissolve at the same rate — they leave the grounds in a rough sequence. The most soluble compounds go first: fruit acids and some of the salts, which is why a too-short extraction tastes sour and sharp. Sugars and the compounds that read as sweetness and balance come out in the middle of the extraction — this is the desirable heart you're trying to capture. Last to leave are the heavier, less soluble molecules: bitter compounds and drying, astringent polyphenols, which dominate when extraction runs too far. So the 18–22% window isn't arbitrary — it's the span where you've moved past the raw acidity but haven't yet dragged out the bitterness. Under-extract and you stop in the sour early phase; over-extract and you push into the bitter late phase; hit the middle and you get sweetness and balance. This sequence is why the same coffee can taste sour, sweet, or bitter depending only on how far you take it.

sections.h: Extraction vs strength: two different dials
sections.body: The most common confusion in brewing is treating 'strong' and 'over-extracted' as the same thing — they aren't. Extraction yield is how MUCH of the coffee you dissolved (a quality axis: sour → sweet → bitter). Strength, measured as TDS, is how CONCENTRATED the drink is (a preference axis: weak → intense). They're set by different things: extraction is governed by the four levers, while strength is governed mostly by your brew ratio — how much water you use per gram of coffee. This is why you can have a weak-but-bitter coffee (over-extracted yet dilute) or a strong-but-sour one (concentrated yet under-extracted). A useful mental model is the Brewing Control Chart, the classic grid coffee professionals use: extraction yield runs along one axis, strength along the other, and the 'ideal' box sits where a good extraction (18–22%) meets your preferred strength. Dial extraction with grind and time; dial strength with the ratio. Keeping them separate in your head is what turns guesswork into control.

Four variables control extraction. Grind size sets surface area — finer extracts faster. Water temperature — hotter extracts faster (brewing runs ~90–96°C). Contact time — longer extracts more. And agitation — stirring or turbulence speeds extraction by moving fresh water past the grounds. When a cup is wrong, you're almost always adjusting one of these four to move extraction up or down.

Across every method, one rule holds: sour means under-extracted, bitter means over-extracted. Sour cup → extract more (grind finer, hotter water, longer time). Bitter cup → extract less (grind coarser, cooler, shorter). This single sour-vs-bitter axis is the most useful thing a barista or roaster can internalize.

keypoints: Extraction yield = % of coffee mass dissolved; TDS = concentration; brew ratio links them.
Golden Cup window: 18–22% extraction. Under = sour/weak, over = bitter/dry.
Strength: filter TDS ~1.15–1.35%, espresso ~8–12%.
Four levers: grind, temperature, time, agitation. Sour → extract more; bitter → extract less.

Flavors leave the grounds IN ORDER (by solubility): fruit ACIDS + salts first (too short = sour/sharp) → SUGARS/sweetness/balance in the middle (the heart you want) → bitter compounds + astringent polyphenols LAST (too far = bitter/drying). The 18–22% window = past the raw acidity, before the bitterness. Same coffee tastes sour/sweet/bitter purely by how far you take it.

EXTRACTION vs STRENGTH are different dials (the #1 brewing confusion). Extraction yield = how MUCH you dissolved (quality: sour→sweet→bitter, set by the 4 levers). Strength/TDS = how CONCENTRATED (preference: weak→intense, set mostly by brew RATIO). → you can be weak-but-bitter or strong-but-sour. The Brewing Control Chart grids the two; dial extraction with grind/time, strength with ratio.

refs.label: SCA — towards a new brewing chart (Golden Cup)
Coffee Brews Hub — science of extraction

brew_espresso.name: Dialing In Espresso

sections.body: The modern specialty default is a 1:2 ratio by weight: 18g of ground coffee in, 36g of liquid espresso out, pulled in about 25–30 seconds at 9 bar of pressure and roughly 90–96°C. That 1:2 exists because, with a good grind, most espresso lands cleanly inside the 18–22% extraction window there. It's the starting point for any new bag or setup — always measured by weight, because crema makes volume unreliable.

Dialing in has a sequence, and following it prevents most frustration. First fix your dose and ratio (18g → 36g). Pull a shot and time it. Then adjust grind to hit the target time: if the shot runs too fast (under ~25s), grind finer to slow it; too slow (over ~35s), grind coarser. Only once grind and time are in range do you taste and fine-tune. Most home espresso problems come from changing the ratio when grind was the real variable.

Once it's pulling near 1:2 in 25–30s, taste drives the rest. Sour or sharp means under-extracted — grind finer, or nudge the ratio longer (18g → 40g). Bitter or harsh means over-extracted — grind coarser. Roast level shifts the targets: light roasts are dense and hard to extract, wanting higher temperature (93–96°C) and sometimes a longer ratio; dark roasts extract easily and prefer lower temperature (88–91°C) and often a shorter ratio.

Change the ratio and you change the shot's character. A ristretto (1:1 to 1:1.5) is short and concentrated — syrupy, sweet, less bitter, favored for milk drinks. A lungo (1:3 and up) is long and dilute — lighter and more aromatic, sometimes good for very light roasts. The 1:2 normale sits balanced between them and is the default for a reason.

keypoints: Standard: 1:2 by weight (18g → 36g), 25–30s, 9 bar, ~90–96°C. Weigh everything.
Dial-in order: fix dose/ratio → adjust grind to hit time → then taste and fine-tune.
Sour = grind finer / longer ratio; bitter = grind coarser. Light roast hotter, dark roast cooler.
Ristretto (1:1–1:1.5) concentrated/sweet; lungo (1:3+) light/dilute; 1:2 is the balanced default.

refs.label: Podium Coffee Club — espresso brew ratio

brew_machine.name: The Espresso Machine

sections.body: Every espresso machine, from a $400 home unit to a commercial three-group, does the same fundamental thing: force hot water through a compacted bed of finely-ground coffee at around nine bars of pressure. The easiest way to understand any machine is to follow the water through four stages: a source (tank or plumbed line), a pump that builds the pressure, a boiler that heats the water, and a group head that distributes it evenly through the coffee held in the portafilter. Everything else — PIDs, dual boilers, pressure profiling — is refinement of how precisely those four stages are controlled.

sections.h: The pump: where pressure comes from
sections.body: Espresso needs about 9 bars of pressure, and a pump provides it (steam-pressure and manual lever machines are the exceptions). Two pump types dominate. Vibration (vibratory) pumps are compact, affordable, and common in home machines; they're a little noisy and ramp up pressure gradually — which incidentally gives a brief natural pre-infusion. Rotary pumps use a spinning motor for quieter, steadier pressure and long life (15+ years), and can be plumbed to a water line; they're the commercial standard and larger and pricier. Many machines include an OPV (over-pressure valve) to cap pressure at the target so the shot sees a consistent ~9 bars.

sections.h: The boiler: single, heat exchanger, dual, thermoblock
sections.body: The central design question is how a machine gets water to brew temperature (~90–96°C) while also making much hotter steam (~125°C+) for milk. Four approaches: A single boiler holds one temperature at a time, so you brew, then wait ~30–60 seconds for it to heat up to steam — cheap, compact, fine for straight espresso. A heat exchanger (HX) runs one hot steam boiler with a pipe of fresh brew water passing through it, so you can brew and steam at once — but the brew water can sit and overheat, so you 'temperature surf' (purge a little water first). A dual boiler has two separate boilers, one for brew and one for steam, giving the most stable, independently-controllable temperatures and simultaneous operation — the priciest. A thermoblock/thermocoil flash-heats small amounts of water on demand — cheap and fast but less stable for high volume.

sections.h: PID, the group head, and pressure profiling
sections.body: Temperature stability is what separates a good shot from a lucky one, and a PID (proportional-integral-derivative) controller is the key upgrade: instead of a boiler cycling fully on and off (swinging 5–10°F), a PID reads the temperature continuously and nudges the heater in small increments, holding within about 1°F. The group head is where water meets coffee; the classic Faema E61 group (from 1961) uses a thermosiphon to stay heated and enables pre-infusion. The frontier is pressure profiling and flow control: rather than a flat 9 bars, advanced machines let you ramp pressure up gradually (a gentle pre-infusion / bloom under low pressure), which reduces channeling and especially helps dense light-roasted single origins that would otherwise channel at full pressure. This is the same instinct as everything else in specialty — more control over the variables that shape the cup.

keypoints: All espresso machines force ~9 bar water through coffee. Follow the water: source → pump → boiler → group head/portafilter.
Pump: vibratory (cheap, compact, gradual ramp = natural pre-infusion) vs rotary (quiet, steady, long-lived, commercial, plumbable). OPV caps pressure at ~9 bar.

Boiler: single (one temp at a time, wait to steam), heat exchanger (brew+steam at once, needs temp surfing), dual boiler (two boilers, most stable/simultaneous, priciest), thermoblock (flash-heat, cheap, less stable).
PID holds temp within ~1°F (vs 5–10°F swings) — the key consistency upgrade. E61 group enables pre-infusion. Pressure profiling/flow control ramps pressure to cut channeling, esp. for light roasts.

refs.label: Clive Coffee — how espresso machines work
CoffeeRoast Co — single vs dual boiler & PID

brew_machinetypes.name: Espresso Machine Types

sections.body: Every espresso machine has one job at its core: force hot water through a compacted puck of coffee at around 9 bars of pressure. How a machine generates that pressure is the first thing that divides the categories. The oldest method is steam pressure (cheap, weak, and rarely above 1.5–2 bars — not real espresso). The breakthrough was Achille Gaggia's 1948 lever machine, which used a hand-pulled spring-loaded piston to reach 8–10 bars and, for the first time, produced crema. Then Faema's legendary E61 in 1961 introduced the motor-driven pump and heat exchanger, and the electric pump — vibratory or rotary — became the standard for nearly every machine made today. Manual lever machines still exist (and are beloved by purists for their control and 30–50-year longevity), but most of the decision now is about pump machines and, specifically, their boiler design.

The simplest and most affordable pump machine has one boiler that does everything — it heats water for brewing, then you flip a switch and it reheats to a higher temperature for steaming milk. The catch is right there: you can't brew and steam at the same time, and you have to wait for the boiler to change temperature between the two (annoying when making milk drinks, fine when pulling a solo shot). Single boilers are compact, cheap to run, and quick to heat up, and — crucially — a good one with a PID temperature controller can hold brew temperature every bit as precisely as far pricier machines. For someone making one or two drinks at a time, a PID single boiler is excellent value (the Rancilio Silvia and Lelit Anna are classics).

To brew and steam simultaneously, there are two solutions. A heat exchanger (HX) has one large steam boiler with a pipe running through it: cold brew water is drawn through that pipe (via natural thermosiphon circulation) and heated to brew temperature on the way to the group, without mixing with the hot steam-boiler water. It's an elegant compromise — simultaneous brew-and-steam at a lower price and size than a dual boiler — but the brew temperature isn't set directly, so on non-PID HX machines you 'cooling-flush' the group briefly before pulling to knock the temperature down to the right range (a learnable but imprecise ritual). The E61 group with its thermosiphon is the classic HX design. A dual boiler is the premium answer: two entirely separate boilers, one held at brew temperature and one at steam temperature, each often PID-controlled. You get rock-solid, directly-set brew temperature and no waiting or flushing — grind, dose, brew, steam, all at once. It's what serious home baristas and cafes want, and what makes light roasts and back-to-back milk drinks easiest.

sections.h: PID, pressure profiling, and the levers of control
sections.body: Two more concepts separate machines. PID (a proportional-integral-derivative temperature controller) replaced the old mechanical pressurestat: it holds boiler temperature far more precisely and, on many machines, lets you set brew temperature to the degree — the single biggest gain in shot consistency, and especially valuable for temperature-sensitive light roasts (which often want 94–96°C). Pressure profiling (and flow control) is the frontier: instead of a flat 9 bars, the machine varies pressure during the shot — a gentle low-pressure pre-infusion to wet the puck and reduce channeling, ramping to full pressure, sometimes declining at the end (which mimics the natural taper of a lever machine and is prized for sweetness). Lever machines do this pressure taper inherently; high-end pump machines (Slayer, Lelit Bianca's flow paddle, the Decent) make it adjustable. One important caveat: PID sets the boiler, not the temperature in the cup — the group head, your flush routine, and workflow all still matter. And the honest bottom line from decades of testing: within-shot temperature or pressure quirks matter far less than stability and a good grinder — machine category is about convenience and control, not a guarantee of better coffee on its own.

keypoints: Core job: ~9 bars through the puck. Pressure sources: steam (weak, not real espresso), LEVER (Gaggia 1948, spring piston, first crema, declining pressure, 30–50yr life), or electric PUMP (vibratory/rotary) — the modern standard (Faema E61, 1961).

Single boiler: one boiler brews AND steams, switch between (can't do both at once, wait to change temp). Compact, cheap, quick; a PID single boiler holds temp as well as pricey machines. Great value for 1–2 drinks.

Heat exchanger (HX): one steam boiler + a pipe drawing brew water through it (thermosiphon) → simultaneous brew+steam, cheaper/smaller than dual boiler, but temp isn't direct — needs a 'cooling flush' (E61 classic). Dual boiler: two separate boilers (brew + steam), often PID → rock-solid direct temp, no flush/wait; the premium serious-barista choice.

PID (vs old pressurestat) = precise, settable brew temp — biggest consistency gain, key for light roasts (94–96°C). Pressure profiling/flow control = varying pressure in the shot (pre-infusion → full → taper); levers do it inherently. Caveat: PID sets the BOILER not the cup; stability + grinder matter more than machine category.

refs.label: That Espresso Guy — machine categories & history
Home Grounds — HX vs dual boiler

brew_grind.name: Grind & Burrs

sections.body: Grind size is the single most powerful lever in brewing because it sets the surface area water works on. Finer grounds expose more surface, so water dissolves compounds faster; coarser grounds slow extraction down. This is why grind is the primary thing you adjust to move a cup in or out of the ideal window — it changes extraction more than any other single control.

Each method wants a grind matched to its contact time. Espresso is fine (25–30 second shot). Drip and pour-over are medium (4–6 minute brew). French press is coarse (a 4-minute steep). The logic is consistent: short, intense methods need fine grounds to extract enough quickly; long, gentle methods need coarse grounds so they don't over-extract.

A burr grinder crushes beans between two shaped surfaces to a consistent, adjustable size. A blade grinder chops randomly, producing a chaotic mix of dust and boulders. That inconsistency is fatal to good coffee: the dust over-extracts (bitter) while the boulders under-extract (sour) in the same cup. A quality burr grinder is the single most important piece of brewing gear — more than the machine — because consistent particle size is what makes extraction predictable.

sections.h: Fresh grind, and the CO2 effect
sections.body: Grind immediately before brewing; ground coffee stales in hours as aromatics escape and oxidation accelerates. Freshness also affects the shot mechanically: very fresh beans still full of CO2 create more resistance and run slower, while degassed beans run faster — which is part of why the same grind setting behaves differently across a bag's life.

sections.h: Grinding fresh, and why it’s non-negotiable
sections.body: Grind timing matters as much as grind size. Coffee's aromatics are volatile and its huge new surface area oxidizes fast, so ground coffee goes stale in minutes to hours — far faster than whole beans, which hold for weeks. This is why every serious setup grinds immediately before brewing and never stockpiles grounds. It's also why pre-ground supermarket coffee, however good the bean, is already compromised. The practical rule: buy whole bean, grind only what you're about to brew, and if you taste a flat, hollow cup from good beans, stale grind is a prime suspect.

Not all grinders are equal, and the difference shows up in the cup. A blade grinder chops beans unevenly into a mix of dust and boulders, which extract at different rates — the dust over-extracts (bitter) while the boulders under-extract (sour) in the same brew, muddying everything. A burr grinder crushes beans between two surfaces set a fixed distance apart, producing far more uniform particles that extract evenly, and it lets you dial a repeatable, specific grind size for each method. For anyone serious about coffee, a burr grinder is the single highest-impact equipment upgrade — often more than the brewer itself.

keypoints: Grind sets surface area — finer extracts faster, coarser slower; it's the primary extraction lever.
Match to method: espresso fine, drip/pour-over medium, French press coarse.
Burr grinders give consistent size; blades make dust + boulders that over- and under-extract at once.
Grind fresh; CO2 in fresh beans adds resistance, so grind behaves differently as a bag ages.

refs.label: Complete Home Barista — extraction chart & grind

brew_grinder.name: The Grinder
brew_grinder.sub: The most important piece of gear

sections.h: Why the grinder matters more than the machine
sections.body: Ask experienced baristas where to spend money and most will say the grinder before the espresso machine — because if you can't produce a consistent, uniform grind, you can't produce a consistent shot, no matter how good the machine is. The grinder is what turns beans into the particle bed that water flows through, and the size and uniformity of those particles set the extraction. A great machine fed by a bad grinder makes bad coffee; a modest machine fed by a great grinder can make excellent coffee. This is the single most under-appreciated priority in home coffee.

The foundational divide is burr vs blade. A blade grinder chops beans into a random mix of dust and boulders that extract at wildly different rates — the dust over-extracts bitter while the boulders under-extract sour, in the same cup. A burr grinder crushes beans between two hard surfaces set a precise distance apart, producing far more uniform particles and a repeatable, dialable grind size. Burrs also run cooler and preserve aromatics. For anyone serious, a burr grinder is non-negotiable — it's the difference between guessing and controlling.

Among burr grinders, the two geometries are conical and flat, and the difference is real if subtle. Conical burrs are a cone-shaped inner burr inside a ring; gravity feeds beans down through them, they're efficient, run cooler, retain less coffee, and are common from cheap hand grinders up to high-end machines — many find them more forgiving and they tend to produce a slightly punchier, textured, 'gooey' espresso (they make more fines, adding flow restriction). Flat burrs are two parallel serrated discs; beans are flung outward by centrifugal force and cut to a very uniform (unimodal) particle size, which many argue gives cleaner, more flavor-clarity espresso — the commercial-espresso standard — but they run hotter, draw more power, and often retain more grounds. Neither is strictly 'better': flats for clarity and consistency, conicals for forgiveness, body, and lower retention.

Three finer factors separate good grinders from frustrating ones. Alignment: burrs only produce uniform particles if the two burrs are truly parallel; poor factory alignment (common even on pricey grinders) widens the particle spread and hurts the cup — enthusiasts sometimes shim burrs to align them. Retention: coffee trapped in the burr chamber and chute is a problem — it goes stale and mixes into your next grind, and it means the weight you put in isn't the weight you get out. Low-retention (often single-dose) grinders minimize this; with a hopper grinder, purge a few grams each morning to clear yesterday's stale grounds. Adjustment: stepped grinders 'click' between fixed positions (easy to repeat, but the perfect setting can fall between clicks), while stepless grinders allow infinite micro-adjustment — important for espresso, where humidity, bean age, and even the day's temperature shift the ideal grind and you need to compensate in tiny increments.

keypoints: The grinder matters more than the espresso machine — no consistent grind, no consistent shot. Highest-impact home upgrade.
Burr (crushes to uniform, dialable particles, runs cool) beats blade (random dust + boulders → simultaneously bitter and sour). Non-negotiable for serious coffee.

Conical: gravity-fed cone-in-ring, efficient, cooler, LOW retention, forgiving, punchier/textured shot (more fines). Flat: parallel discs, centrifugal, very uniform (unimodal) = clean clarity, commercial-espresso standard, but hotter and often HIGHER retention.

Alignment (parallel burrs = uniform grind; often imperfect from factory), retention (stale grounds trapped in chamber/chute — purge hopper grinders, prefer single-dose/low-retention), and stepped vs stepless adjustment (stepless = fine espresso dialing) all matter.

refs.label: Prima Coffee — conical vs flat & retention

brew_puckprep.name: Espresso Puck Prep

sections.body: Espresso forces water through a compacted bed of coffee at ~9 bars, and for a good shot that water must meet uniform resistance and pass evenly through every part of the bed. When the puck has clumps, gaps, or uneven density, water does what water does — finds the path of least resistance and channels through the weak spots, blasting some grounds (over-extraction, bitter) while barely touching others (under-extraction, sour). A channeled shot tastes both sour and bitter at once, lacks sweetness, and sprays if you're using a bottomless portafilter. Nearly all of puck prep exists to prevent channeling by making the bed as uniform as possible before water ever hits it. As the saying goes, espresso is about 80% preparation.

Ground coffee clumps — from static electricity (worse in dry air) and from fines sticking together — and those clumps are density variations that cause channeling. The Weiss Distribution Technique (WDT), popularized by John Weiss in 2005, fixes this: you use a tool of thin needles (typically 0.3–0.4 mm, acupuncture-gauge) to gently stir the grounds in the basket, breaking clumps and evening out distribution before tamping. Hold it like a pencil, make slow circles from the bottom up, keep a dosing funnel on to stop grounds escaping. The key is gentleness — aggressive stirring drives fines to the bottom (fines migration) and can create its own problems, and needles thicker than ~0.4 mm just push grounds around and make new channels instead of breaking clumps.

After WDT the bed needs to be flat and level, because an uneven surface produces uneven tamping pressure, which recreates the density differences you just fixed. Two habits help before you even distribute: dose into a dry, room-temperature portafilter, and move the portafilter in a circle while grinding so grounds don't pile in the center. Then level — either a light tap of the portafilter to settle everything, or a distributor tool (a spinning blade set to a shallow depth, rotated with light pressure) to sweep the surface flat. Some baristas add a paper or metal puck screen on top at this stage, which helps keep the shower screen clean and can promote even flow. The goal at the end of this step is a flat, even, uniformly-dense bed.

The final step compresses the bed into a solid puck, and the single most important thing about tamping is that it's level, not that it's hard. A tamp that leans even slightly creates a denser side that water avoids — a direct route to channeling — so the priority is a perfectly flat, straight-down press (use your fingers against the basket rim to feel for level). Pressure matters far less than people think: a firm, consistent press until the bed stops compressing is plenty (commonly cited as ~15–30 lb, but consistency matters more than the exact number), and you don't need to twist or 'polish.' One straight press, then lock in and pull immediately. And the humbling truth underneath all of it: great puck prep can't rescue stale or badly roasted coffee — the beans set the ceiling, prep just makes sure you reach it.

keypoints: Channeling = water finding paths of least resistance through an uneven puck → simultaneously sour AND bitter, no sweetness, spritzing. Puck prep exists to prevent it. Espresso is ~80% prep.

WDT (Weiss Distribution Technique, 2005): thin 0.3–0.4mm needles gently stir out clumps (from static + fines) before tamping. Be GENTLE — aggressive stirring causes fines migration; too-thick needles make new channels.
Distribute & level: dose into a dry room-temp portafilter, circle while grinding, then tap or use a distributor to flatten. Optional puck screen on top.

Tamp LEVEL, not hard: a tilted tamp makes a dense side water avoids. Firm consistent straight press (~15–30 lb, consistency > amount), no twist, pull immediately. Prep can't fix stale/bad beans — beans set the ceiling.

refs.label: Mug Cult — puck prep & tamping workflow

brew_dialing.name: Dialing In Espresso

sections.h: The three numbers of an espresso recipe
sections.body: Every espresso shot is described by three measurements, and learning to control them is the single most important skill in espresso — the thing that separates consistently good cafes from inconsistent ones. Dose is the weight of dry ground coffee going into the basket (in grams). Yield is the weight of liquid espresso coming out (also grams, weighed on a scale under the cup). Time is how long the shot runs. You cannot dial in by eye; you need a burr grinder and a scale. A standard starting recipe: 18 g dose, 36 g yield, about 27–30 seconds — and everything else is adjusting from there by taste.

The relationship between dose and yield is the brew ratio, and it sets the shot's strength and style. The classic modern ratio is 1:2 — 18 g in, 36 g out — balanced and versatile, good straight or in milk. Shorter ratios concentrate the shot: a ristretto (~1:1 to 1:1.5) is thick, intense, and syrupy. Longer ratios stretch it: a lungo (~1:3+) is milder and more diluted. Ratio is a flavor lever, too — a common move is that if a shot tastes too sour, pull a longer ratio (say 18 g to 40 g) to extract more; if it tastes too bitter, pull a shorter one (18 g to 32 g). Decide your target ratio first, then use grind and time to hit it.

With dose and target yield fixed, grind size is the primary control, because it changes how fast water flows through the puck and therefore both the time and the extraction. Grind finer and you increase resistance: the shot runs slower, contact time rises, and you extract more (fixes sour). Grind coarser and water flows faster: shorter time, less extraction (fixes bitter). The dial-in loop is: pull a shot at your recipe, taste it (after letting it cool 30 seconds — tasting it screaming-hot tells you nothing), and adjust grind in tiny steps, changing only one variable at a time and verifying with the next shot. It usually takes three to five shots to dial in a fresh bag.

The crucial mindset: chase a taste target, not a number. A shot that 'hits the time' but tastes wrong isn't dialed in — it's merely timed. Sour and sharp means under-extracted (grind finer, or lengthen the ratio, or raise temperature a touch, especially for light roasts). Bitter and drying means over-extracted (grind coarser, shorten the ratio, or lower temperature slightly for dark roasts). Beyond grind, dose and temperature are secondary levers (light roasts often want hotter water, ~94–96°C; dark roasts a bit cooler), and pre-infusion — a gentle low-pressure wetting before full pressure — improves even saturation and lets you grind finer without channeling. Finally, expect drift: coffee changes as it ages off-roast (you'll usually grind finer over days), and even humidity and temperature shift the ideal grind day to day — which is why baristas re-dial and taste continually, not once. And no recipe rescues stale or badly-roasted beans; the beans set the ceiling.

keypoints: Three numbers: dose (dry grams in), yield (liquid grams out), time. Need a burr grinder + scale. Standard start: 18g → 36g in ~27–30s. Dialing in is the #1 espresso skill.
Brew ratio (dose:yield) sets strength: 1:2 balanced; ristretto ~1:1–1.5 (intense/syrupy); lungo ~1:3+ (mild). Too sour → longer ratio; too bitter → shorter.

Grind is the master dial: finer = more resistance, slower, more extraction (fixes sour); coarser = faster, less extraction (fixes bitter). Change ONE variable at a time; taste after cooling 30s; ~3–5 shots to dial a new bag.

Chase TASTE not the clock. Secondary levers: dose, temp (light roast hotter ~94–96°C, dark cooler), pre-infusion (even saturation, grind finer without channeling). Recipe drifts as beans age + with humidity — re-dial continually. Beans set the ceiling.

refs.label: Clive Coffee — dialing in by taste
My Coffee Explorer — dialing in guide

brew_methods.name: Brew Methods

sections.body: Most brewing falls into three families. Percolation (filter) — water passes through a bed of grounds once and drains away: pour-over (V60, Chemex), drip machines. Immersion — grounds steep in water for a set time, then separate: French press, cupping, cold brew. Pressure — hot water forced through a compacted puck: espresso and its stovetop cousin the moka pot. Each family extracts differently and suits different grind sizes and ratios.

The specialty workhorse for showcasing origin clarity. The SCA ratio guide is 1:16 to 1:17 — roughly 60g of coffee per liter of water — at 90–96°C, medium grind, over about 4–6 minutes total. Pour-over rewards technique: an even bloom (a short pre-wet that lets fresh coffee degas) and steady pouring produce even extraction. It's the method that best highlights a light roast's acidity and aromatics.

The most forgiving family, because contact time is fixed and even. French press uses a coarse grind and a ~4-minute steep, then you press the grounds down; it gives a heavy, full body since no paper filter removes oils. Cold brew steeps a coarse grind in cold water for many hours (often around 1:8), producing a low-acid, smooth concentrate. Immersion is hard to mess up, which makes it a good place to taste a coffee honestly.

The roast and the method should suit each other. Light and medium-light roasts shine in filter and pour-over, where their acidity and clarity come through. Fuller and darker roasts, and purpose-built espresso profiles, come alive under pressure where body and sweetness concentrate. This is exactly the 'use for' guidance on each roast profile in this app — the brew method is the other half of that decision.

keypoints: Three families: percolation/filter (pour-over, drip), immersion (French press, cold brew), pressure (espresso, moka).
Filter: SCA 1:16–1:17 (~60g/L), 90–96°C, medium grind, ~4–6 min; bloom for even extraction.
Immersion: fixed even contact time, forgiving; French press = full body, cold brew = low-acid concentrate.
Light/medium roasts favor filter; darker and espresso profiles favor pressure.

brew_pourover.name: Pour-Over, Step by Step

sections.h: What pour-over is and why people love it
sections.body: Pour-over is a percolation method: you pour hot water over a bed of ground coffee in a filter cone, and gravity pulls it through once into the cup below. Because you control the water — how fast, where, in how many pours — you control the extraction, which makes pour-over the method for clarity and for showing off a distinctive single origin. The paper filter traps oils and fines, giving a clean, transparent, tea-like cup that highlights acidity and aromatics. The trade-off is that it rewards technique: an uneven pour or wrong grind shows up immediately.

Most pour-overs follow the same shape. Rinse the paper filter with hot water (removes papery taste, preheats the vessel) and discard that water. Add grounds, make a well. Start a timer and pour twice the coffee's weight in water to wet all the grounds — the bloom — and wait 30–45 seconds while the coffee degasses and puffs up. Then pour the rest in slow, controlled additions (often concentric circles or pulses), keeping the bed level, until you hit your target water weight. A rough starting ratio is 1:16 to 1:17 (e.g. 22 g coffee to ~360 g water). Total brew time is typically 2:30–4:00. A gooseneck kettle and a scale make it repeatable.

The three common cones differ in ways you can taste. The Hario V60 has a steep 60° cone, a large single hole, and spiral ridges — fast flow, bright and expressive, but less forgiving and best with a medium-fine grind and a steady pour. The Chemex uses a thick bonded filter and brews up to several cups; it flows slower, strips the most oils, and gives the cleanest, smoothest cup — use a slightly coarser (medium-coarse) grind, and it pairs beautifully with light roasts. The Kalita Wave has a flat bottom and three small holes, which regulates flow and makes it the most forgiving of the three, closer to the V60 in grind. None is “best” — V60 for expressive single cups, Chemex for a clean carafe for a few people, Kalita for forgiving consistency.

keypoints: Pour-over = percolation: water passes once through a filter bed. Paper filter → clean, bright, tea-like cup that showcases origin.
Universal method: rinse filter → add grounds → bloom (2× coffee weight, 30–45s) → slow controlled pours to target. Start ~1:16–1:17, 2:30–4:00 total. Gooseneck + scale help.
V60: fast, bright, medium-fine, needs technique. Chemex: thick filter, cleanest, medium-coarse, serves several. Kalita Wave: flat-bottom, most forgiving.
Grind and pour matter more here than any other method — it rewards and exposes technique.

refs.label: Perfect Daily Grind — brewing methods compared

brew_immersion.name: Immersion Brewing

sections.body: Immersion brewing fully submerges the grounds in water for a set time, then separates them. Because every ground sits in water for the same duration, immersion is inherently more even and forgiving than pour-over — there's no pour technique to master, so it's very hard to make a truly bad cup. The character depends heavily on the filter: a metal mesh (French press) lets oils and fine sediment through for a heavy, rich, textured cup, while a paper microfilter (AeroPress) gives a cleaner result.

The classic immersion brewer. Use a coarse grind (like breadcrumbs) to avoid muddy sludge and a hard plunge, and water just off the boil (~200°F/93°C). A good starting ratio is about 1:15. Add grounds, pour water, optionally bloom 30 seconds, then top up, stir gently, and steep four minutes total before plunging the metal filter down slowly and evenly. Crucially, decant immediately — leaving coffee sitting on the grounds keeps extracting and turns it bitter. The metal filter's signature is a full, heavy body with some sediment; it suits darker, chocolatey coffees well.

Invented by Alan Adler in 2005, the AeroPress steeps like a small French press but then pushes the coffee through a paper microfilter with gentle hand pressure, giving a fast, clean, low-bitterness, low-sediment cup that's remarkably forgiving of grind and pour. It uses a fine-to-medium grind (around table salt), a short steep, and often a lower water temperature (~80–85°C works well). There are two camps: the standard method (brew right-side-up, press into the cup) and the inverted method (assemble upside down so nothing drips during a longer steep, then flip and press). It's endlessly tweakable — grind, temp, time, pressure are all in your hands — which is why it has its own world championship, and it's the go-to travel brewer.

keypoints: Immersion = full submersion for a set time, then separate. Even and forgiving; no pour technique. Filter defines the cup.
French press: coarse grind, ~93°C, ~1:15, steep 4 min, plunge slowly, decant immediately. Metal filter → heavy body + sediment.
AeroPress: paper microfilter + gentle pressure → fast, clean, forgiving. Fine-medium grind, short steep, lower temp. Standard vs inverted method.
Immersion suits people who want a reliable cup without fuss; AeroPress is the great travel/experiment brewer.

refs.label: Stillwater — AeroPress vs V60 vs Chemex

brew_cold.name: Cold Brew & Iced Coffee

sections.h: Cold brew vs iced coffee — not the same thing
sections.body: People conflate these, but they're made completely differently. Iced coffee is hot-brewed coffee cooled down and poured over ice — it keeps the acidity and aromatics of a hot brew (and can taste watery if the ice dilutes it, which is why “flash brew” / Japanese iced coffee brews hot directly onto ice to preserve strength). Cold brew never uses heat at all: coffee steeps in cold or room-temperature water for many hours. The absence of heat changes the chemistry — fewer of the acids and aromatic compounds that heat extracts dissolve out — so cold brew is characteristically smooth, low-acid, heavy, and sweet-tasting, with a very different flavor from the same beans brewed hot.

Cold brew is essentially a long immersion. Use a coarse grind and a strong ratio (often 1:8 to 1:5 by weight if you're making a concentrate to dilute later, or around 1:15 for ready-to-drink), combine coffee and cold/room-temp water, and steep 12–24 hours — in the fridge for a cleaner result, at room temperature for a faster, punchier one. Then filter out the grounds (a paper filter or fine mesh; concentrate is usually double-filtered). The result keeps in the fridge for a week or more, which is part of the appeal for cafes. Concentrate is diluted to taste with water or milk over ice.

Cold brew anchors a whole modern category. Nitro cold brew is cold brew infused with nitrogen gas and poured through a pressurized tap, giving a cascading, creamy, Guinness-like texture and a naturally sweet, smooth mouthfeel without any dairy. Ready-to-drink (RTD) canned and bottled cold coffees — a massive and growing market segment, especially with younger drinkers — are largely cold-brew or cold-brew-style products. The low acidity, sweetness, shelf stability, and refreshing serve have made cold coffee one of the defining growth stories of contemporary coffee, and a fixture of the so-called fourth wave.

keypoints: Iced coffee = hot-brewed then cooled/over ice (keeps acidity & aroma; flash-brew onto ice preserves strength). Cold brew = never heated, steeped cold for hours.
No heat → fewer acids/aromatics extracted → cold brew is smooth, low-acid, heavy, sweet, and tastes different from hot brew of the same beans.
Make it: coarse grind, strong ratio (1:8–1:5 for concentrate), steep 12–24h cold/room-temp, filter. Keeps a week+ refrigerated.
Nitro = nitrogen-infused cold brew, creamy cascading texture. RTD canned cold coffee is a huge, growing market segment.

brew_pressure.name: Moka Pot & Siphon

sections.body: The moka pot (stovetop “espresso” maker) is a fixture of Italian homes. It has three chambers: water in the bottom, a funnel basket of ground coffee in the middle, and an empty top chamber. As the bottom water heats, steam pressure forces hot water up through the coffee and into the top. It produces a strong, concentrated, bold brew — not true espresso (the pressure is far lower than a machine's nine bars, so there's no real crema), but far stronger than drip. Use a grind finer than drip but coarser than espresso, take it off the heat as soon as it gurgles and sputters (that's the sign it's done), and don't over-tamp or over-fill. It's cheap, durable, and makes a great café-style base for milk drinks at home.

The siphon (or vacuum pot / vac pot) is coffee theater with real substance, developed over 160 years ago. It uses two chambers and a heat source: heating the lower water chamber forces water up into the upper chamber holding the grounds, where it steeps (immersion); when you remove the heat, the resulting vacuum pulls the brewed coffee back down through a filter into the lower chamber. Because it combines full immersion with a filter and precise, consistent heat, the siphon produces a cup with both a substantial body and striking clarity — clean yet full. It's demanding and delicate glassware, more ritual than daily driver, but prized for the control and the show. Use a medium grind, like fine sand.

Both are older, characterful methods that sit outside the pour-over/immersion mainstream. The moka pot is about strength and everyday practicality — the affordable way to get a concentrated, espresso-adjacent shot at home. The siphon is the opposite: a special-occasion, high-control, sensory-spectacle brew you'll more often see at a dedicated brew bar than on a kitchen counter. Neither is the easiest way to make coffee, but each does something the clean modern methods don't, and both are part of a well-rounded understanding of how coffee can be made.

keypoints: Moka pot: three chambers; steam pressure pushes water up through coffee. Strong & bold but NOT true espresso (low pressure, no real crema). Pull off heat when it gurgles.
Grind for moka: finer than drip, coarser than espresso. Cheap, durable, good home base for milk drinks.
Siphon/vac pot: immersion in the upper chamber, then vacuum pulls it down through a filter. Full body + high clarity. Theatrical, delicate, ritual brewing.
Moka = everyday strength & practicality; siphon = special-occasion control and spectacle.

refs.label: Balance Coffee — Chemex vs V60 vs Moka vs AeroPress

brew_milk.name: Milk & Steaming

sections.body: Great milk drinks depend on microfoam — steamed milk with bubbles so tiny and uniform it looks glossy and pours like wet paint. It's not the stiff, dry froth of a cheap frother; it's a smooth emulsion of air and milk proteins that integrates into the espresso and makes latte art possible. The SCA describes excellent milk foam as smooth, glossy, and moist with no visible bubbles.

Steaming has two stages. First stretch (aeration): the steam tip sits just below the surface so it draws air in — you hear a steady 'tss-tss.' This is done while the milk is still cold, adding roughly 20–30% volume for a latte (more for a cappuccino). Then texture (emulsification): sink the wand deeper and tilt the pitcher to create a whirlpool that shreds big bubbles into microfoam and heats evenly. Stop aerating once the milk warms, or the foam turns stiff and dry.

Aim for about 60–65°C (140–150°F). Milk's lactose tastes sweetest here because heat converts some of it to simpler sugars — properly steamed milk is noticeably sweeter than cold milk. Above ~65°C whey proteins denature and the foam gets thin and unstable; above ~70°C the milk scalds and turns flat and sulphurous. A common no-thermometer check: pull off when the pitcher is almost too hot to hold. And don't re-steam milk — once its proteins are stretched, they won't rebuild.

Plant milks behave differently. Oat is the most barista-friendly — 'barista editions' add stabilizers (like dipotassium phosphate) that stop curdling against acidic espresso and help it foam. Soy froths aggressively and needs a shorter stretch. Almond is trickier for stable foam. In all cases the barista-formulated versions steam far better than the standard cartons, and plant milks generally want a shorter aeration phase and slightly lower temperatures.

keypoints: Target microfoam: glossy, wet-paint texture — an emulsion of air and proteins, not stiff froth.
Two phases: stretch (aerate cold milk, +20–30% for a latte) then texture (whirlpool to shred bubbles).
Sweet spot ~60–65°C; above ~65°C foam destabilizes, above ~70°C it scalds. Don't re-steam.
Barista-edition oat/soy (added stabilizers) steam best; plant milks want shorter aeration.

brew_milkscience.name: Milk Science

sections.h: What’s in milk, and why it matters
sections.body: Steaming milk for coffee is really about manipulating three components: proteins, fats, and sugar (lactose). Proteins (mainly casein and whey) are what trap air and stabilize foam — they wrap around air bubbles and hold them, which is why milk foams and, say, water doesn't. Fat adds richness, mouthfeel, and flavor and influences foam texture and stability. Lactose is milk's natural sugar, and heating makes milk taste sweeter by making that lactose more perceptible — which is why properly steamed milk tastes sweet without any sugar added. Good steaming is the art of using heat and air to turn cold milk into sweet, glossy microfoam.

sections.h: The two phases: stretch then texture
sections.body: Steaming has two distinct phases and getting them in the right order is everything. First, stretching (aeration): with the steam wand tip near the surface, you introduce air, growing the milk's volume — this is where you add foam, and it should happen early while the milk is still cold (below about body temperature), making a gentle hissing 'tsss.' Second, texturing (emulsifying): you submerge the tip slightly and angle the pitcher to create a whirlpool that spins the milk, breaking any large bubbles down into fine, uniform microfoam and folding the foam into the liquid. You stretch only in the early cold phase, then stop adding air and just spin/texture the rest of the way. Add air too late (when hot) and it won't integrate — you get stiff, separated foam instead of glossy microfoam.

The goal is microfoam: milk and foam emulsified into one glossy, paint-like fluid with bubbles too small to see, not a dry meringue floating on thin milk. Microfoam is what makes milk pourable for latte art and gives the silky texture of a good flat white. Temperature matters and there's a ceiling: aim for roughly 55–65°C (about 140–150°F). Below that it's under-sweet; push much past ~70°C and the proteins denature and break down, the milk scalds, loses sweetness, and takes on a cooked flavor, and the foam collapses. Since most people can't hold a 65°C pitcher comfortably, 'too hot to hold for more than a second or two' is a rough tactile cue to stop. Overheated milk can't be fixed — you have to start over.

After steaming, a couple of habits keep the texture right: give the pitcher a few taps on the counter to pop any remaining large bubbles, then swirl continuously to keep the microfoam integrated and glossy until you pour — milk left sitting separates into foam-on-top and liquid-below. And a note on alternative milks, since they're now everywhere: they behave differently because they lack milk's exact protein/fat structure. 'Barista' editions of oat, soy, and almond milk exist specifically because they add proteins, fats, or stabilizers to mimic dairy's foaming and steaming behavior — oat milk in particular has become popular for steaming close to dairy. They can also scorch and split at high heat, and often want a slightly gentler touch, but the same stretch-then-texture principle applies.

keypoints: Steaming manipulates milk's proteins (trap air → foam), fat (richness, texture), and lactose (heating makes it taste sweeter — sweet without added sugar).

Two phases IN ORDER: (1) STRETCH/aerate — tip near surface, add air EARLY while cold, gentle 'tsss'; (2) TEXTURE — submerge tip, spin a whirlpool to break bubbles into microfoam. Air added late (hot) won't integrate = stiff separated foam.

Goal = microfoam: glossy, paint-like, bubbles too small to see (not dry meringue). Target ~55–65°C / 140–150°F; past ~70°C proteins break down, milk scalds, foam collapses. 'Too hot to hold' = stop. Overheated milk can't be saved.

Finish: tap out big bubbles, then swirl continuously until pouring (sitting milk separates). Alt-milks lack dairy's protein/fat structure — 'barista' versions add proteins/stabilizers to mimic it; oat steams closest; same stretch-then-texture rules.

brew_drinks.name: Espresso Milk Drinks
brew_drinks.sub: The ratios that define each drink

sections.h: It’s all espresso + milk, in ratios
sections.body: The whole menu of café milk drinks is really just espresso combined with steamed milk and microfoam in different proportions. Once you know that, the names stop being intimidating: the difference between a cortado, a flat white, a latte, and a cappuccino is mostly how much milk and how much foam sit on top of the same shot. “Steamed milk” is the hot, textured liquid; “microfoam” is the glossy layer of tiny bubbles you build while steaming; the ratio of espresso to milk to foam is the recipe.

From strongest-per-volume to milkiest: a macchiato is a shot “stained” with just a dollop of foam. A cortado is roughly equal parts espresso and steamed milk (about 1:1–1:2), served small, minimal foam. A flat white is a double shot with steamed milk and a thin layer of microfoam, usually ~5–6 oz — stronger and less foamy than a latte. A cappuccino is classically equal thirds espresso, steamed milk, and thick foam (~5–6 oz), the foamiest of the group. A latte is a shot with a lot of steamed milk and only a thin foam cap, the mildest and largest (~8–12+ oz). A mocha is a latte with chocolate. Sizes and exact ratios vary by shop, but the relationships hold.

sections.h: Latte art and why it works
sections.body: Latte art — the hearts, rosettas, and tulips poured on top — isn't just decoration; it's a sign that the milk was steamed correctly. You can only pour art with properly textured microfoam: glossy, paint-like, with no big bubbles, integrated into the milk rather than sitting as dry foam on top. The pour works by floating that white microfoam on the darker crema surface — you start pouring high and to the back to sink the milk under the crema, then drop the pitcher close and let the foam surface to “draw” as you move. If your milk won't make art, the fix is upstream in the steaming (stretch cold, texture to a smooth whirlpool, no big bubbles) — which is covered on the milk-steaming page.

keypoints: Every café milk drink = espresso + steamed milk + microfoam in different ratios. The names are just proportions.
Macchiato: shot + dollop of foam. Cortado: ~1:1–1:2 espresso:milk, small, little foam. Flat white: double shot + thin microfoam, ~5–6oz.
Cappuccino: ~1/3 each espresso/milk/thick foam, foamiest. Latte: shot + lots of milk + thin foam cap, mildest/largest. Mocha: latte + chocolate.
Latte art requires correctly textured glossy microfoam (no big bubbles) — it's a sign the milk was steamed right. Fix bad art at the steaming stage.

brew_latteart.name: Latte Art

sections.h: Art is a symptom of good milk
sections.body: Latte art — the hearts, rosettas, and tulips on top of a drink — is often treated as decoration, but among baristas it's really a diagnostic: you can only pour clean art with correctly textured milk. That means glossy, paint-like microfoam with no visible bubbles, milk and foam fully integrated into one silky fluid rather than a dry foam floating on thin milk. If your milk won't pour a pattern, the problem is almost always upstream in the steaming, not the pour — so latte art and milk steaming are inseparable skills. Good espresso with a stable crema surface is the other half; the pattern is white microfoam displacing dark crema.

sections.h: The two moves: sink, then surface
sections.body: Every pour is a sequence of two things. First you sink the milk: holding the pitcher high and pouring a thin stream into the center, you drive the milk down under the crema without disturbing the surface, filling the cup and building a canvas. Then you surface the foam: as the cup fills, you bring the spout close to the surface and increase the flow, so the white microfoam floats up and stays on top instead of sinking. Once it's surfacing, moving the pitcher — side to side, or forward — 'draws' with that white foam on the dark background. Finish by cutting a thin stream straight through to draw the stem or point.

Most designs build from three. The heart is simplest: pour into the center until a white disc surfaces, then cut straight up through it and the drag pulls it into a heart. The rosetta (leaf): once foam surfaces, wiggle the pitcher steadily side to side while slowly drawing backward, laying down a stack of arcs, then cut forward through them to spine the leaf. The tulip: pour a blob, stop; pour another blob behind it and let it push the first forward; repeat for stacked layers, then cut through. All three depend on the same fundamentals — the milk surfacing at the right moment and a controlled, steady hand. Latte art is mostly milk texture and practice, not talent.

Once the heart, rosetta, and tulip are reliable, the advanced world opens up along a few axes. STACKING is the first: instead of one push of milk, you make many small, deliberate pulses — each one a bump of the pitcher — to build multi-layered tulips (a '6-stack' is six nested pushes) or the winged 'wave-heart.' The key skill is control of flow: starting and stopping the stream cleanly without dribbling, so each layer sits crisp against the last. ROSETTA VARIATIONS come next — wider leaves, more of them, or the 'inverted' rosetta poured from the far side toward you. Then there's ETCHING and free-pour hybrids: dragging a fine tool (or a thin stream) through the surface to add antennae to a swan, whiskers to a cat, or fine detail no pour alone can make. The true advanced tier is SLOW POURING and the '3D' foam sculptures — but those rely entirely on the foundations: none of it works without milk textured to wet-paint microfoam and a shot with good crema to paint on. Advanced latte art isn't new tricks so much as extreme control of the same two moves — sink and surface — repeated with precision.

When art goes wrong, the cause is usually diagnosable. No white surfaces / everything sinks: you poured too high or too slowly for too long, or the foam is too thin — get closer and increase flow, and check your steaming built enough microfoam. Big holes and blotches instead of crisp lines: bubbles in the milk — fix the steaming (stretch less air, texture longer to a smooth whirlpool, tap and swirl the pitcher before pouring). Pattern won't move or 'draw': the flow is too gentle once you're at the surface, or the crema broke up — pour with more confidence and make sure the shot has a good crema. Above all, the fix for bad latte art is usually better milk, which loops back to the milk-steaming fundamentals.

keypoints: Latte art requires correctly textured glossy microfoam (no bubbles, milk+foam integrated) — it's a sign the milk was steamed right. Bad art = fix the steaming, not the pour.
Two moves: SINK the milk (pitcher high, thin center stream, under the crema) then SURFACE the foam (spout close, more flow) so white floats and 'draws' on dark crema.
Three foundations: heart (pour + cut through), rosetta (wiggle side-to-side drawing back, then cut forward), tulip (stacked blobs pushed forward, then cut).
Failures: nothing surfaces = poured too high/thin foam; holes/blotches = bubbles (fix steaming); won't draw = too-gentle flow or broken crema. The fix is usually better milk.

Advanced = extreme control of the SAME two moves (sink + surface), not new tricks. STACKING = many small pitcher pulses → multi-layer tulips (e.g. 6-stack), wave-hearts (needs clean flow start/stop). ROSETTA variations (wider/more leaves, inverted). ETCHING/hybrids = drag a tool or thin stream for detail (swan antennae, cat whiskers). All of it still needs wet-paint microfoam + good crema.

refs.label: Barista Life — latte art & dual boilers

brew_water.name: Water Chemistry

sections.body: A cup of coffee is about 98% water, so water isn't a neutral background — it's the solvent doing the extracting, and its mineral content directly changes what and how much it pulls from the grounds. Two identical coffees brewed with different water taste genuinely different. This is why serious cafés treat water as an ingredient to be specified, not whatever comes from the tap.

Coffee water is characterized mainly by two things. Total hardness — the dissolved minerals (mostly magnesium and calcium) that actively grab and extract flavor compounds; too little and coffee tastes flat and empty, too much and it can taste harsh or chalky. And alkalinity (buffering capacity, largely bicarbonate) — which neutralizes acidity; too high and it flattens the bright, pleasant acids that make specialty coffee sing, too low and the cup can taste sharp. The SCA also points to a near-neutral pH (roughly 6.5–7.5) as a target range.

You don't need a chemistry lab, but you should know your water. Very hard tap water over-buffers acidity and scales your machine; distilled or zero-mineral water extracts poorly and tastes hollow. Many cafés filter, blend, or build water to a target. There's a practical safety angle too: hardness minerals are what form limescale inside espresso machines and boilers, so water treatment protects both flavor and equipment.

You don't need a lab, but you do need to know roughly what your water is. Very hard, high-alkalinity tap water buffers away the bright acidity that makes specialty coffee interesting and scales up your machine; very soft or distilled water extracts poorly and tastes hollow and flat. Common fixes: filtering to reduce hardness and off-flavors, blending tap with distilled to hit a target, or building water from distilled using mineral packs. A rough specialty target is moderate hardness with lower alkalinity and a near-neutral pH. Whatever you do, keep it consistent — changing your water changes every coffee you brew.

keypoints: Coffee is ~98% water; the water is the solvent and shapes extraction directly.
Two key numbers: hardness (minerals that extract flavor) and alkalinity (buffer that neutralizes acidity).
Too-hard/high-alkalinity water flattens acidity; distilled/zero-mineral extracts hollow. SCA target pH ~6.5–7.5.
Know and treat your water — it protects both flavor and machines (limescale).

brew_waterchem.name: Water Chemistry

sections.body: A cup of brewed coffee is roughly 98–99% water, and espresso is around 90% water — which makes water by far the largest ingredient, and one of the most overlooked. Water isn't a neutral solvent; its mineral content actively shapes both how much flavor gets extracted from the coffee and how that flavor tastes in the cup. The same beans, same grinder, same technique will produce noticeably different coffee on different water, which is why serious cafes treat their water as carefully as their beans. And it's also the single biggest factor in whether your espresso machine survives — water chemistry is simultaneously a flavor issue and an equipment-longevity issue.

sections.h: GH and KH: the two hardnesses
sections.body: The key to coffee water is understanding two different 'hardnesses,' because they do different jobs. General Hardness (GH) is the concentration of dissolved minerals — mainly calcium and magnesium. These minerals are what actually extract and bind flavor compounds from the coffee: magnesium in particular latches onto fruity, acidic flavor molecules, so water with too little GH tastes flat and hollow (it can't pull the flavor out), while too much can taste heavy or chalky and over-extract. Carbonate Hardness (KH, also called alkalinity or temporary hardness) is specifically the bicarbonate content, and it acts as a buffer — it neutralizes acidity. This matters enormously: too much KH buffers away the bright, pleasant acids and makes coffee taste dull and flat, while too little leaves nothing to tame the acidity and coffee can taste sharp and sour. GH gives you extraction and body; KH controls how much of the coffee's acidity comes through.

Because water matters so much, the Specialty Coffee Association publishes a water standard — a target recipe for ideal brewing water. The headline numbers: a total dissolved solids (TDS) target around 150 mg/L (with an acceptable range roughly 75–250), calcium hardness around 50–75 mg/L (as calcium carbonate), alkalinity (KH) around 40 mg/L, a neutral pH near 7, and essentially no chlorine (which tastes and smells bad and damages machines). The guiding idea is balance: enough calcium and magnesium to extract flavor and give body, enough (but not too much) bicarbonate to buffer acidity into pleasantness, and clean, odor-free, roughly neutral water. Many specialty pros go further and mix their own water from distilled or reverse-osmosis water plus mineral concentrates (recipes like the well-known 'rao/perger' or third-wave water approaches), which gives total control and consistency.

sections.h: Flavor, scale, and the practical path
sections.body: There's a genuine tension between the two goals. For flavor, you want meaningful GH and moderate KH. But for your machine, KH is the villain: the bicarbonate in hard water is exactly what precipitates out as limescale when heated, coating boilers and heating elements and eventually destroying espresso machines — so equipment longevity pushes you toward softer, low-KH water. This is why filtered or softened water is the standard recommendation: it protects the machine, and (done right) improves the cup. The practical path for most people: avoid distilled water alone (too little mineral — flat coffee and it can even be corrosive) and avoid hard tap water (scale and often chlorine); instead use good filtered water in the right hardness range, or for the obsessive, mix to a recipe. Test your water hardness (cheap GH/KH test kits or strips exist), because it varies enormously by location — and once you dial in good water, it's one of those upgrades that quietly improves every single cup.

keypoints: Brewed coffee is ~98–99% water (espresso ~90%) — the biggest, most overlooked ingredient. Water's minerals actively shape BOTH extraction and taste; same beans on different water = different coffee. Also the #1 factor in machine survival.

Two hardnesses: GH (General Hardness = calcium + magnesium) = what EXTRACTS & binds flavor (esp. magnesium → fruity acids); too little = flat/hollow. KH (Carbonate Hardness/alkalinity = bicarbonate) = a BUFFER that neutralizes acidity; too much = dull/flat, too little = sharp/sour.

SCA water standard (target): TDS ~150 mg/L (range ~75–250), calcium ~50–75 mg/L, alkalinity ~40 mg/L, pH ~7, ~zero chlorine. Idea = balance: enough minerals to extract + buffer acidity into pleasantness, clean & neutral. Pros mix their own from RO/distilled + concentrates.

Flavor vs machine tension: flavor wants GH + moderate KH, but KH's bicarbonate is exactly what forms SCALE when heated (kills machines) → longevity wants soft/low-KH. Use good FILTERED water in range (not distilled-alone = flat/corrosive, not hard tap = scale/chlorine). Test hardness (varies by location); good water quietly improves every cup.

brew_waterrecipe.name: Water Recipes

sections.h: Why water is 98% of the cup — and worth engineering
sections.body: A brewed coffee is around 98–99% water, so the water isn't a neutral background — it's most of what you're drinking, and it's an active solvent that decides which compounds get extracted. Two aspects matter most: hardness (dissolved minerals, mainly magnesium and calcium, which grab onto and extract flavor compounds) and alkalinity (buffering capacity, mostly bicarbonate, which neutralizes acids). Get these wrong and even perfect beans and technique can't produce a good cup; get them right and everything improves at once. For serious brewers, controlling water is one of the highest-leverage and most overlooked upgrades.

The widely-cited reference is the SCA's water standard, which centers around roughly 150 mg/L total dissolved solids (a rough band of ~75–250 acceptable), with calcium hardness and alkalinity ideally near ~40–75 mg/L (as CaCO3), a neutral pH around 7, and no chlorine or off-odors. The logic in plain terms: you want enough hardness to extract flavor and body, but low enough alkalinity that the coffee's bright acidity comes through instead of being buffered flat. Too hard and high-alkalinity (typical hard tap water) mutes acidity and scales your machine; too soft or distilled and the cup is hollow, flat, and under-extracted.

You don't need a lab, just a strategy. Filter your tap: a good carbon filter removes chlorine and off-flavors and some hardness — the easy first step, though it doesn't fix high alkalinity. Blend: mix hard tap water with distilled or RO water to dilute minerals toward the target — cheap and effective if you know roughly how hard your tap is. Build from zero: start with distilled or RO water (a blank slate) and add minerals back with a measured recipe or commercial mineral packs — the most precise and repeatable approach, favored by competitors. Espresso adds a wrinkle: lower hardness/alkalinity protects the machine from scale, which is why plumbed setups often use softened or built water.

The build-from-distilled method is worth understanding because it's how you get truly repeatable water. The idea: distilled water has essentially no minerals, so you dose in exactly what you want. Hobbyist recipes (the widely-shared 'Rao/Perger' style and others) add small measured amounts of two concentrates to distilled water — one supplying magnesium/calcium (hardness, for extraction) and one supplying bicarbonate (buffer, for balance) — to land near the target numbers. You can tweak the ratio to taste: a touch more hardness for body and extraction, a touch more buffer if your coffee tastes too sharp. Commercial mineral sachets do the same thing in premeasured form. Whatever route you choose, the golden rule is consistency — changing your water changes every coffee you brew, so lock in a recipe and judge your beans against a fixed background.

keypoints: Brewed coffee is ~98–99% water; water is an active solvent. Two key traits: hardness (Mg/Ca — extracts flavor) and alkalinity (bicarbonate buffer — neutralizes acidity).
Target (SCA-ish): ~150 mg/L TDS (band ~75–250), hardness & alkalinity ~40–75 mg/L as CaCO3, pH ~7, no chlorine. Enough hardness to extract, low enough alkalinity to keep acidity.
Too hard/high-alkalinity (hard tap) = flat, muted, scaly; too soft/distilled = hollow, under-extracted.

Three routes: filter tap (easy, doesn't fix alkalinity); blend tap + distilled/RO; build from distilled with a mineral recipe/packs (most precise). Espresso wants low hardness to avoid scale. Consistency is the golden rule.

brew_troubleshoot.name: Brew Troubleshooting

sections.h: The two questions behind every bad cup
sections.body: Almost every brewing problem comes down to two independent things: extraction (how much coffee dissolved) and strength (how concentrated it is). Extraction is mostly controlled by grind size, water temperature, and contact time; strength is controlled by the coffee-to-water ratio. The trick to fixing coffee is diagnosing which one is off. A cup can be under-extracted but strong, or over-extracted but weak — they're separate dials. Once you can tell whether a cup is sour (under-extracted), bitter (over-extracted), or just weak/strong, you know which knob to turn.

sections.h: Tastes sour, sharp, or thin → under-extracted
sections.body: Sourness, sharp acidity, a thin or empty finish, and a quick fade mean you didn't pull enough out of the grounds. Fix it by extracting more: grind finer (more surface area), use hotter water (closer to 96°C), extend the contact time (slower pour, longer steep), or pour more evenly so all the grounds contribute. On espresso, a sour, fast-running shot means grind finer and/or dose a touch more. Under-extraction is the most common home-brew fault, usually from too coarse a grind or too short a brew.

sections.h: Tastes bitter, harsh, or dry → over-extracted
sections.body: Harsh bitterness, an astringent drying feeling, and a hollow, ashy finish mean you pulled too much out. Do the opposite: grind coarser, use slightly cooler water, shorten the contact time, and don't agitate excessively. On espresso, a bitter, slow-dripping shot means grind coarser and/or dose a little less. Over-extraction often comes from grinding too fine, water too hot, or letting immersion coffee sit on the grounds too long (decant your French press promptly).

sections.h: Tastes weak/watery or too intense → ratio
sections.body: If the cup is cleanly flavored but just watery, or conversely overpowering, that's strength, not extraction — adjust the ratio. Weak means use more coffee (or less water); too strong means the reverse. A common mistake is trying to fix a weak cup by over-extracting (grinding finer and brewing longer), which just adds bitterness on top of the wateriness. Fix strength with the ratio and extraction with grind/temp/time, and keep the two adjustments separate. And always start from fresh, properly stored beans ground just before brewing — stale coffee and stale grind produce flat cups that no dial can rescue.

keypoints: Two independent dials: extraction (grind/temp/time) and strength (ratio). Diagnose which is off.
Sour / sharp / thin = under-extracted → grind finer, hotter water, longer contact, even pour.
Bitter / harsh / drying = over-extracted → grind coarser, cooler water, shorter contact, less agitation.
Weak/watery or too intense = strength → change the ratio (more/less coffee). Don't fix strength with extraction. Start from fresh beans + fresh grind.

plant.name: The Coffee Plant

sections.body: The coffee you roast and brew is the seed of a fruit. Coffee grows on evergreen shrubs or small trees in the genus Coffea, which in the wild reach 3–5 metres (Robusta 10–12) but are pruned to about 2–3 metres for harvesting. The trees produce fragrant white flowers, and after flowering, green fruits that ripen through yellow to a bright red (or yellow) “cherry.” Inside each cherry are usually two flat-faced seeds — the coffee beans. Occasionally a cherry produces a single round seed instead of two: a peaberry, prized by some for a more intense cup. Everything downstream, from processing to roasting, is about turning that seed into something delicious.

From the outside in, a coffee cherry is layered like any stone fruit: the outer skin (exocarp); the sweet fruit pulp (mesocarp); a sticky, sugary layer of mucilage clinging to the seed; a papery parchment layer (the endocarp) that encases the seed; a thin silverskin (the seed's own coat); and finally the seed itself — the green bean. Processing is essentially the job of removing these fruit layers and drying the seed, and how much of the mucilage and pulp is left on during drying is exactly what separates washed, honey, and natural processing. The silverskin largely flakes off during roasting as chaff.

Quality coffee is fussy about where it lives. Arabica thrives at high altitude (roughly 900–2,000 m), in steady rainfall, often under shade, at mild temperatures around 15–24°C — conditions found in the tropical “bean belt” between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn. Altitude matters because cooler high-elevation air slows the cherry's ripening, letting the seed develop more density and complex sugars, which is why high-grown coffee is prized. The tree is slow: it takes roughly three to four years from planting before a coffee tree yields its first real harvest, and a cherry itself takes several months (about 7–9 for Arabica) to ripen after flowering.

There are over a hundred Coffea species, but three carry the trade. Arabica (Coffea arabica) is the specialty workhorse — delicate, complex, lower in caffeine (~1.2–1.5%), high-grown, and susceptible to disease; it's roughly 60–70% of world production. Robusta (Coffea canephora) grows hardier at lower altitude, resists pests (its higher caffeine, ~2.2–2.7%, acts as a natural pesticide), yields more, and tastes harsher and more bitter; it dominates commodity coffee and instant, and adds body and crema to some espresso blends. Liberica (and its relative Excelsa) is a distant third — large, irregular beans with a polarizing woody, jackfruit-like flavor, grown in pockets of Asia and West Africa.

keypoints: Coffee is the seed of a fruit (“cherry”); usually two flat seeds per cherry, sometimes one round peaberry.
Cherry layers: skin → pulp → mucilage → parchment → silverskin → seed. Processing removes the fruit; how much mucilage stays on defines washed/honey/natural.
Arabica wants altitude (900–2000m), shade, mild temps, ~7–9 months to ripen, ~3–4 years to first harvest. Altitude slows ripening → denser, more complex beans.
Three trade species: Arabica (~60–70%, complex, lower caffeine), Robusta (hardy, bitter, high caffeine, commodity), Liberica (rare, woody).

refs.label: Farm & Forest — anatomy of a coffee cherry

varietals.name: Varietals & Cultivars
varietals.sub: Why the variety on the bag matters

sections.body: First, a distinction people mix up: species (Arabica vs Robusta) sits above variety. Within Arabica there are dozens of named types — Bourbon, Typica, Caturra, SL28, Geisha, and so on. These are varieties or, more precisely, cultivars (cultivated varieties selected and propagated by humans for desirable traits). The word “varietal” is borrowed from wine, where it technically means the wine made from a grape; coffee uses varietal and variety interchangeably. What a variety describes is the plant's genetics — not the roast level and not the flavor notes, though genetics set the ceiling for what flavors are possible.

sections.h: The two founding lines: Typica & Bourbon
sections.body: Most traditional Arabica varieties trace back through Typica and Bourbon. Coffee spread from Ethiopia to Yemen, and from there two great dispersals occurred: seeds sent to Asia and the Americas became known as Typica (clean, sweet, excellent quality but low-yielding — the basis of Kona and Jamaica Blue Mountain), while seeds cultivated on the island of Bourbon (Réunion) became Bourbon (more productive than Typica, complex and sweet). Nearly the whole family tree of classic Latin American coffee branches from these two — which is why they're called the founding varieties.

From Bourbon and Typica came a cascade of natural mutations and deliberate crosses. Caturra is a compact, higher-yielding natural mutation of Bourbon found in Brazil — bright but with slightly less clarity than its parent. Maragogype is a giant-beaned Typica mutation. Mundo Novo is a natural Bourbon × Typica cross; Catuai is Caturra × Mundo Novo; Pacamara is Pacas (a Bourbon mutation) × Maragogype, a Cup-of-Excellence darling with huge beans and complex flavor. In Kenya, Scott Laboratories selected SL28 and SL34 in the 1930s — drought-tolerant, wine-like, blackcurrant-forward, and the backbone of Kenyan quality (though rust-susceptible).

No variety made the case for genetics like Geisha (often spelled Gesha). Originally collected in Ethiopia's Gesha region and long ignored, it exploded onto the scene at the 2004 Best of Panama competition with an intensely floral, jasmine-and-bergamot, tea-like cup unlike anything judges had tasted — and it now commands auction prices in the hundreds or thousands of dollars per pound. Before Geisha, many believed origin and terroir alone determined flavor; Geisha proved that the plant's genetics can dominate. Certain varieties — Geisha, SL28, Pacamara, Bourbon — simply have a genetic capacity for complexity that others lack.

The other half of the varietal story is survival. When coffee leaf rust devastates crops, breeders reach for resistance — and the key was the Timor Hybrid, a natural Arabica×Robusta cross found on Timor around 1920 that carries Robusta's disease resistance. Crossing it with compact Arabicas produced the Catimor (Caturra × Timor) and Sarchimor (Villa Sarchi × Timor) families: hardy, high-yielding, rust-resistant, but often lower in cup quality. Kenya's Ruiru 11 and Batian, Colombia's Castillo, and modern F1 hybrids (first-generation crosses with hybrid vigor — high yield and resistance) are all part of this ongoing effort to balance flavor, yield, and resilience as the climate changes.

sections.h: How much does variety actually matter?
