sections.body: Here's the honest nuance: variety sets the genetic ceiling, but terroir, altitude, and processing often matter more for the cup in front of you. A Geisha grown carelessly at low altitude will disappoint; a well-processed Caturra from a great high-altitude farm will beat a neglected Bourbon every time. The same SL28 tastes radically different grown in Kenya versus Guatemala. So when a bag reads “Caturra, washed, 1,800m, Huila,” the last three descriptors often tell you more than the variety alone. Variety is a powerful predictor — but it's one factor among several, not destiny.

keypoints: Species (Arabica/Robusta) > variety/cultivar (Bourbon, Geisha, SL28…). Variety = plant genetics, which set the flavor ceiling.
Typica & Bourbon are the founding Arabica lines; most classic varieties (Caturra, Mundo Novo, Catuai, Pacamara) branch from them.
Geisha proved genetics can dominate flavor — floral/tea-like, exploded at 2004 Best of Panama, commands huge prices. SL28/Pacamara/Bourbon also have high genetic ceilings.
Rust-resistance hybrids (Catimor, Sarchimor, Castillo, Ruiru 11, Batian, F1s) trace to the Arabica×Robusta Timor Hybrid; hardy/high-yield but often lower quality.
Variety sets the ceiling, but terroir + altitude + processing often matter more for the actual cup.

variety_tree.name: The Variety Family Tree

sections.body: Nearly all of the world's classic Arabica varieties descend from just two closely-related founders: Typica and Bourbon. Both trace back to the small pool of coffee the Dutch took from Yemen. Typica is the older lineage — the plant that spread from Yemen to Asia (Java) and then to the Americas, giving us prized coffees like Kona and Jamaica Blue Mountain; it's tall, low-yielding, and celebrated for clean sweetness. Bourbon arose when coffee was taken to the French island of Bourbon (now Réunion) in the Indian Ocean, where it mutated into a distinct variety — also tall, a bit higher-yielding than Typica, and prized for sweetness and complexity. Almost everything else is a mutation of one of these, a cross between them, or a cross with a completely different species. Understanding the family tree turns the confusing variety names on coffee bags into a story you can follow.

Bourbon proved wonderfully prone to useful natural mutations, especially dwarf ones that make trees shorter (easier to pick and denser to plant). The most important is Caturra, a single-gene dwarf mutation of Bourbon discovered in Brazil around 1937 — the first natural mutation ever isolated, and a workhorse across Latin America. Remarkably, the same short-stature mutation appeared independently elsewhere: Pacas is the Bourbon dwarf found in El Salvador (1949), Villa Sarchi the one found in Costa Rica, and Pache the equivalent in Guatemala (technically from Typica). These dwarf Bourbons matter enormously not just on their own but as parents of later varieties. Yellow Bourbon (a yellow-cherried version) and selections like Tekisic (an improved Salvadoran Bourbon) round out the family.

sections.h: The Brazilian crosses and the SLs
sections.body: Brazil, the giant producer, gave us the great natural crosses. Mundo Novo is a natural Bourbon × Typica cross found in Brazil in the 1940s — tall, vigorous, productive. Cross that tall Mundo Novo with the dwarf Caturra and you get Catuaí (released 1968) — compact, highly productive, with red or yellow cherry versions, still a huge share of Brazilian and Central American coffee. Elsewhere, two other famous lineages branch off: the SL varieties (SL28 and SL34), selected by Scott Laboratories in 1930s Kenya, are the backbone of Kenyan quality — both give extraordinary cups, though genetic testing suggests SL28 is Bourbon-related while SL34 is actually closer to Typica. And Geisha (Gesha), the variety that made Panama famous, isn't from this Typica/Bourbon domestication at all — it's a distinct Ethiopian landrace, which is why its floral, tea-like character is so unlike everything else.

sections.h: The Timor Hybrid and disease resistance
sections.body: The other half of the family tree exists to solve one problem: coffee leaf rust. The key is the Timor Hybrid (Hibrido de Timor) — a spontaneous natural cross between Arabica and Robusta discovered on the island of Timor, which carries Robusta's disease resistance in an Arabica-like plant. Breeders crossed it with the dwarf Bourbons to create two great families of rust-resistant varieties: Catimor (Timor Hybrid × Caturra) and Sarchimor (Timor Hybrid × Villa Sarchi). Crucially, 'Catimor' and 'Sarchimor' aren't single varieties but whole families of many cultivars with similar parentage — saying 'this is a Catimor' is like saying 'this is a European.' From these come the modern disease-resistant workhorses: Colombia's Castillo (Caturra × Timor Hybrid, from Cenicafé), the Variedad Colombia, Kenya's Ruiru 11 and Batian, Costa Rica's various Sarchimor selections, and many more. Historically these prioritized survival over flavor, but newer generations — and F1 hybrids like Centroamericano and Starmaya — increasingly aim to combine disease resistance with genuine cup quality.

keypoints: Nearly all classic Arabica descends from two related founders: TYPICA (Yemen→Asia→Americas; tall, clean/sweet; Kona, Jamaica Blue Mountain) and BOURBON (Yemen→Île Bourbon/Réunion mutation; tall, sweet/complex). Everything else = a mutation, cross, or cross with another species.

Bourbon DWARF mutations (shorter = easier pick, denser plant): Caturra (Brazil ~1937, first isolated mutation), Pacas (El Salvador), Villa Sarchi (Costa Rica), Pache (Guatemala, from Typica). Key as parents of later varieties.

Brazilian crosses: Mundo Novo (natural Bourbon×Typica, 1940s) → crossed with Caturra = Catuaí (1968, red/yellow, hugely productive). Kenya's SL28 (Bourbon-related) & SL34 (Typica-related) = elite cups. Geisha = a distinct ETHIOPIAN landrace (not this domestication) → its unique floral character.

Disease resistance via the TIMOR HYBRID (natural Arabica×Robusta from Timor → Robusta's rust resistance): crossed with dwarf Bourbons → Catimor (×Caturra) & Sarchimor (×Villa Sarchi) — both FAMILIES not single varieties. Modern workhorses: Castillo/Colombia (Cenicafé), Ruiru 11/Batian (Kenya). Newer F1 hybrids (Centroamericano, Starmaya) chase resistance + quality.

refs.label: SCA / WCR — coffee plants of the world
Daily Coffee News — varieties & cultivars guide

species_deep.name: Arabica, Robusta & Beyond

sections.body: There are over 100 species in the Coffea genus, but two of them account for essentially all the coffee traded and drunk in the world: Coffea arabica (Arabica) and Coffea canephora (Robusta). They're genuinely different plants with different chemistry, and the split runs through everything in coffee. Arabica makes up roughly 60–70% of world production and dominates specialty; Robusta is most of the rest, the backbone of instant coffee, espresso blends, and commercial coffee. Knowing how the two differ explains an enormous amount — why some coffee is bright and complex and other coffee is harsh and strong, why one costs more, and why the industry is watching the balance between them shift.

Arabica is the quality species. It originated in Ethiopia's cool highlands and needs those conditions — high altitude, mild temperatures (~18–22°C), reliable rain — to thrive, which limits where it grows. Its cup is why specialty coffee exists: more complex, aromatic, and sweeter, with the bright acidity, floral and fruit notes, and nuance that this whole app is about. Genetically, Arabica is unusual — it's a natural hybrid of two other species (Coffea eugenioides × Coffea canephora) that occurred long ago, and it's self-pollinating, which makes it genetically narrow and therefore vulnerable to disease and climate stress. It also has about half the caffeine of Robusta (~1.2–1.5%). Arabica is the delicious, delicate, high-maintenance species — wonderful in the cup and precarious in the field.

Robusta is the survivor species. Native to central and western Africa, it grows at lower altitudes, tolerates heat and humidity, resists disease (including leaf rust) far better than Arabica, and yields more — all of which make it cheaper and easier to grow. Its cup is the tradeoff: traditionally harsher, more bitter, woody or rubbery, with less acidity and complexity, though well-grown specialty Robusta can be much better than its reputation. It has roughly double Arabica's caffeine (~2.2–2.7%), which contributes to its bitterness and its use in high-caffeine and instant products, and it produces a thick, persistent crema prized in some espresso blends (especially Italian ones). Robusta's disease resistance is also why it's the source, via the Timor Hybrid, of the rust-resistance bred into modern Arabica varieties — and why, as the climate warms, it's a growing part of the picture.

Beyond the big two, coffee's wider genetic family is drawing new attention — partly out of curiosity, partly as an insurance policy against climate change. Coffea liberica is a third species, grown in pockets of Southeast Asia and West Africa (the Philippines call their variety 'Barako'); it has huge beans, a distinctive smoky, jackfruit-like flavor, and heat tolerance. Most exciting is Coffea stenophylla, a West African species long thought nearly extinct and recently rediscovered — remarkably, tasting panels found it can rival Arabica in cup quality while tolerating far higher temperatures, making it a serious candidate for a warming world. Excelsa (often classed within Liberica) is another. These 'forgotten' species probably won't replace Arabica and Robusta soon, but in an industry facing climate pressure on its two workhorses, coffee's overlooked genetic diversity has suddenly become one of its most valuable assets.

keypoints: 100+ Coffea species, but two run the world: ARABICA (Coffea arabica, ~60–70% of production, dominates specialty) and ROBUSTA (Coffea canephora, most of the rest — instant, blends, commercial).

Arabica = quality & fragility: Ethiopian highland origin, needs altitude/mild temp/rain (limited range); complex/aromatic/sweet/bright. A natural hybrid (eugenioides × canephora), self-pollinating → genetically narrow → disease/climate vulnerable. ~1.2–1.5% caffeine.

Robusta = hardiness & strength: African origin, low-altitude, heat/humidity/disease tolerant, higher yield → cheaper. Harsher/more bitter/woody, less complex (but good specialty Robusta exists). ~2x caffeine (~2.2–2.7%), thick crema. Source of rust resistance via the Timor Hybrid; growing with climate change.

Forgotten species (climate insurance + curiosity): Liberica (huge beans, smoky/jackfruit, heat-tolerant; Philippine 'Barako'), STENOPHYLLA (West African, near-extinct then rediscovered — rivals Arabica quality but far more heat-tolerant), Excelsa. Coffee's overlooked genetic diversity is now a key asset.

refs.label: SCA / WCR — coffee plants of the world

spot_typica.name: Spotlight: Typica

sections.h: The one that started it all
sections.body: If Bourbon is the sweet founder, Typica is THE founder — the original cultivated Arabica, the variety at the root of nearly the entire family tree. Trace almost any Arabica variety back far enough (Bourbon, Caturra, Geisha, SL28, Mundo Novo, Maragogipe) and you eventually reach Typica. Like all Arabica it began in the coffee forests of southwestern Ethiopia, but the 'Typica' we know is defined by one specific, remarkable journey out of Yemen and around the world. It's a tall, elegant plant — up to about 5 metres, with a thin trunk, widely spaced drooping branches, and distinctive bronze-tipped leaves — and while it's been largely displaced by higher-yielding varieties, it remains a benchmark for pure cup quality.

Typica's spread is one of the great stories in coffee, a chain of single plants and small seed lots that seeded whole continents. Arabica went from Ethiopia to Yemen in the 15th–16th century; by 1700 seeds from Yemen were being grown in India (the Baba Budan legend), and from India's Malabar coast a few seeds reached the Dutch colony of Java around 1696–99 — those seeds are what became the distinct Typica variety. In 1706 a single Typica plant was carried from Java to the botanical gardens in Amsterdam; from there a plant went to France in 1714, and Dutch and French colonial routes then pushed Typica to the Caribbean and the Americas in the early 1700s. It reached the Caribbean, Mexico, and Colombia by the late 1700s, and until the 1940s the great majority of coffee in Central and South America was Typica. An astonishing amount of the world's coffee descends from a literal handful of plants.

Typica is really a whole group, and its regional subtypes include some of the most storied names in coffee. Jamaica Blue Mountain — grown high in Jamaica's Blue Mountains, washed, famously shipped in barrels, a registered origin that commands some of the highest prices in the world — is Typica. So is Hawaiian Kona, prized for its smooth balance. Maragogipe, the giant 'elephant bean,' is a natural Typica mutation. Other names for Typica and its local selections include Criollo ('creole') in Spanish-speaking countries, Arábigo, Pluma Hidalgo in Mexico, and the Sumatra typicas. It's still widely grown in Peru, the Dominican Republic, Ecuador, and Jamaica, where quality-focused producers keep it alive despite the agronomic difficulty.

Typica's cup is its whole argument: clean, elegant, sweet, and refined, with excellent clarity, a silky medium body, and a gentle acidity that can range from bright citrus at high altitude to softer, malic notes lower down — often with chocolate, nut, caramel, and delicate floral or dried-fruit tones. It's the coffee that shows off place and process transparently rather than shouting. But that quality comes with a hard tradeoff: Typica yields roughly 20–30% less than Bourbon, grows tall and awkward to manage, and is highly susceptible to leaf rust, coffee berry disease, and nematodes. That's exactly why it was replaced across most of the Americas after the 1940s — and why growing it today is a deliberate quality-over-productivity choice, viable mainly for producers who can reach specialty and microlot markets. Typica is the reminder that the coffee family began not with the most productive plant, but with the one that simply tasted wonderful.

keypoints: Typica = THE original cultivated Arabica, the ROOT of nearly the whole family tree (Bourbon, Caturra, Geisha, SL28, Mundo Novo, Maragogipe all trace back to it). Tall (~5m), thin trunk, drooping branches, bronze-tipped leaves. Largely displaced by higher-yielders but a benchmark for pure cup quality. (With Bourbon = the two founders.)

The great JOURNEY (chain of single plants): Ethiopia → Yemen (15–16th c.) → India ~1700 (Baba Budan) → Java ~1696–99 (these seeds BECAME Typica) → one plant to Amsterdam 1706 → France 1714 → Caribbean & Americas early 1700s. Until the 1940s MOST Central/South American coffee was Typica. A huge share of world coffee descends from a handful of plants.

Famous descendants/subtypes: Jamaica BLUE MOUNTAIN (registered, barrel-shipped, among the priciest), Hawaiian KONA, Maragogipe ('elephant bean' — a Typica mutation). Other names: Criollo, Arábigo, Pluma Hidalgo, Sumatra. Still grown in Peru, Dominican Republic, Ecuador, Jamaica.

The BARGAIN: cup is clean/elegant/sweet/refined — great clarity, silky medium body, gentle acidity (bright citrus high, softer malic low), choc/nut/caramel/floral. Transparent to place & process. BUT ~20–30% lower yield than Bourbon, tall & hard to manage, very susceptible to rust/CBD/nematodes → replaced post-1940s; growing it now is a deliberate quality-over-yield choice for specialty/microlot markets.

refs.label: Perfect Daily Grind — the Typica variety

geisha_spotlight.name: Spotlight: Geisha

sections.body: No single coffee variety has a story like Geisha (also spelled Gesha — both are accepted, the split reflecting the region's Amharic name). It begins in the Gori Gesha forest of southwestern Ethiopia, where the variety was identified in the 1930s and seeds were collected by a British consul around 1936. From there it traveled the long research route: to a coffee station in Tanzania, then in the 1950s to CATIE, the tropical agricultural research center in Costa Rica, where it was logged as accession T2722. CATIE distributed it around Central America in the 1960s — including to Panama, where growers wanted it purely for one practical reason: it showed good resistance to coffee leaf rust. For decades, that's all Geisha was — a finicky, tall, low-yielding disease-resistant plant that farmers mostly ignored or used as a windbreak. There was no specialty coffee then to reward its quality, so it languished as a genetic curiosity.

Everything changed at the 2004 Best of Panama competition. The Peterson family of Hacienda La Esmeralda in Boquete had noticed, during a wet and disease-prone season, that one high-altitude section of their plants stayed remarkably healthy — they isolated it, found it was Geisha, and entered a separate lot in the competition. What the judges tasted stunned the room: an explosively floral, jasmine-and-bergamot, tea-like cup with tropical fruit and a clarity nobody associated with coffee. It won with an unprecedented score (around 94 points) and sold for about $21 per pound — nearly ten times the going rate for high-quality coffee at the time. Overnight, Geisha became the talk of the coffee world, and the Best of Panama had to reckon with a flavor category that didn't previously exist. It was the perfect specialty story: a forgotten Ethiopian lineage, rediscovered on one farm, revealed through a dramatic competition win.

Geisha's signature — jasmine, orange blossom, bergamot (think Earl Grey), peach and tropical fruit, a clean tea-like body — comes from an unusually high concentration of aromatic compounds, especially linalool and geraniol, the same floral molecules found in flowers and citrus. But the variety only expresses this magic under specific conditions: grown at high altitude (the best Panamanian lots sit at 1,700–2,100 m, where cool nights and misty mornings slow the cherries' development), hand-picked at peak ripeness, and processed with obsessive care. This is why the same genetic Geisha can be sublime in the Panamanian highlands and unremarkable when grown low or handled carelessly — the variety sets a very high ceiling, but terroir and processing decide whether you reach it. Geisha now grows in Colombia, Costa Rica, Guatemala, Hawaii, and increasingly back in Ethiopia, each region giving a slightly different accent on the same floral theme.

sections.h: The most expensive coffee in the world
sections.body: Geisha didn't just win a competition — it rewrote coffee economics. Panamanian Geisha became, and remains, the most expensive coffee in the world at auction, with prices climbing almost yearly as roasters from Dubai, China, Japan, and Korea bid against each other for tiny lots. Records tell the story: a 2017 Esmeralda lot hit $601/lb; in 2019 a Lamastus Elida Geisha broke $1,000/lb for the first time; by the 2020s, top micro-lots crossed $2,000/lb green, and recent Best of Panama winners have sold for tens of thousands of dollars per kilogram — putting coffee in the same luxury tier as rare whisky. Two forces drive this: genuine scarcity (top lots are grown in tiny quantities, often under 1,000 pounds a harvest) and narrative (specialty coffee runs on stories, and Geisha's is perfect). For most drinkers the lesson isn't the auction madness — it's that a well-grown, sensibly-priced Geisha from Panama, Colombia, or Ethiopia is one of the most distinctive things you can taste in a cup, closer to a delicate floral tea or fine wine than to a typical morning coffee. It's the variety that proved just how extraordinary coffee could be.

keypoints: Geisha/Gesha (both spellings accepted) began in the Gori Gesha forest, SW Ethiopia (identified 1930s, seeds collected ~1936). Traveled: Tanzania → CATIE Costa Rica (1950s, accession T2722) → Panama (1960s), wanted only for LEAF RUST resistance. Ignored for decades — finicky, tall, low-yield, no specialty market to reward it.

The 2004 moment: Peterson family (Hacienda La Esmeralda, Boquete) noticed healthy high-altitude plants in a rust season, isolated them, entered Best of Panama. Judges stunned — explosive jasmine/bergamot/tea-like cup; won ~94 pts, sold ~$21/lb (~10× the going rate). Overnight fame; a new flavor category.

Why: unusually high aromatic compounds (linalool, geraniol — floral/citrus molecules) → jasmine, orange blossom, bergamot, peach, tea-like body. But ONLY expressed grown high (best Panama 1,700–2,100 m, cool/misty slows cherries), hand-picked ripe, obsessively processed. High ceiling, but terroir + processing decide if you reach it. Now also Colombia/Costa Rica/Guatemala/Hawaii/Ethiopia.

The world's most EXPENSIVE coffee at auction: 2017 ~$601/lb, 2019 broke $1,000/lb (Lamastus Elida), 2020s top micro-lots >$2,000/lb green, recent BoP winners tens of thousands $/kg (luxury-whisky tier). Driven by scarcity (<1,000 lb lots) + narrative. But a sensibly-priced Geisha is still one of the most distinctive cups you can taste — the variety that proved how extraordinary coffee could be.

refs.label: Boquete Coffee Traders — Hacienda La Esmeralda

spot_sl28.name: Spotlight: SL28

sections.body: SL28 is the variety behind the intense, unmistakable character of great Kenyan coffee — and it has an unusually institutional origin. In the 1930s, Scott Agricultural Laboratories (the 'SL' in the name) near Nairobi ran a program to select superior coffee varieties for Kenya's conditions. From a group of drought-tolerant, Bourbon-related trees, they selected a single standout individual, labeled it SL28, and propagated it. That one tree's descendants became the backbone of Kenyan specialty coffee, and SL28 is now planted across East Africa and increasingly beyond, always chasing that original Kenyan magic.

What makes SL28 legendary is its cup. At its best, grown in Kenya's high-altitude, mineral-rich soils, SL28 produces a wildly complex, intense coffee with a signature note that coffee people describe again and again: blackcurrant. Alongside that deep berry character comes bright, structured, wine-like acidity, layers of dark fruit, and a full, juicy body — the combination that made Kenyan coffee a benchmark for brightness and complexity. It's a tall, low-yielding, drought-tolerant plant with a deep root system, but it's susceptible to leaf rust and coffee berry disease, which is part of why breeders later developed disease-resistant alternatives (like Ruiru 11 and Batian) to protect Kenya's crop.

sections.h: SL28, SL34, and the Kenyan system
sections.body: SL28 is usually mentioned alongside its sibling SL34, selected in the same program (from a tree in the Kabete area, from French Mission/Bourbon-related stock). SL34 is a bit more productive and tolerant of wetter conditions and higher rainfall, and shares much of the celebrated cup quality, though connoisseurs often give SL28 the edge for sheer complexity. Together they define Kenyan coffee's flavor, supported by the country's distinctive quality infrastructure — the centralized washing-station ('factory') system, meticulous processing, and a rigorous grading system (AA, AB, and so on) that made Kenya a model for traceable, quality-focused African coffee. When you taste a classic bright, blackcurrant-driven Kenyan cup, you're tasting SL28 doing what it does better than almost any variety on earth.

keypoints: SL28 = the variety behind great KENYAN coffee's intense character. Institutional origin: selected in the 1930s by Scott Agricultural Laboratories ('SL') near Nairobi from drought-tolerant Bourbon-related trees — one standout tree, propagated. Now the backbone of Kenyan specialty.

Signature cup: BLACKCURRANT — plus bright structured wine-like acidity, dark fruit layers, full juicy body. The benchmark for brightness/complexity. Tall, low-yield, drought-tolerant, deep roots BUT susceptible to leaf rust & CBD (why Ruiru 11/Batian were later bred).

Paired with sibling SL34 (same program, more productive, handles wetter/higher-rainfall, shares quality — but SL28 often edged for complexity). Together define Kenyan flavor, backed by Kenya's washing-station 'factory' system + rigorous AA/AB grading = a model for traceable quality African coffee.

refs.label: Daily Coffee News — roaster’s guide to varieties

spot_bourbon.name: Spotlight: Bourbon

sections.body: Bourbon is one of the two foundational Arabica varieties (with Typica), and its name is a piece of colonial history. In the early 1700s, the French cultivated coffee from Yemen on the Indian Ocean island of Bourbon — today called Réunion. There, the Yemeni plants naturally mutated into a distinct variety, which took the island's name. From Réunion, Bourbon spread — via French missionaries and colonial routes — across Africa and, crucially, to Latin America (arriving in Brazil in the mid-1800s and radiating out from there), where it became one of the most important and widely-planted varieties in the Americas.

Bourbon's enduring appeal is its cup: it's prized for pronounced sweetness, a rounded body, and a pleasant, balanced complexity — often with notes of caramel, chocolate, red fruit, and a syrupy or winey quality in the best examples. Compared to Typica it yields somewhat more (though still low by modern standards) and its cherries are known for that sweet, clean character. It comes in several color forms — red, yellow (Yellow Bourbon, a beloved Brazilian sub-type), orange, and pink — which can carry subtle flavor differences. For many drinkers and roasters, a well-grown Bourbon is the reference point for what balanced, sweet, classic specialty coffee should taste like.

Beyond its own cup, Bourbon's greatest legacy is genetic: it's the most productive parent in all of coffee. Its natural dwarf mutations became major varieties in their own right — Caturra (Brazil), Pacas (El Salvador), and Villa Sarchi (Costa Rica) are all dwarf Bourbons. Its crosses seeded whole families: Mundo Novo (Bourbon × Typica) and, through that, Catuí; the Kenyan SL varieties are Bourbon-related; and Villa Sarchi, a Bourbon mutation, became a parent of the disease-resistant Sarchimor family. Trace almost any classic Latin American variety back far enough and you'll usually find Bourbon. It's not just a great coffee — it's a great-grandparent to most of them.

keypoints: Bourbon = one of the two founding Arabicas (with Typica). Named for the island of Bourbon (now Réunion) where French-grown Yemeni coffee naturally MUTATED into it (early 1700s). Spread via missionaries/colonial routes to Africa & Latin America (Brazil mid-1800s) → hugely important in the Americas.

Cup: pronounced SWEETNESS, rounded body, balanced complexity — caramel/chocolate/red fruit, syrupy or winey at best. Higher-yield than Typica (still low by modern standards). Color forms: red, YELLOW (beloved Brazilian Yellow Bourbon), orange, pink. A reference for classic sweet specialty coffee.

Genetic legacy = the most PRODUCTIVE parent in coffee: dwarf mutations Caturra/Pacas/Villa Sarchi; crosses Mundo Novo (×Typica) → Catuaí; SL varieties Bourbon-related; Villa Sarchi → Sarchimor family. Trace most classic Latin American varieties back → Bourbon.

spot_pacamara.name: Spotlight: Pacamara

sections.body: Pacamara is unusual among famous varieties because it was deliberately bred, and recently. It was created in El Salvador by the national coffee institute (ISIC), starting in 1958, by crossing two other varieties: Pacas (a dwarf Bourbon mutation from El Salvador) and Maragogipe (a giant-beaned natural mutation of Typica). The name simply fuses the two parents: PACA-MARA. The goal was to combine Pacas's compact, manageable plant size with Maragogipe's enormous beans — and the result is one of the most distinctive and sought-after varieties in specialty coffee.

Pacamara's most obvious trait is inherited from Maragogipe: the beans are huge, among the largest in coffee. But it's the flavor that made it a specialty darling. At its best, Pacamara is intense and complex, often wildly so — known for bold fruit, floral and herbal notes, chocolate and brown-sugar sweetness, bright acidity, and sometimes savory or even tomato-like and buttery notes that make it genuinely unusual. It can be polarizing and inconsistent (it's a somewhat unstable variety that can throw variation), but when it's good, it's spectacular, and it has been a repeat winner and finalist in Cup of Excellence competitions across Central America.

Pacamara has become a signature of Central American specialty coffee, especially in its home country El Salvador but also Guatemala, Honduras, and Nicaragua. Its dramatic, high-ceiling flavor makes it a favorite for competitions and micro-lots, and its distinctiveness — those giant beans and that explosive, sometimes-savory cup — gives it a cult following among roasters and drinkers who want something that doesn't taste like anything else. It's a great example of what deliberate breeding can achieve: a variety created in a lab that turned out to have a genuinely wild, memorable personality in the cup.

keypoints: Pacamara = a DELIBERATE cross, bred in El Salvador by the coffee institute (ISIC) from 1958: Pacas (dwarf Bourbon mutation) × Maragogipe (giant-beaned Typica mutation). Name fuses parents PACA+MARA. Goal: Pacas's manageable size + Maragogipe's huge beans.

Traits: ENORMOUS beans (among coffee's largest, from Maragogipe). Cup: intense, complex, often WILD — bold fruit, floral/herbal, chocolate/brown-sugar, bright acidity, sometimes savory/tomato-like/buttery. Can be polarizing/inconsistent (somewhat unstable) but spectacular at best.

A Central American specialty signature (El Salvador home, also Guatemala/Honduras/Nicaragua). Repeat Cup of Excellence winner/finalist; a competition & micro-lot favorite with a cult following. Proof that deliberate lab breeding can yield a genuinely wild, memorable personality.

spot_maragogipe.name: Spotlight: Maragogipe

sections.body: Maragogipe (also spelled Maragogype) is famous for one spectacular trait: it produces the largest beans in the coffee world, earning it the nickname 'elephant bean.' It's a natural mutation of Typica, discovered in Brazil around 1870 near the town of Maragogipe in Bahia, from which it takes its name. The mutation dramatically enlarges the plant, leaves, and especially the seeds — the beans are so big they're immediately recognizable and have long been prized as a novelty and specialty curiosity.

Maragogipe's cup is generally the opposite of its dramatic appearance: it tends toward a soft, mild, smooth, and clean profile — often mellow and delicate, with gentle sweetness, low-to-moderate acidity, and a light, pleasant body. It's not usually an intense or wildly complex coffee on its own (some find it a bit understated), which is part of why its most important role has been genetic rather than as a standalone star. But grown well at altitude, a good Maragogipe can be lovely and refined, and its sheer bean size makes it a distinctive offering.

Maragogipe's lasting importance is as the source of the 'giant bean' gene in coffee breeding. Its most famous contribution is Pacamara — the celebrated El Salvadoran variety created by crossing Maragogipe with Pacas, which inherited Maragogipe's enormous beans but gained a much more intense, complex cup. Maragogipe has also been crossed into other large-beaned varieties. It's low-yielding and its large plant needs generous spacing, so it's never been a high-volume commercial variety — but as the origin of coffee's most dramatic physical trait, and the genetic parent behind one of specialty's wildest cups, the humble elephant bean has left an outsized mark.

keypoints: Maragogipe/Maragogype = the 'ELEPHANT BEAN' — the LARGEST beans in coffee. A natural mutation of TYPICA, discovered ~1870 near Maragogipe, Bahia, Brazil (its namesake). Enlarges plant, leaves & especially seeds — instantly recognizable.

Cup: opposite of its looks — soft, mild, smooth, clean, mellow/delicate; gentle sweetness, low-moderate acidity, light body. Not intensely complex alone (can be understated) — which is why its main role is GENETIC. Lovely & refined grown well at altitude.

The 'giant bean' GENE source: most famously crossed with Pacas → PACAMARA (inherited the huge beans but a far more intense cup). Low-yielding, needs wide spacing → never high-volume commercial. But as coffee's most dramatic physical trait + parent of a wild specialty cup, the elephant bean left an outsized mark.

spot_workhorse.name: Spotlight: Caturra & Catuaí

sections.body: The famous spotlight varieties — Geisha, SL28, Pacamara — get the auction records and the magazine covers, but the coffee most people actually drink from Latin America is built on two humble workhorses: Caturra and its offspring Catuaí. These aren't exotic; they're the reliable, high-yielding, farm-friendly varieties that made specialty-quality coffee economically viable across Brazil, Colombia, and Central America. Understanding them is understanding the everyday backbone of the coffee world, not the rarefied top 1%.

sections.h: Caturra: the dwarf that changed farming
sections.body: Caturra is a natural mutation of Bourbon, discovered on a plantation in Minas Gerais, Brazil, sometime between 1915 and 1918. Its single-gene mutation makes the plant grow small and compact — its name comes from a Guarani word meaning 'small.' That compactness was revolutionary for farming: short trees with densely spaced branches can be planted much closer together (higher density = more coffee per hectare) and are far easier to harvest. Brazil's Instituto Agronômico de Campinas (IAC) selected and stabilized it from 1937, and by the 1950s–60s it had become a cornerstone across Colombia, Costa Rica, and Central America. In the cup it keeps Bourbon's clean sweetness with a bright, citric acidity and a light-to-medium body — very good at altitude, if rarely spectacular. Its big weakness: it's highly susceptible to leaf rust and coffee berry disease. Caturra is also the standard reference variety that World Coffee Research uses for yield comparisons — the baseline everything else is measured against.

sections.h: Catuaí: even higher yield, cherries that hold on
sections.body: Catuaí is Caturra's most successful offspring. Brazil's IAC crossed Caturra with Mundo Novo (itself a natural Bourbon × Typica hybrid) starting in 1949, and released it in 1972. The name comes from a Guarani phrase meaning 'very good.' It combines Caturra's compact, high-density stature with Mundo Novo's vigor and even higher productivity — it can be planted at nearly double normal density and yields around 20% more than Caturra. Its signature practical trait is that the cherries cling firmly to the branches, which is hugely valuable in windy or rainy regions and for mechanized harvesting, because the fruit doesn't drop prematurely. It comes in red (Vermelho) and yellow (Amarelo) forms. The cup is balanced and accessible — chocolate, nuts, soft acidity — 'good but not great' by the official reckoning, and it holds up well to darker roasts, which is why it's a blend staple. But in skilled hands, especially with honey or natural or anaerobic processing at altitude, Catuaí can produce genuinely vibrant, clean single-origin coffees.

Caturra and Catuaí are a lesson in what actually drives the coffee industry. They were bred not for exotic flavor but for productivity, compactness, and ease of farming — and that practicality is exactly what let good coffee scale. Between them they dominate Brazilian and much of Central American production, and they anchor countless blends and honest single origins. Their shared weakness — susceptibility to leaf rust — is also why the industry keeps breeding rust-resistant hybrids (Catimor and the rest, many of them using Caturra genetics) to protect the crop. And Caturra's genes echo through the family: beyond Catuaí it's a parent of Catimor and of Maracaturra (a cross with giant-beaned Maragogype). So while nobody auctions a Catuaí lot for four figures a pound, these two varieties quietly do more to fill the world's cups than all the celebrated rarities combined.

keypoints: Caturra & Catuaí = the everyday WORKHORSES behind most Latin American coffee (Brazil, Colombia, Central America) — the reliable high-yield backbone, not the exotic top 1% (Geisha/SL28/Pacamara). Bred for productivity & farm-friendliness, which is what let good coffee SCALE.

CATURRA: natural dwarf mutation of Bourbon, Minas Gerais Brazil ~1915–18 ('caturra' = Guarani 'small'). Compact → higher planting density + easy harvest (revolutionized farming). IAC-selected from 1937; cornerstone by 1950s–60s. Cup: Bourbon sweetness + bright citric acidity, light-medium body — solid, rarely spectacular. WEAK: very susceptible to leaf rust/CBD. The WCR yield-reference baseline.

CATUAÍ: Caturra's top offspring — IAC crossed Caturra × Mundo Novo (1949, released 1972; 'very good' in Guarani). Even higher yield (~20% over Caturra, ~2× density); signature: cherries CLING to branches (great for wind/rain + mechanized harvest). Red (Vermelho) & yellow (Amarelo). Cup: balanced choc/nut/soft-acid, 'good not great', holds dark roasts — but vibrant with honey/natural/anaerobic at altitude. Also rust-susceptible.

Why they matter: bred for PRODUCTIVITY not exotic flavor — practicality that scaled good coffee. Dominate Brazilian + much Central American production; anchor blends & honest single origins. Their rust weakness drives the breeding of resistant hybrids (Catimor etc., often using Caturra genes). Caturra also parents Catimor & Maracaturra (×Maragogype). Quietly fill more cups than all the famous rarities combined.

harvest.name: Harvest & Picking
harvest.sub: Getting the cherry off the tree

sections.body: The single biggest quality lever at harvest is picking cherries at peak ripeness. A coffee tree doesn't ripen all its cherries at once — on the same branch you'll find green, yellowing, ripe-red, and overripe cherries side by side. Only fully ripe cherries have developed the sugars that translate into sweetness and clarity in the cup; underripe cherries taste green, astringent, and vegetal, and overripe or dried ones bring fermenty, off flavors. This is why, for specialty coffee, how the cherry is picked matters as much as the variety or the farm.

There are two broad approaches. Selective (or “multiple-pass”) picking sends workers through the same trees repeatedly over weeks, hand-picking only the ripe cherries each time. It's labor-intensive and expensive but yields uniform ripeness — the specialty standard. Strip picking removes everything from a branch in one pass, ripe or not, either by hand or machine; it's far cheaper and faster but mixes ripe, unripe, and overripe cherries together, capping quality. Mechanical harvesting (used heavily in flatter Brazilian farms) is a form of strip picking, though modern harvesters can be tuned to knock off riper cherries preferentially.

Harvest is the most labor-intensive moment in coffee's life, and it's worth understanding the human side. Selective picking depends on a large seasonal workforce moving through steep, often remote terrain, paid typically by weight of cherry picked. Because ripeness is spread over weeks and quality demands multiple passes, labor is a major cost and a real constraint — rising labor costs and worker shortages are among the pressures pushing up specialty prices and driving interest in mechanization and more uniform-ripening varieties. When you pay a premium for a clean, sweet, uniform lot, a lot of what you're paying for is the careful hand-picking behind it.

keypoints: Only fully ripe cherries have the sugars for sweetness and clarity; underripe = green/astringent, overripe = fermenty. Ripeness at picking is the top harvest-quality lever.
Selective picking: multiple hand passes, only ripe cherries — uniform, expensive, the specialty standard.
Strip picking (hand or machine): one pass, everything at once — cheap, fast, mixes ripeness, caps quality.
Harvest is coffee's most labor-intensive stage; picking labor is a major cost and a driver of specialty pricing.

refs.label: Green Coffee Collective — the coffee trade

threats.name: Pests, Disease & Climate

sections.body: The most feared coffee disease is leaf rust, a fungus (Hemileia vastatrix) that coats leaves in orange spores, starves the tree of photosynthesis, and can collapse yields across whole regions. Major outbreaks — like the one that swept Central America in 2012–13 — have devastated farmer livelihoods and reshaped which varieties get planted. Rust is the single biggest reason breeders developed the disease-resistant Catimor, Sarchimor, and modern hybrid varieties by crossing Arabica with rust-resistant Robusta genetics. Warming temperatures are pushing rust to higher altitudes that were once too cool for it, making it worse.

sections.h: The borer beetle and other pests
sections.body: The coffee borer beetle (broca) is the most damaging insect pest worldwide — it bores into the cherry and lays eggs in the bean itself, and the small holes it leaves are exactly the “insect damage” defect graders look for on the sorting table. Because it lives inside the cherry, it's hard to control without careful farm hygiene and timing. Other threats include nematodes (root pests, which some hybrids are bred to resist) and various fungal diseases like coffee berry disease (CBD). Robusta's higher caffeine content acts as a natural insecticide, part of why it's hardier than Arabica.

Looming over all of it is climate change, which is arguably coffee's defining long-term challenge. Arabica is narrowly adapted to cool, high-altitude tropics, and as temperatures rise, the band of land suitable for quality coffee is shrinking and shifting uphill — studies project large reductions in suitable growing area in coming decades. Warming also worsens rust and pests and disrupts the flowering and ripening cycle. The industry's responses run from breeding climate-resilient and disease-resistant varieties, to shade and agroforestry systems that buffer temperature, to moving farms to higher elevations — and, underlying all of it, the economic question of whether farmers can afford to adapt.

keypoints: Coffee leaf rust (Hemileia vastatrix): the most devastating disease; collapsed Central American yields in 2012–13; the reason rust-resistant hybrids exist. Warming pushes it higher.
Coffee borer beetle (broca): worst insect pest; bores into the bean — its holes are the “insect damage” green defect.
Robusta's high caffeine acts as a natural insecticide, part of why it's hardier than Arabica.
Climate change is the existential threat: shrinking/shifting the land suitable for quality Arabica, worsening pests, disrupting ripening. Responses: resilient varieties, shade, higher elevation.

green_intro.name: Buying Green Coffee

sections.h: The roaster can't fix the green
sections.body: Everything in this guide up to now assumes you already have good green coffee. But a great roaster can only enhance a high-quality bean — they can't rescue a mediocre one. If the green is defective, flat, or dull, no amount of Maillard reaction or careful development will save it; you're just polishing a flawed gem. Specialty starts at the bean level, not the bag, which is why green buying and QC is the foundation under everything else.

sections.h: Buy on data, verify with your palate
sections.body: The professional approach is two-sided. Every lot worth considering comes with data: a cupping score, a defect report, moisture and often water-activity readings. You use that to filter. Then you verify with your own senses — blind cup the sample, ideally next to a control coffee, roasted on a standard grading profile that shows the bean rather than your house style. Scores are signals, not trophies; a number gets you to the cupping table, but your palate makes the call.

Green QC breaks into a few skills: physically grading a sample for defects, measuring its moisture, density, and water activity, understanding how processing method shapes the cup, knowing the difference between the two coffee species, and reading the country grading systems on a listing. Each has its own page here. Together they're how a buyer decides what's worth roasting — and worth paying for.

Green coffee reaches a roaster through a few channels, and which one a roaster uses shapes everything downstream. Most buy through importers who hold green in climate-controlled warehouses and offer it by the bag — convenient, lower-commitment, and how most small roasters operate. Larger or more relationship-driven roasters buy through direct trade, contracting with a specific farm or cooperative, often visiting origin and committing before harvest. Either way, buying decisions rest on a sample: roasters request green samples, roast and cup them, and only then commit. Coffee is also seasonal — it's an agricultural product with a harvest calendar — so freshness of the crop (“current crop” vs “past crop”) is part of the buying conversation.

keypoints: A roaster enhances good green but can't fix bad green — specialty starts at the bean.
Buy on data (score, defect report, moisture), then verify by blind cupping against a control.
Scores are signals, not trophies; your palate makes the final call.
Green QC = defect grading + physical measurement + processing + species + country grades.

refs.label: Ebru Coffee — how specialty is graded

green_grading.name: Green Grading & Defects

sections.body: The SCA green grading protocol works on a 350g representative sample — ideally pulled from across at least 10% of a lot's bags so it reflects the whole shipment. You assess color and smell, then hand-sort the beans against a matte black surface under full-spectrum light (4000K, ~1200 Lux), pulling out every defect. Carefully grading a 350g sample takes an experienced grader 20–30 minutes, so it's real work — six samples can eat an afternoon.

Defects split into Category 1 (primary) — severe faults like full black beans, full sour beans, and foreign matter (stones, sticks) — and Category 2 (secondary) — milder issues like partial black, insect damage, broken/chipped/cut beans, immature (quaker) beans, and shells. If a single bean has two defects, you count only the more severe one. The categories exist because the SCA system weights defects by how much they actually hurt the cup.

Not every defective bean counts as a full defect. The system uses equivalences: one full black (Cat 1) equals three partial blacks (Cat 2); you need five broken/chipped/cut beans to make one full Category 2 defect. So ten broken beans = two full defects. You tally everything into a single 'full defect' count for the sample. If your sample is smaller than 350g, sort it anyway and scale the count — multiply a 100g count by 3.5 — accepting it's an approximation.

To qualify as specialty grade, a 350g sample must have zero Category 1 defects and no more than five full Category 2 defects. That's a strict bar: a single full black or full sour bean disqualifies the lot. Roasted specialty coffee also permits no quakers (underdeveloped beans) in a 100g sample. This physical inspection sits alongside the cup score — a coffee needs to pass both to be truly specialty. (Note: the SCA has also used water activity below 0.70 as a heritage requirement, and the CVA now folds physical grading into its framework.)

On the bench you work methodically: spread the 350g sample on a black tray, and pick through it bean by bean, pulling defects into groups so you can count them. Graders develop a fast eye for the Category 1 faults first — a single full black or full sour bean ends the specialty conversation immediately, so those are worth spotting early. Category 2 defects get tallied and converted to full-defect equivalents. Colour, uniformity, smell, and the presence of foreign matter (stones, sticks, husk) all factor in. The same sample is then usually roast-graded too: a small sample roast reveals quakers — beans that stay stubbornly pale because they came from unripe cherries — which don't show reliably in the green.

keypoints: 350g representative sample, sorted under 4000K/1200 Lux on matte black; ~20–30 min per sample.
Cat 1 (primary): full black/sour, foreign matter. Cat 2 (secondary): partial black, insect, broken, quaker, shells.
Equivalences: 1 full black = 3 partial; 5 broken = 1 full Cat-2. Tally to a single full-defect count.
Specialty = 0 Cat-1 and ≤5 full Cat-2 in 350g; no quakers in roasted. Scale small samples (×3.5 for 100g).

refs.label: SCA — grounding green grading in sensory science

visuals.title: What the defects look like
visuals.note: Green graders learn to spot these by eye against a black tray. The signatures below are the common ones — the two Category 1 faults (full black, full sour) that disqualify a specialty lot outright, and the milder Category 2 defects you're allowed a few of.

beans.label: Healthy bean
beans.cap: Even color, intact, clean center crease.

beans.label: Full black — Cat 1
beans.cap: Dead/overripe or fermented; blackened through. Disqualifying.

beans.label: Partial black — Cat 2
beans.cap: Less than half black; 3 = one full defect.

beans.label: Insect damage — Cat 2
beans.cap: Small bore holes from the coffee borer beetle.

beans.label: Quaker
beans.cap: Underdeveloped, pale after roasting. Not allowed in roasted specialty.

green_measure.name: Density, Moisture & Water Activity

sections.body: Moisture is the headline physical number. Specialty green should sit around 10–12%. Too high (above ~12.5%) and the coffee risks mold during the long, humid sea freight and in storage. Too low (below ~9%) and it's likely over-dried or aged, tending to taste flat. Contractual specs of 10–12% protect both buyer and seller, and you verify moisture at both pre-shipment and arrival, because it can drift in transit. A calibrated meter, sampled from several spots, does the job.

Water activity is often confused with moisture but measures something different: not how much water is present, but how much is available for microbial growth, on a 0–1 scale. Green coffee is considered shelf-stable roughly between 0.50 and 0.70; above ~0.70 the mold and microbial risk climbs sharply. Two coffees with identical moisture can differ in water activity depending on how tightly the water is bound in the bean. This matters most for lots you'll store more than a few months — it's the better predictor of storage safety.

Density reflects how hard and compact the bean is, which tracks with growing altitude — high-grown coffee matures slowly and comes out denser. It matters to the roaster because heat moves differently through dense material: dense beans generally take and want more energy. Buyers use density to confirm altitude claims and to benchmark a new arrival against previous ones. A simple farm-level version is the float test — healthy dense beans sink, low-density floaters (often defective or under-developed) rise.

Screen size grades beans by physical size, measured in 64ths of an inch — a screen 18 bean is 18/64 inch, about 7.1mm. Green is passed through a stack of calibrated screens to sort by size; specialty coffee commonly falls in the screen 14–18 range. Size isn't quality directly, but uniform size means even roasting, which is why it's specified. This is also the basis of some country grades (Kenya's AA, Colombia's Supremo).

sections.h: Why buyers care about all four numbers
sections.body: None of these numbers is quality on its own, but together they de-risk a purchase. Moisture and water activity predict whether a lot will survive months in a warehouse or arrive moldy. Density confirms the altitude story a seller tells and hints at how the coffee will take heat in the roaster. Screen size predicts even roasting and underpins many country grades. A careful buyer reads them as a set: a high-altitude claim with low density is a red flag; specialty moisture with high water activity warns of storage risk. The cup score tells you how good it is today; these numbers tell you whether it'll still be good when you roast it.

keypoints: Moisture ~10–12%; >12.5% risks mold in freight, <9% is over-dried/flat. Check pre-ship and on arrival.
Water activity (0–1) measures water available to microbes; <0.70 is shelf-stable — key for long storage.
Density tracks altitude/hardness and affects roast energy; float test separates dense (sink) from defective (float).
Screen size = 64ths of an inch (screen 18 ≈ 7.1mm); specialty ~14–18; uniform size = even roasting.

refs.label: Ethio Coffee — moisture & water activity in QC

green_processing.name: Processing Methods
green_processing.sub: How the cherry becomes the bean

sections.body: How a coffee cherry is turned into a green bean after harvest shapes the cup as profoundly as origin or variety. 'Washed,' 'natural,' and 'honey' aren't marketing words — they're descriptions of physical process, and knowing them lets you predict roughly what a coffee will taste like before you ever roast it. The same farm can produce dramatically different cups depending on how the lot was processed.

Washed (wet) process removes all the fruit and mucilage before drying — often via fermentation, then washing clean. The result is a clean, bright, transparent cup where the origin and terroir show clearly with defined acidity. It's the default reference for cupping because it hides nothing. Natural (dry) process is the oldest method: the whole cherry dries around the bean, 'raisining' onto it. This gives bold, sweet, fruity cups (think blueberry, jammy notes) with heavier body and lower perceived acidity — but drying is harder to control, so defect risk is higher.

Honey (pulped natural) process is the hybrid: the skin is removed but some sticky mucilage is left on the bean during drying. It lands between washed and natural — more sweetness and body than washed, more clarity than natural. It's often graded by how much mucilage stays on: white (most removed, lightest/cleanest), yellow, red, and black (least removed, richest, sweetest, and slowest/riskiest to dry).

Two more you'll see on listings. Anaerobic ferments the coffee sealed off from oxygen (often 48–72 hours), producing intense, complex, sometimes polarizing flavors — the experimental frontier. Wet-hulled (giling basah), used almost only in Indonesia, hulls the parchment off while the bean is still wet (around 30–35% moisture), then finishes drying exposed. It's what gives Sumatran coffee its signature earthy, full-bodied, very-low-acid, sometimes savory character. Each method carries a different defect and mold risk when drying is done poorly.

sections.h: Why processing is a lever, not a label
sections.body: For a buyer or roaster, processing is one of the few things that reliably predicts the cup before you taste it, so it's worth reading on every offer sheet. The same variety from the same farm can land anywhere from crisp and tea-like to jammy and wild depending only on how the cherry was handled after picking. It also carries risk information: naturals and honeys involve fruit sitting on the bean while it dries, which builds sweetness but invites fermentation faults and mold if the drying isn't carefully managed — so processing tells you something about both the flavor and the care that went into the lot.

keypoints: Processing shapes the cup as much as origin; the terms describe physical process, not marketing.
Washed = clean, bright, transparent (cupping reference). Natural = sweet, fruity, heavy body, higher defect risk.
Honey = hybrid; graded white→black by mucilage left on (light/clean → rich/sweet/riskier).
Anaerobic = sealed-oxygen ferment, intense/experimental. Wet-hulled (giling basah) = Indonesian, earthy, low-acid.

refs.label: We Are Coffee Co — processing methods explained
Craft Coffee Guru — honey process detail

processing_experimental.name: Experimental Processing

sections.body: For most of coffee history there were three ways to process a cherry — washed, natural, and honey — and they were covered elsewhere as the foundation. What's changed in the last decade is that producers realized processing isn't just a way to get the fruit off the seed; it's a powerful, controllable flavor lever. The key insight is that flavor is created by fermentation — the microbial activity (yeasts and bacteria) that acts on the cherry's sugars after harvest. Every method is really about controlling which microbes work on the coffee, for how long, and under what conditions. Once producers started manipulating that deliberately — borrowing ideas from winemaking, brewing, and food science — they unlocked flavors that traditional processing never reached: raspberry, white wine, mango, cinnamon, tropical fruit. This is the frontier of specialty coffee, and it's moved from rare competition lots to bags you can buy today.

The two headline techniques both remove oxygen, but differently. Anaerobic fermentation is the workhorse: coffee (as cherries or depulped beans) ferments in sealed, oxygen-free tanks or barrels fitted with a one-way valve — as fermentation gases build up, they push the oxygen out. Without oxygen, a different set of microbes dominates, fermentation proceeds slower and more controlled, and the result is typically a heavier body with intense winey, boozy, or fruit-forward notes; done well, it can actually tame the wild 'funk' of a natural while maxing out juicy fruit and body. Carbonic maceration is the show pony, borrowed directly from Beaujolais winemaking: whole, intact cherries go into a tank flushed with carbon dioxide, so fermentation begins inside each individual cherry rather than in the surrounding mass. That produces unusually uniform, vibrant, juicy red-fruit flavors. It's harder and costlier to do — and it earned its fame when Sasa Sestic won the 2015 World Barista Championship using a carbonic-maceration coffee, after which it became a fixture of high-end lots.

sections.h: Lactic, thermal shock, co-fermentation, and more
sections.body: Beyond those, the toolkit keeps expanding. Lactic fermentation is a targeted anaerobic process that favors lactic-acid bacteria (sometimes with a salt brine or starter culture), often running 80+ hours — pioneered at farms like La Palma y El Tucán in Colombia — giving a velvety, creamy, yogurt-tangy cup with intense sweetness. Thermal shock cycles the coffee through dramatic hot-and-cold temperature swings to shift which microbes dominate and intensify sweetness or structure, often paired with anaerobic methods. Honey processing itself comes in graded levels — white, yellow, red, and black honey — referring to how much sticky mucilage is left on the bean to dry (more mucilage = darker color, more fermentation, richer and fruitier, but riskier). Yeast-inoculation adds selected yeast strains for controlled, repeatable results, and even koji (the Aspergillus fungus behind sake and miso) is being used for umami-rich sweetness. Multi-stage processes chain several of these together. It's genuinely a period of wild, science-driven experimentation.

sections.h: The co-fermentation controversy — and how to buy well
sections.body: The most debated technique is co-fermentation: adding ingredients — fruits, cacao, spices, botanicals — during fermentation. Advocates say the additions create specific bacterial cultures (via their sugars and pH) that steer how the bean ferments, which is legitimately different from simply soaking beans in flavoring. Critics worry the line to 'infused' or artificially flavored coffee gets blurry, and ask whether you're still tasting the coffee at all when the cup screams of added mango or cinnamon. Both concerns are fair, and the industry hasn't fully settled where the line sits. The practical guidance for buyers cuts through it: a trustworthy experimental coffee names its process and its numbers — the method, the fermentation time, the origin, ideally the producer. Vague labels like 'specialty fermented' with no detail, or a flavor that reads like pure candy with no explanation, are red flags. And a fair warning: these coffees are polarizing and often expensive, they're usually best brewed as pour-over to showcase clarity (many are ruined by milk), and there's no shame in preferring a clean washed Kenyan. Experimental processing is one of the most exciting things happening in coffee — approach it with curiosity and a little healthy skepticism.

keypoints: Processing isn't just fruit-removal — it's the FLAVOR lever. Flavor comes from FERMENTATION (yeasts + bacteria on the cherry's sugars). Every method = controlling WHICH microbes work, how long, under what conditions. Deliberate manipulation (borrowed from wine/brewing) unlocks raspberry/white-wine/mango/cinnamon notes traditional processing can't reach.

ANAEROBIC (the workhorse): sealed oxygen-free tanks/barrels w/ one-way valve; different microbes dominate, slower/controlled → heavier body, winey/boozy/fruity; can tame natural 'funk' while maxing juice+body. CARBONIC MACERATION (the show pony, from Beaujolais wine): whole cherries in a CO2-flushed tank → fermentation INSIDE each cherry → uniform, vibrant, juicy red fruit. Sasa Sestic won 2015 WBC with it.

More tools: LACTIC (targeted anaerobic favoring lactic-acid bacteria, 80+ hrs, La Palma y El Tucán → velvety/yogurt-tang/sweet); THERMAL SHOCK (hot-cold swings shift microbes, intensify sweetness); HONEY LEVELS (white→yellow→red→BLACK = more mucilage left on = more fermentation, richer/fruitier/riskier); yeast-inoculation, koji (sake/miso fungus, umami), multi-stage chains.

CO-FERMENTATION debate: adding fruits/spices/cacao during fermentation — advocates say it steers the bacterial culture (legit, not just soaking); critics say the line to 'infused'/flavored coffee blurs. Buy well: trustworthy exotic lots NAME the method + fermentation time + origin + producer. Vague 'specialty fermented' or candy-with-no-explanation = red flag. Polarizing, pricey, best as pour-over (not milk); a clean washed coffee is fine too.

refs.label: CoffeeGeek — impact of processing on character

decaf_methods.name: Decaffeination Methods

sections.body: An important fact people miss: decaffeination is done to green coffee, before roasting. Every method shares the same basic challenge — caffeine is bound up inside the bean alongside hundreds of flavor compounds, and the goal is to pull the caffeine out while leaving the flavor precursors as intact as possible. All commercial methods start by steaming or soaking the green beans to swell them and open their pores, then use some agent (a solvent, water, or CO2) to carry the caffeine away, and finally dry the beans back down. No method removes 100% — regulations and industry norms target around 97–99.9% caffeine removal, so decaf always contains a small amount.

sections.h: Solvent methods: methylene chloride & ethyl acetate
sections.body: The oldest approach uses a solvent that selectively binds caffeine. Two food-grade solvents dominate today: methylene chloride (MC) and ethyl acetate (EA). In a direct process the steamed beans are washed repeatedly with the solvent, which bonds to caffeine and carries it off; a final steaming drives off residual solvent (and roasting evaporates the rest — MC vaporizes around 40°C). Residues are tightly regulated (the FDA limit for MC is 10 ppm, an extremely small amount). EA is often marketed as the “sugarcane” or “natural” process because it can be derived from fermented sugarcane — in Colombia especially, locally-fermented sugarcane EA is used, creating a tidy circular economy — though EA can also be made synthetically, so “natural” depends on the source. EA decaf is prized for keeping sweetness and body (great for espresso) but has a shorter shelf life after roasting.

sections.h: Water methods: Swiss Water & Mountain Water
sections.body: Water-based methods use no added chemicals at all — the selling point for many drinkers. The Swiss Water Process (commercialized in British Columbia, Canada) works through a clever trick: green beans are soaked to create a “Green Coffee Extract” (GCE), water already saturated with all of coffee's soluble compounds except caffeine. When fresh beans are introduced to this GCE, the flavor compounds have nowhere to diffuse to (the water is already full of them), so they stay in the bean — but caffeine, which the GCE lacks, diffuses out and is stripped away by carbon filters. The Mountain Water Process (Descamex, Mexico) uses the same water-and-carbon-filter principle with glacier water from Pico de Orizaba. Swiss Water removes ~99.9% of caffeine, is certified organic and chemical-free, and tends to accentuate body.

The supercritical CO2 method pressurizes carbon dioxide above its critical point so it behaves between a gas and a liquid; circulated through the beans at high pressure, it dissolves caffeine selectively while leaving flavor compounds largely behind, and the caffeine is then scrubbed from the CO2 in a separate vessel. It's excellent at removing caffeine with minimal flavor impact, but the equipment is expensive, so it's mostly used for large commercial volumes rather than specialty microlots. The takeaways for buying decaf: the method matters for flavor and for how you feel about chemicals; water and CO2 are chemical-free while MC/EA are solvent-based (and safe by regulation); EA/sugarcane is a specialty favorite for sweetness; and — above all — great decaf starts with great green coffee, since a good process can't rescue a bad bean.

keypoints: Decaffeination is done to GREEN coffee before roasting; all methods steam/soak to open pores, extract caffeine, then re-dry. Target ~97–99.9% removal — never 100%.

Solvent: methylene chloride (MC) or ethyl acetate (EA). EA = “sugarcane/natural” process (can be sugarcane-derived), sweet & full but shorter shelf life. Residues tightly regulated & mostly evaporate in roasting.
Water: Swiss Water (Canada) & Mountain Water (Mexico) — no added chemicals, use a saturated Green Coffee Extract so only caffeine leaves. ~99.9% removal, organic-certified.
CO2: supercritical CO2 dissolves caffeine selectively; great results, expensive, used for commercial volumes.
Great decaf needs great green coffee first — process can't fix a bad bean.

refs.label: Swiss Water — water vs solvent decaf

decaf_deep.name: How Decaf Is Made

sections.body: Decaffeination is a deceptively hard problem. The goal is to remove around 97–99% of the caffeine (the legal 'decaf' threshold is usually 97% in the US) while leaving the hundreds of other flavor and aroma compounds untouched — and caffeine is chemically tangled up with everything else in the bean. Every method must start with the same reality: you have to get the caffeine out of green, unroasted beans without stripping the flavor precursors that make coffee taste like coffee. All the major methods begin by steaming or soaking the green beans to swell them and open their pores, then use some agent to pull the caffeine out, then dry the beans back down. The differences — and the reason decaf ranges from awful to excellent — come down to which agent does the extracting.

sections.h: Solvent methods (and a note on safety)
sections.body: The oldest commercial approach uses a chemical solvent that binds to caffeine. It was pioneered in 1903 by German merchant Ludwig Roselius (originally using benzene, long since abandoned), and today the two solvents in use are methylene chloride (MC, dichloromethane) and ethyl acetate (EA). In the 'direct' method the beans are washed directly with solvent; in the 'indirect' method the caffeine is drawn into water first and the solvent treats the water. Methylene chloride is the aggressive one — the process behind the flat, medicinal taste that gave commercial decaf its bad reputation — though regulators cap residue at tiny levels (10 ppm in the US) and the solvent largely evaporates during roasting. Ethyl acetate is gentler and tends to leave more sweetness. EA is often marketed as 'natural' or 'sugarcane process' because ethyl acetate occurs in fruit and can be derived from sugarcane/molasses fermentation — but industrial EA can also be synthesized from petroleum, and 'natural' on a label doesn't tell you which. If it matters to you, ask the roaster.

sections.h: Swiss Water and CO2: the clean methods
sections.body: Two solvent-free methods dominate specialty decaf. The Swiss Water Process (pioneered in Switzerland, commercialized from the 1970s–80s) is beautifully clever: beans are soaked in 'green coffee extract' — water already saturated with all of coffee's soluble compounds except caffeine. Because the water is saturated with everything but caffeine, only caffeine diffuses out of the beans (down the concentration gradient), leaving the flavor compounds behind; the caffeine is then filtered from the water with activated charcoal and the water reused. It's 99.9% caffeine-free, chemical-free, certified organic-compatible, and the reason you see the 'Swiss Water' logo on bags. (Mountain Water Process, from Mexico, works on the same principle.) The CO2 method (developed by Kurt Zosel of the Max Planck Institute) uses carbon dioxide pressurized into a 'supercritical' state — behaving as both liquid and gas — which acts as a highly selective solvent that bonds to caffeine while leaving flavor molecules largely intact. It offers the highest flavor retention of any method, but the pressurized equipment is expensive, so it's used for larger commercial volumes and commands a premium.

For decades, 'decaf' was a punchline — thin, dull, vaguely chemical coffee that serious drinkers avoided. That's genuinely changed, and it's worth knowing why. Three things converged: the clean water and CO2 methods removed the solvent taste and residue concerns; decaffeination technology improved at protecting delicate aromatics; and, crucially, the specialty industry started decaffeinating good green coffee. A lot of old decaf tasted bad partly because low-quality beans were used (why waste specialty coffee on decaf?) — but now high-quality lots are decaffeinated with gentle methods, and the results can be excellent, nearly indistinguishable from caffeinated coffee. The one unavoidable truth is that decaffeination is an extra processing step that always costs some subtlety and adds expense, so decaf will typically be a touch less vibrant and a bit pricier than its caffeinated equivalent. But the era of undrinkable decaf is over — a well-made Swiss Water or CO2 decaf from a quality roaster is a real coffee, not a compromise.

keypoints: Goal: remove ~97–99% caffeine (US legal decaf = 97%+) while leaving the other flavor/aroma compounds. All methods: steam/soak green beans to open pores → extract caffeine with some agent → dry. The AGENT is what differs (and decides quality).

SOLVENT methods (oldest; Roselius 1903, orig. benzene): today methylene chloride (MC — aggressive, the 'flat medicinal' reputation; residue capped ~10 ppm, evaporates in roast) or ethyl acetate (EA — gentler, sweeter). EA marketed 'natural'/'sugarcane' (from molasses) BUT can also be petroleum-synthesized — 'natural' label doesn't say which; ask.

Clean methods: SWISS WATER — soak in caffeine-free 'green coffee extract' so only caffeine diffuses out (flavor stays), charcoal-filter & reuse; 99.9%, chemical-free, organic (Mountain Water = same idea). CO2 — supercritical CO2 (liquid+gas) selectively bonds caffeine; HIGHEST flavor retention but pricey equipment → commercial volumes + premium.

Good decaf now exists because: clean water/CO2 methods killed solvent taste/residue; tech better protects aromatics; and specialty started decaffeinating GOOD green (old decaf used junk beans). Still an extra step → always slightly less vibrant + pricier, but a quality Swiss Water/CO2 decaf is a real coffee, not a compromise.

green_species.name: Arabica vs Robusta

sections.body: Of 80-plus coffee species, only two matter commercially. Coffea arabica is about 70% of the world market and essentially all of specialty coffee. Coffea canephora — robusta — is the rest, dominant in commodity blends, instant coffee, and as an espresso crema-booster. They're genuinely different plants producing genuinely different cups, and knowing which you're buying frames every other expectation.

Arabica grows at higher altitude (roughly 800–2200m), matures slowly in cool conditions, and rewards that with higher sugars, more aromatic complexity, and pronounced, pleasant acidity — the fruit, floral, and nuanced flavors specialty is built on. The tradeoffs: it's more susceptible to disease (especially leaf rust) and pests, yields less, and costs more. Its caffeine content is relatively low, around 1.2–1.5%. Nearly every origin and variety in this app's profiles is arabica.

Robusta is the hardy one — it grows at lower elevations in warmer, tougher conditions, resists disease and pests, and yields more, which makes it roughly 30–50% cheaper. Its caffeine is nearly double arabica's, around 2.2–2.7% (the plant evolved caffeine partly as a natural pesticide). The cup is heavier, more bitter, earthier, and less complex — traditionally seen as lower quality, though a small movement of 'fine robusta' aims to change that. The higher caffeine and crema are why robusta shows up in traditional Italian espresso blends.

For a specialty roaster, the working assumption is arabica, and the interesting decisions live inside it — origin, variety, altitude, processing. Robusta enters the conversation deliberately: for a traditional espresso blend wanting body, crema, and a caffeine kick, or at the commodity end. The species tells you which set of expectations to bring to the cup before you taste.

keypoints: Only two species matter: arabica (~70% of market, ~all of specialty) and robusta (commodity, instant, crema).
Arabica: higher altitude (800–2200m), complex/acidic, disease-prone, lower yield, ~1.2–1.5% caffeine, pricier.
Robusta: hardy/low-altitude, heavier/bitter/earthier, high yield, ~2.2–2.7% caffeine, 30–50% cheaper.
Specialty defaults to arabica; robusta is a deliberate choice for espresso body/crema/caffeine or commodity.

refs.label: Old Chicago Coffee — caffeine by type

green_grades.name: Country Grading Systems

sections.body: The SCA green grading system is universal, but every producing country also has its own grading scheme, shaped by its coffee history, trade infrastructure, and the coffee it grows. That's why a green listing often shows both a country grade and an SCA cup score — they measure different things. The country grade tells you about physical sorting (size or defect count); the cup score tells you how it tastes. You need both to read a coffee.

Two broad approaches exist. Defect-count systems grade by how many defects appear in a sample: Ethiopia runs G1 (fewest defects, best) through G5, counted per 300g; Brazil grades on defects (per 300g) combined with screen size. Size systems grade by bean dimension: Kenya's famous AA/AB/PB grades are pure screen size (AA is the largest, not necessarily the best-tasting), and Colombia grades by screen too (Supremo largest, then Excelso).

The most important trap: bean-size grades don't directly mean quality. Kenya AA tells you the beans are large and will roast evenly, but a well-processed AB from a top estate can out-cup a mediocre AA. Always read the estate/producer name and processing method alongside the grade, and trust the cup score over the size letter. A Grade 2 Ethiopian cupping 86 will beat a Grade 1 cupping 80.

You'll also see preparation standards like 'EP' (European Preparation) — an extra sorting pass, typically meaning no more than 8 defects per 300g and no beans below screen 15. It signals cleaner, more uniform green, though not guaranteed cup quality. And a key clarification: certifications like Organic or Fair Trade are about process and ethics, not cup quality — a Fair Trade coffee can score 78 and a certification-free microlot can score 89. Certifications describe how a coffee was grown and traded; specialty is about what's in the cup.

keypoints: Every origin has its own grade system; listings show country grade AND SCA score because they measure different things.
Defect-count systems: Ethiopia G1–G5, Brazil (defects + screen), per 300g. Size systems: Kenya AA/AB/PB, Colombia Supremo/Excelso.
Bean size ≠ quality — read estate + processing, and trust the cup score over the size letter.
EP = extra sorting (≤8 defects/300g, no beans below screen 15); Organic/Fair Trade are ethics/process, not cup quality.
